D: Original: The Bhagavad Gita — "The Song Celestial" (Sir Edwin Arnold translation, 1885)
D: Refashioned: The Bhagavad Gita — Refashioned by Zzy

D: Original: Chapter 1: Arjuna's Despair (Arjuna Vishada Yoga)

[Dhritirashtra]
Ranged thus for battle on the sacred plain--
On Kurukshetra--say, Sanjaya! say
What wrought my people, and the Pandavas?
D: Refashioned:
[Dhritirashtra]
Tell me, Sanjaya — on the sacred field of Kurukshetra, what did my sons and the sons of Pandu do?
D: Elegant: The blind king asks: what happened on the battlefield between my sons and theirs?
D: Distilled: A father who cannot see asks what his children have done.
Notes: Dhritirashtra is the blind king of the Kuru dynasty. He cannot witness the battle himself and relies on Sanjaya, who has been granted divine sight by the sage Vyasa. The Gita opens with a question from a ruler who is literally blind — a framing device that resonates with the framework's emphasis on the gap between holding power and perceiving reality.

D: Original:
[Sanjaya]
When he beheld the host of Pandavas,
Raja Duryodhana to Drona drew,
And spake these words: "Ah, Guru! see this line,
How vast it is of Pandu fighting-men,
Embattled by the son of Drupada,
Thy scholar in the war! Therein stand ranked
Chiefs like Arjuna, like to Bhima chiefs,
Benders of bows; Virata, Yuyudhan,
Drupada, eminent upon his car,
Dhrishtaket, Chekitan, Kasi's stout lord,
Purujit, Kuntibhoj, and Saivya,
With Yudhamanyu, and Uttamauj
Subhadra's child; and Drupadi's;-all famed!
All mounted on their shining chariots!
On our side, too,--thou best of Brahmans! see
Excellent chiefs, commanders of my line,
Whose names I joy to count: thyself the first,
Then Bhishma, Karna, Kripa fierce in fight,
Vikarna, Aswatthaman; next to these
Strong Saumadatti, with full many more
Valiant and tried, ready this day to die
For me their king, each with his weapon grasped,
Each skilful in the field. Weakest-meseems-
Our battle shows where Bhishma holds command,
And Bhima, fronting him, something too strong!
Have care our captains nigh to Bhishma's ranks
Prepare what help they may! Now, blow my shell!"
D: Refashioned:
[Sanjaya]
When Prince Duryodhana saw the Pandava army drawn up for battle, he went to his teacher Drona and said: 'Look at this enormous force, assembled by your own former student, Drupada's son. They have warriors as great as Arjuna and Bhima — master archers, powerful fighters, all mounted on brilliant chariots. And on our side too we have great commanders: yourself, Bhishma, Karna, Kripa, and many others, all willing to die for me, all skilled in war. Yet our strength under Bhishma seems somehow less than theirs under Bhima. Let our captains close ranks around Bhishma. Now — blow the conch!'
D: Elegant: Duryodhana surveys the enemy, lists his own champions, and sounds the war horn — a prince cataloguing his power to reassure himself it is enough.
D: Distilled: A prince counts his advantages and sounds the horn, trying to convince himself his side will prevail.
Notes: Duryodhana is Dhritirashtra's eldest son, the antagonist of the Mahabharata. Drona (Dronacharya) is the great weapons-master who trained both the Pandavas and Kauravas. This passage catalogs warriors on both sides — a literary convention in Indian epic poetry (similar to Homer's Catalogue of Ships in the Iliad). The detail matters: many of these warriors are teachers, relatives, and mentors of the fighters on both sides, establishing the moral crisis Arjuna will face.

D: Original:
[Sanjaya]
Then, at the signal of the aged king,
With blare to wake the blood, rolling around
Like to a lion's roar, the trumpeter
Blew the great Conch; and, at the noise of it,
Trumpets and drums, cymbals and gongs and horns
Burst into sudden clamour; as the blasts
Of loosened tempest, such the tumult seemed!
Then might be seen, upon their car of gold
Yoked with white steeds, blowing their battle-shells,
Krishna the God, Arjuna at his side:
Krishna, with knotted locks, blew his great conch
Carved of the "Giant's bone;" Arjuna blew
Indra's loud gift; Bhima the terrible--
Wolf-bellied Bhima-blew a long reed-conch;
And Yudhisthira, Kunti's blameless son,
Winded a mighty shell, "Victory's Voice;"
And Nakula blew shrill upon his conch
Named the "Sweet-sounding," Sahadev on his
Called"Gem-bedecked," and Kasi's Prince on his.
Sikhandi on his car, Dhrishtadyumn,
Virata, Satyaki the Unsubdued,
Drupada, with his sons, (O Lord of Earth!)
Long-armed Subhadra's children, all blew loud,
So that the clangour shook their foemen's hearts,
With quaking earth and thundering heav'n.
D: Refashioned:
[Sanjaya]
At the old king's signal, a great conch horn blew — a sound like a lion's roar — and suddenly the whole field erupted with trumpets, drums, cymbals, and horns, a tumult like a storm breaking loose.

Then Krishna and Arjuna, riding their golden chariot drawn by white horses, blew their own war conches. Krishna blew Panchajanya, carved from a giant's bone. Arjuna blew Devadatta, Indra's gift. Bhima blew his great reed-conch Paundra. Each of the Pandava brothers and their allies sounded their own horns, and the noise shook the earth and sky and the hearts of their enemies.
D: Elegant: The conches answer each other across the field — first the Kauravas, then Krishna and Arjuna, then every Pandava champion in turn — until the sound shakes earth and sky and the hearts of those who must face it.
D: Distilled: Both armies announce themselves with sound, and the earth shakes with what is about to happen.
Notes: Panchajanya: Krishna's conch, taken from the demon Panchajana. Devadatta: Arjuna's conch, a gift from the god Indra. Paundra: Bhima's great conch. The blowing of conches is both ritual and tactical — it announces battle, signals formations, and psychologically prepares the armies. The escalation from one horn to a full orchestra mirrors the escalation toward a war that cannot be undone.

D: Original:
[Sanjaya]
Then 'twas-
Beholding Dhritirashtra's battle set,
Weapons unsheathing, bows drawn forth, the war
Instant to break-Arjun, whose ensign-badge
Was Hanuman the monkey, spake this thing
To Krishna the Divine, his charioteer:
"Drive, Dauntless One! to yonder open ground
Betwixt the armies; I would see more nigh
These who will fight with us, those we must slay
To-day, in war's arbitrament; for, sure,
On bloodshed all are bent who throng this plain,
Obeying Dhritirashtra's sinful son."
D: Refashioned:
[Sanjaya]
Seeing Dhritirashtra's forces arrayed for battle, weapons drawn, war about to break — Arjuna, whose banner bore the monkey-god Hanuman, spoke to Krishna, his divine charioteer:

'Drive between the two armies. I want to see more clearly who we are about to fight — who has come here to die, obeying Dhritirashtra's corrupt son.'
D: Elegant: Arjuna asks Krishna to drive between the armies. He wants to look into the faces of the men he is about to kill.
D: Distilled: Before fighting, Arjuna asks to see his enemies up close — and discovers they are his family.
Notes: Hanuman on Arjuna's banner: the monkey-god from the Ramayana, symbolizing devotion and strength. Krishna as charioteer: God has taken the role of servant, driving the warrior's vehicle. This is a central image — the divine does not fight for you but positions you to fight correctly. The request to 'see more clearly' is the beginning of Arjuna's crisis: he has been thinking of this as a war against enemies and is about to realize it is a war against family.

D: Original:
[Sanjaya]
Thus, by Arjuna prayed, (O Bharata!)
Between the hosts that heavenly Charioteer
Drove the bright car, reining its milk-white steeds
Where Bhishma led,and Drona,and their Lords.
"See!" spake he to Arjuna, "where they stand,
Thy kindred of the Kurus:" and the Prince
Marked on each hand the kinsmen of his house,
Grandsires and sires, uncles and brothers and sons,
Cousins and sons-in-law and nephews, mixed
With friends and honoured elders; some this side,
Some that side ranged: and, seeing those opposed,
Such kith grown enemies-Arjuna's heart
Melted with pity, while he uttered this:
D: Refashioned:
[Sanjaya]
Krishna drove the bright chariot between the armies, stopping where Bhishma and Drona stood with their forces. 'Look,' Krishna said. 'There are your Kuru kinsmen.'

Arjuna looked — and saw grandfathers, fathers, uncles, brothers, sons, cousins, nephews, friends, and honored elders arrayed on both sides. His own family, drawn up as enemies.
D: Elegant: Krishna parks the chariot between the armies and says simply: 'Look. Your family.' Arjuna looks — and sees grandfathers, sons, teachers, and friends on both sides of the killing field.
D: Distilled: Krishna shows Arjuna that the enemy army is his own family.
Notes: Bhishma is Arjuna's great-uncle and patriarch of the dynasty. Drona is Arjuna's beloved weapons-teacher. The moment of recognition — seeing your family arrayed as the enemy — is the catalyst for the entire Gita. Without this crisis, there is no need for Krishna's teaching. The philosophical depth of the text grows from an intensely personal and emotional seed.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Krishna! as I behold, come here to shed
Their common blood, yon concourse of our kin,
My members fail, my tongue dries in my mouth,
A shudder thrills my body, and my hair
Bristles with horror; from my weak hand slips
Gandiv, the goodly bow; a fever burns
My skin to parching; hardly may I stand;
The life within me seems to swim and faint;
Nothing do I foresee save woe and wail!
It is not good, O Keshav! nought of good
Can spring from mutual slaughter! Lo, I hate
Triumph and domination, wealth and ease,
Thus sadly won! Aho! what victory
Can bring delight, Govinda! what rich spoils
Could profit; what rule recompense; what span
Of life itself seem sweet, bought with such blood?
Seeing that these stand here, ready to die,
For whose sake life was fair, and pleasure pleased,
And power grew precious:-grandsires, sires, and sons,
Brothers, and fathers-in-law, and sons-in-law,
Elders and friends! Shall I deal death on these
Even though they seek to slay us? Not one blow,
O Madhusudan! will I strike to gain
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
Krishna — seeing my own people gathered here to shed each other's blood, my body fails me. My mouth dries. I tremble. My skin burns. The bow Gandiva slips from my hand. I can barely stand.

I see nothing ahead but grief. What good can come from slaughtering our own family? I don't want victory. I don't want kingdoms or pleasure or even life itself, bought at such a price.

The people for whose sake power and pleasure were worth having — grandfathers, fathers, sons, brothers, fathers-in-law — they are standing here, ready to die. Should I kill them? Even if they would kill us? I would not strike a single blow to rule all three worlds — let alone this patch of earth.
D: Elegant: Arjuna's body revolts before his mind can rationalize. His hands shake, his bow drops, his mouth goes dry. 'I see nothing ahead but grief. The people for whose sake life was worth living are standing here ready to die. I would not kill them for dominion over three worlds — let alone this one.'
D: Distilled: Arjuna's body knows what his mind has not yet articulated: killing his family will not be a victory but a destruction of everything victory was meant to serve.
Notes: Gandiva: Arjuna's divine bow, a gift from the fire-god Agni. The physical symptoms — trembling, dry mouth, burning skin, inability to stand — are a realistic depiction of what we now call a panic attack or acute stress response. Arnold's verse captures something often lost in philosophical commentary: the Gita begins not with an intellectual problem but with a visceral, embodied crisis. The body refuses before the mind has finished its reasoning. This connects to the framework: wisdom is not just logos (reasoning) — it involves the whole person.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
The rule of all Three Worlds; then, how much less
To seize an earthly kingdom! Killing these
Must breed but anguish, Krishna! If they be
Guilty, we shall grow guilty by their deaths;
Their sins will light on us, if we shall slay
Those sons of Dhritirashtra, and our kin;
What peace could come of that, O Madhava?
For if indeed, blinded by lust and wrath,
These cannot see, or will not see, the sin
Of kingly lines o'erthrown and kinsmen slain,
How should not we, who see, shun such a crime--
We who perceive the guilt and feel the shame--
O thou Delight of Men, Janardana?
By overthrow of houses perisheth
Their sweet continuous household piety,
And-rites neglected, piety extinct--
Enters impiety upon that home;
Its women grow unwomaned, whence there spring
Mad passions, and the mingling-up of castes,
Sending a Hell-ward road that family,
And whoso wrought its doom by wicked wrath.
Nay, and the souls of honoured ancestors
Fall from their place of peace, being bereft
Of funeral-cakes and the wan death-water.
So teach our holy hymns. Thus, if we slay
Kinsfolk and friends for love of earthly power,
Ahovat! what an evil fault it were!
Better I deem it, if my kinsmen strike,
To face them weaponless, and bare my breast
To shaft and spear, than answer blow with blow.
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
Even if they are blinded by greed and cannot see the sin of destroying their own family line — we can see it. We who perceive the guilt and feel the shame, how could we not turn away from such a crime?

When families are destroyed, the ancient customs that hold them together die with them. Without those customs, lawlessness enters. With lawlessness comes corruption. The women of the family are dishonored, the bloodlines mix, and the whole lineage descends toward ruin. Even the souls of the ancestors suffer, deprived of their funeral rites.

Better, I think, to face my own kinsmen weaponless and bare my chest to their arrows than to answer blow with blow.
D: Elegant: We see the guilt even if they do not. Destroying a family destroys its customs, its piety, its continuity — and the corruption passes down through generations like a curse. Better to die unarmed than to purchase a kingdom with the blood of your own people.
D: Distilled: The cost of victory is the destruction of everything that made victory worth seeking.
Notes: Arjuna's argument is essentially conservative in the framework's sense: he is arguing that the cost of change (war, destruction of family structure, loss of tradition) outweighs any possible gain. His reasoning about social decay — that destroying family customs leads to lawlessness, which leads to moral corruption — is a sophisticated argument about institutional erosion. The 'funeral-cakes and death-water' (pinda and udaka) refer to Hindu ancestral rites (shraddha) essential for the spiritual welfare of the dead. Arjuna's crisis is not cowardice but a genuine ethical impasse — the exact situation where wisdom (not just courage or duty) is needed.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
So speaking, in the face of those two hosts,
Arjuna sank upon his chariot-seat,
And let fall bow and arrows, sick at heart.
D: Refashioned:
[Sanjaya]
Having spoken, Arjuna sank down in his chariot and let his bow and arrows fall. He was overwhelmed with grief.
D: Elegant: Arjuna stops speaking, drops his weapons, and collapses in his chariot — undone not by the enemy but by the weight of what he understands.
D: Distilled: The warrior who sees too clearly to fight has not yet learned to see clearly enough to act.
Notes: This single image — the greatest warrior of his age sitting down in the middle of the battlefield, refusing to fight — is the dramatic setup for the entire Gita. Everything Krishna says for the next 17 chapters is an answer to this moment. The bow (Gandiva) on the ground is the symbol: competence without orientation is paralysis.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
HERE ENDETH CHAPTER I. OF THE BHAGAVAD-GITA,
Entitled "Arjun-Vishad,"
Or "The Book of the Distress of Arjuna."
Notes: Chapter colophon. In the original text, each chapter ends with a formal closing identifying the chapter name and its place within the Mahabharata. 'Arjuna Vishada Yoga' — the Yoga of Arjuna's Despair. Even despair is called a 'yoga' (discipline/path) — suggesting that the crisis itself is a necessary stage of development, not merely a failure to be overcome.

D: Original: Chapter 2: The Yoga of Knowledge (Sankhya Yoga)

[Sanjaya]
Him, filled with such compassion and such grief,
With eyes tear-dimmed, despondent, in stern words
The Driver, Madhusudan, thus addressed:
D: Refashioned:
[Sanjaya]
To Arjuna, filled with compassion and grief, his eyes blurred with tears, Krishna spoke these stern words:
D: Elegant: To the despairing prince, the god speaks — not with comfort but with challenge.
D: Distilled: Krishna's response to Arjuna's despair is not sympathy but confrontation.
Notes: Madhusudan is an epithet of Krishna meaning 'slayer of the demon Madhu.' The transition from Chapter 1 to Chapter 2 shifts from Arjuna's emotional crisis to Krishna's philosophical response. Note that Krishna does not begin gently — he begins with rebuke. This is consistent with the framework's distinction between pathos (emotional activation) and logos (precise reasoning): Krishna refuses to meet Arjuna at the level of feeling and instead pulls him toward reason.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
How hath this weakness taken thee? Whence springs
The inglorious trouble, shameful to the brave,
Barring the path of virtue? Nay, Arjun!
Forbid thyself to feebleness! it mars
Thy warrior-name! cast off the coward-fit!
Wake! Be thyself! Arise, Scourge of thy Foes!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Where does this weakness come from? It is shameful, unworthy of you, and it blocks the path forward. Do not give in to it. This is not who you are. Stand up.
D: Elegant: This cowardice is beneath you. It does not lead to virtue — it blocks it. Wake up. Be who you are.
D: Distilled: The compassion that prevents right action is not virtue — it is weakness wearing virtue's face.
Notes: Krishna's opening move is to reframe Arjuna's compassion as a form of self-indulgence. This is confrontational and counterintuitive — Arjuna's reluctance to kill seems morally admirable. Krishna argues it is actually a failure of duty (dharma) disguised as sensitivity. This parallels the framework's ethos corruption concept: something that looks like wisdom (compassion, restraint) can actually be the opposite (paralysis, abdication of responsibility).

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
How can I, in the battle, shoot with shafts
On Bhishma, or on Drona-O thou Chief!--
Both worshipful, both honourable men?
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
But how can I fight against Bhishma and Drona — men I revere, men worthy of worship?
D: Elegant: How do you shoot arrows at the men who taught you to shoot?
D: Distilled: Arjuna's deepest objection: the people he must fight are the people who formed him.
Notes: Bhishma: patriarch of the Kuru dynasty, Arjuna's great-uncle, legendary warrior who took a vow of celibacy and served as regent. Drona: Arjuna's weapons teacher (guru), who taught him everything he knows about archery and warfare. Arjuna's objection here is not just about killing relatives — it is about destroying the very sources of his own formation. This is the Paideia problem stated in personal terms: what do you owe to the institutions and people who educated you, even when they are on the wrong side?

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Better to live on beggar's bread
With those we love alive,
Than taste their blood in rich feasts spread,
And guiltily survive!
Ah! were it worse-who knows?--to be
Victor or vanquished here,
When those confront us angrily
Whose death leaves living drear?
In pity lost, by doubtings tossed,
My thoughts-distracted-turn
To Thee, the Guide I reverence most,
That I may counsel learn:
I know not what would heal the grief
Burned into soul and sense,
If I were earth's unchallenged chief--
A god--and these gone thence!
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
Better to live as a beggar with my family alive than to feast on wealth won through their blood. I don't even know which outcome would be worse — winning or losing. The people whose death would make life meaningless are standing right in front of me.

I am lost. My mind cannot resolve this. I come to you as a student. Teach me. I don't know what would heal this grief — not if I ruled the whole earth, not if I were a god.
D: Elegant: Victory or defeat — I cannot tell which is worse. The people whose death would empty my life are the enemy. I am lost. I come to you not as a warrior but as a student. Teach me.
D: Distilled: When a warrior reaches the limit of his own judgment, the only honest move is to become a student again.
Notes: This is the pivotal moment of the Gita: Arjuna explicitly surrenders his own judgment and asks Krishna to teach him. 'I come to you as a student' (śiṣyas te 'haṁ) — the formal establishment of the guru-disciple relationship. In the framework's terms, Arjuna has reached the limit of his individual logos and is turning to ethos (trust in a teacher) — but this time the ethos is genuine, because Krishna actually is what he claims to be. The Gita's answer to corrupted ethos is not 'trust no one' but 'find a teacher worthy of trust.'

D: Original:
[Sanjaya]
So spake Arjuna to the Lord of  Hearts,
And sighing,"I will not fight!" held silence then.
To whom, with tender smile, (O Bharata! )
While the Prince wept despairing 'twixt those hosts,
Krishna made answer in divinest verse:
D: Refashioned:
[Sanjaya]
Arjuna said 'I will not fight' and fell silent. Krishna, standing between the two armies, looked at the weeping prince with a gentle smile and began to speak in the most luminous language.
D: Elegant: Arjuna declares he will not fight. Krishna smiles — and begins the teaching that will fill the rest of this book.
D: Distilled: The teaching begins not when the student is ready but when the student admits he is not.
Notes: 'Divinest verse' — Arnold signals that what follows is qualitatively different from the narrative frame. The Gita shifts here from dramatic story to philosophical discourse. Krishna's 'tender smile' in the face of Arjuna's despair is significant: the teacher is neither alarmed by the crisis nor dismissive of it. He has been waiting for exactly this moment — when the student has exhausted his own resources and is genuinely open.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Thou grievest where no grief should be! thou speak'st
Words lacking wisdom! for the wise in heart
Mourn not for those that live, nor those that die.
Nor I, nor thou, nor any one of these,
Ever was not, nor ever will not be,
For ever and for ever afterwards.
All, that doth live, lives always! To man's frame
As there come infancy and youth and age,
So come there raisings-up and layings-down
Of other and of other life-abodes,
Which the wise know, and fear not. This that irks--
Thy sense-life, thrilling to the elements--
Bringing thee heat and cold, sorrows and joys,
'Tis brief and mutable! Bear with it, Prince!
As the wise bear. The soul which is not moved,
The soul that with a strong and constant calm
Takes sorrow and takes joy indifferently,
Lives in the life undying! That which is
Can never cease to be; that which is not
Will not exist. To see this truth of both
Is theirs who part essence from accident,
Substance from shadow. Indestructible,
Learn thou! the Life is, spreading life through all;
It cannot anywhere, by any means,
Be anywise diminished, stayed, or changed.
But for these fleeting frames which it informs
With spirit deathless, endless, infinite,
They perish. Let them perish, Prince! and fight!
He who shall say, "Lo! I have slain a man!"
He who shall think, "Lo! I am slain!" those both
Know naught! Life cannot slay. Life is not slain!
Never the spirit was born; the spirit shall cease to be never;
Never was time it was not; End and Beginning are dreams!
Birthless and deathless and changeless remaineth the spirit for ever;
Death hath not touched it at all, dead though the house of it seems!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
You grieve where grief has no place. You speak as though you have wisdom, but the truly wise do not mourn for the living or the dead.

There was never a time when I did not exist, nor you, nor any of these people — and there will never be a time when we cease to exist. Just as a person passes through childhood, youth, and old age in this body, so the soul passes to another body at death. The wise are not disturbed by this.

What disturbs you — heat, cold, pleasure, pain — these are temporary. They come and go like seasons. Endure them. The person who is not shaken by these, who remains steady in pain and pleasure alike — that person is fit for immortality.

What is real never ceases to be. What is unreal never comes into being. Those who see the truth understand this distinction.
D: Elegant: You speak as if you have wisdom, but the wise do not mourn for the living or the dead. Nothing that is real can be destroyed. Nothing unreal has ever existed. The soul passes through bodies the way a person passes through childhood, youth, and age — and the wise are not disturbed by the transition.
D: Distilled: What is real cannot be destroyed; what is unreal was never there — grief comes from confusing the two.
Notes: This is arguably the most famous passage in the Gita and one of the foundational statements of Vedantic philosophy. The distinction between the real (sat) and the unreal (asat) — 'What is real never ceases to be; what is unreal never comes into being' (nāsato vidyate bhāvo nābhāvo vidyate sataḥ) — maps directly onto the framework's Reality pillar. Krishna's argument is that Arjuna's grief is based on a misunderstanding of what is real: he is mourning for bodies (which are temporary) rather than souls (which are permanent). Whether or not one accepts the metaphysics, the logical structure is important: right action depends on correct understanding of reality. This is the Gita's version of 'Reason serves Reality serves Right.'

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Who knoweth it exhaustless, self-sustained,
Immortal, indestructible,--shall such
Say, "I have killed a man, or caused to kill?"
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The one who understands that the soul is indestructible — exhaustless, self-sustaining, immortal — how can such a person say 'I have killed' or 'I have caused someone to be killed'?
D: Elegant: If the soul cannot be killed, then no one kills and no one dies. The sword passes through what was never vulnerable.
D: Distilled: You cannot destroy what is indestructible — so what exactly are you afraid of losing?
Notes: Krishna's argument proceeds logically: if the soul (Atman) is eternal and indestructible, then killing the body does not kill the person. This is not a license for violence — it is a metaphysical reframing of what death actually means. The framework connection: this is Reality informing Right. Your ethical conclusions depend on your understanding of what exists. If the soul is real and the body temporary, grief over bodily death is a category error.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Nay, but as when one layeth
His worn-out robes away,
And taking new ones, sayeth,
"These will I wear to-day!"
So putteth by the spirit
Lightly its garb of flesh,
And passeth to inherit
A residence afresh.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
As a person discards worn-out clothes and puts on new ones, so the soul discards worn-out bodies and enters new ones.
D: Elegant: The soul changes bodies the way you change clothes — putting aside what is worn out, taking up what is fresh.
D: Distilled: Death is not the end of the wearer but the end of the garment.
Notes: The clothing metaphor (vāsāṃsi jīrṇāni) is one of the Gita's most accessible images for the doctrine of reincarnation (punarjanma/saṃsāra). It works because it separates identity from form: you are not your shirt, and you are not your body. Whether taken literally (as reincarnation doctrine) or metaphorically (as a claim about what persists through change), the image addresses the same question the framework asks: what is the thing that endures versus the thing that decays?

D: Original:
[Krishna]
I say to thee weapons reach not the Life;
Flame burns it not, waters cannot o'erwhelm,
Nor dry winds wither it. Impenetrable,
Unentered, unassailed, unharmed, untouched,
Immortal, all-arriving, stable, sure,
Invisible, ineffable, by word
And thought uncompassed, ever all itself,
Thus is the Soul declared! How wilt thou, then,--
Knowing it so,--grieve when thou shouldst not grieve?
How, if thou hearest that the man new-dead
Is, like the man new-born, still living man--
One same, existent Spirit--wilt thou weep?
The end of birth is death; the end of death
Is birth: this is ordained! and mournest thou,
Chief of the stalwart arm! for what befalls
Which could not otherwise befall? The birth
Of living things comes unperceived; the death
Comes unperceived; between them, beings perceive:
What is there sorrowful herein, dear Prince?
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Weapons cannot reach it. Fire cannot burn it. Water cannot drown it. Wind cannot wither it. It is beyond all physical harm — unpierceable, unburnable, undissolvable, undryable. It is eternal, all-pervading, stable, immovable, everlasting.

Knowing this, why do you grieve? Even if you believe the soul is born and dies with each body — still, why grieve? What is born must die, and what dies must be reborn. You grieve for what is inevitable.

Before birth, beings are unmanifest. Between birth and death, they are manifest. After death, unmanifest again. What is there to lament?
D: Elegant: Weapons cannot cut it, fire cannot burn it, water cannot drown it. It was never born and will never die. Even if you reject this — even if you believe life ends at death — what is born must die, and what dies must be born again. Either way, grief changes nothing.
D: Distilled: Whether the soul is eternal or life is brief, grief over the inevitable is wasted — the only question is what you do with the time you have.
Notes: Krishna offers a two-pronged argument: (1) If the soul is eternal, death is an illusion and grief is misplaced. (2) Even if you don't accept an eternal soul, death is still inevitable for everything born, so grief is still futile. This logical structure — 'my conclusion holds whether or not you accept my metaphysics' — is philosophically sophisticated. It does not require faith; it requires only clear thinking about what follows from either premise. This maps to the framework's Reason pillar: a good argument should work from multiple starting points.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Wonderful, wistful, to contemplate!
Difficult, doubtful, to speak upon!
Strange and great for tongue to relate,
Mystical hearing for every one!
Nor wotteth man this, what a marvel it is,
When seeing, and saying, and hearing are done!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The soul is a wonder to behold, a wonder to speak of, a marvel to hear about — and even after seeing, speaking, and hearing, almost no one truly understands it.
D: Elegant: Everyone marvels at the soul. Almost no one comprehends it.
D: Distilled: The deepest truths are the ones we can describe without understanding.
Notes: A moment of epistemic humility within the teaching itself. Krishna acknowledges that even correct description does not guarantee understanding — the concept can be transmitted without being grasped. This is the telephone problem from the Foundations article stated in spiritual terms: the words survive the transmission, but the understanding may not.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
This Life within all living things, my Prince!
Hides beyond harm; scorn thou to suffer, then,
For that which cannot suffer. Do thy part!
Be mindful of thy name, and tremble not!
Nought better can betide a martial soul
Than lawful war; happy the warrior
To whom comes joy of battle--comes, as now,
Glorious and fair, unsought; opening for him
A gateway unto Heav'n. But, if thou shunn'st
This honourable field--a Kshattriya--
If, knowing thy duty and thy task, thou bidd'st
Duty and task go by--that shall be sin!
And those to come shall speak thee infamy
From age to age; but infamy is worse
For men of noble blood to bear than death!
The chiefs upon their battle-chariots
Will deem 'twas fear that drove thee from the fray.
Of those who held thee mighty-souled the scorn
Thou must abide, while all thine enemies
Will scatter bitter speech of thee, to mock
The valour which thou hadst; what fate could fall
More grievously than this? Either--being killed--
Thou wilt win Swarga's safety, or--alive
And victor--thou wilt reign an earthly king.
Therefore, arise, thou Son of Kunti! brace
Thine arm for conflict, nerve thy heart to meet--
As things alike to thee--pleasure or pain,
Profit or ruin, victory or defeat:
So minded, gird thee to the fight, for so
Thou shalt not sin!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
This life within all living things is beyond harm. Do not grieve for what cannot suffer.

Do your duty. Remember who you are. For a warrior, there is nothing greater than a righteous war. It comes to you unsought, like an open door to heaven. But if you refuse this fight — if you abandon your duty out of fear or sentiment — you will lose both your honor and your purpose.

The world will speak of your disgrace forever. For a man of your reputation, dishonor is worse than death. The great warriors will think you fled from cowardice. Those who respected you will hold you in contempt.

Your enemies will mock you with words too harsh to repeat. What could be more painful than that?

If you are killed, you gain heaven. If you win, you gain the earth. Either way, stand up and fight.
D: Elegant: The soul is beyond harm — so do your duty. A righteous war is a warrior's highest calling. Refuse it, and you lose your honor, your purpose, and the respect of everyone who believed in you. Win, and you gain the earth. Die, and you gain heaven. Either way — stand up.
D: Distilled: When both outcomes lead somewhere worth going, the only failure is refusing to act.
Notes: Krishna shifts from metaphysics to practical ethics — from Reality to Right. His argument: once you understand what is real (the soul is indestructible), the ethical conclusion follows (therefore do your duty without fear of death). This is the Gita's central move: Reality informs Right. The dharma-kshetra (field of duty/righteousness) is also a kuru-kshetra (field of action). The word 'Kshattriya' — Arjuna's warrior caste — carries obligation: your role defines your duty, and abandoning duty is worse than death. This is the 'Right' branch in its most demanding form.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Thus far I speak to thee
As from the "Sankhya"--unspiritually--
Hear now the deeper teaching of the Yog,
Which holding, understanding, thou shalt burst
Thy Karmabandh, the bondage of wrought deeds.
Here shall no end be hindered, no hope marred,
No loss be feared: faith--yea, a little faith--
Shall save thee from the anguish of thy dread.
Here, Glory of the Kurus! shines one rule--
One steadfast rule--while shifting souls have laws
Many and hard. Specious, but wrongful deem
The speech of those ill-taught ones who extol
The letter of their Vedas, saying, "This
Is all we have, or need;" being weak at heart
With wants, seekers of Heaven: which comes--they say--
As "fruit of good deeds done;" promising men
Much profit in new births for works of faith;
In various rites abounding; following whereon
Large merit shall accrue towards wealth and power;
Albeit, who wealth and power do most desire
Least fixity of soul have such, least hold
On heavenly meditation. Much these teach,
From Veds, concerning the "three qualities;"
But thou, be free of the "three qualities,"
Free of the "pairs of opposites," and free
From that sad righteousness which calculates;
Self-ruled, Arjuna! simple, satisfied!
Look! like as when a tank pours water forth
To suit all needs, so do these Brahmans draw
Text for all wants from tank of Holy Writ.
But thou, want not! ask not! Find full reward
Of doing right in right! Let right deeds be
Thy motive, not the fruit which comes from them.
And live in action! Labour! Make thine acts
Thy piety, casting all self aside,
Contemning gain and merit; equable
In good or evil: equability
Is Yog, is piety!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Everything I have said so far has been from the perspective of pure analysis — distinguishing the real from the unreal. Now hear the deeper teaching: the yoga of action.

In this path, no effort is wasted and no progress is lost. Even a small amount of this practice protects you from great fear. The undisciplined mind scatters itself across a thousand desires and never reaches a conclusion. But the resolute mind has one aim.

Those who chase pleasure and power through ritual and scripture, who think heaven is the highest goal — they earn temporary rewards and are reborn into temporary circumstances, endlessly cycling. Their minds are so captured by promises of enjoyment that they never achieve the steady concentration needed for real understanding.

The scriptures deal with the three qualities of nature. Rise above them. Free yourself from the pairs of opposites — pleasure and pain, gain and loss. Rest in pure being. Be yourself.
D: Elegant: The scattered mind chases a thousand desires and reaches none. The resolute mind has one aim. Rise above pleasure and pain, gain and loss. Rest in what you actually are.
D: Distilled: The undisciplined mind fragments across desires; the disciplined mind has one aim and reaches it.
Notes: Krishna introduces the distinction between Sankhya (analytical knowledge) and Yoga (disciplined action). The "three qualities of nature" (triguna) — sattva (clarity), rajas (activity), tamas (inertia) — are central to Hindu philosophy and will be explored fully in Chapter 14. "Rise above the pairs of opposites" (dvandvatito) — the Stoic concept of apatheia has a close parallel here: freedom from being controlled by pleasure/pain reactions. The Vedas (scriptures) are necessary but insufficient — like the framework's distinction between mythos (the stories/rituals) and logos (the understanding behind them).

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Yet, the right act
Is less, far less, than the right-thinking mind.
Seek refuge in thy soul; have there thy heaven!
Scorn them that follow virtue for her gifts!
The mind of pure devotion--even here--
Casts equally aside good deeds and bad,
Passing above them. Unto pure devotion
Devote thyself: with perfect meditation
Comes perfect act, and the right-hearted rise--
More certainly because they seek no gain--
Forth from the bands of body, step by step,
To highest seats of bliss. When thy firm soul
Hath shaken off those tangled oracles
Which ignorantly guide, then shall it soar
To high neglect of what's denied or said,
This way or that way, in doctrinal writ.
Troubled no longer by the priestly lore,
Safe shall it live, and sure; steadfastly bent
On meditation. This is Yog--and Peace!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Right action matters less than right understanding. Seek refuge in your own capacity for wisdom. Scorn those who pursue virtue only for its rewards.

The mind devoted to truth casts aside attachment to both good and bad outcomes. Pure devotion leads to perfect action. Those who act from wisdom, who let go of the fruits of their actions, break free from the cycle of rebirth and reach a state beyond suffering.
D: Elegant: Right action is less than right understanding. Act from wisdom, not for reward. Let go of outcomes — both good and bad — and what remains is freedom.
D: Distilled: Acting for the sake of the outcome enslaves you to the outcome; acting from understanding sets you free.
Notes: The core teaching of Karma Yoga: act without attachment to results (nishkama karma). This does not mean acting without purpose — it means acting from duty and understanding rather than from desire for reward. "Seek refuge in thy soul" (buddhi-sharanam) — buddhi here means the discriminating intellect, the faculty of judgment. This maps to the framework's Reason pillar: the capacity to distinguish what matters from what merely feels important.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
What is his mark who hath that steadfast heart,
Confirmed in holy meditation? How
Know we his speech, Kesava? Sits he, moves he
Like other men?
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
What does a person with this kind of steadfast wisdom actually look like? How do they speak? How do they sit? How do they move through the world?
D: Elegant: If such a person exists — steady, unshaken, wise — what do they actually look like? How would I recognize one?
D: Distilled: The student asks the essential practical question: what does wisdom look like when lived?
Notes: Arjuna's question (sthitaprajna — "one of steady wisdom") shifts from theory to practice. This is one of the most important questions in the Gita because it demands that wisdom be describable in observable terms, not just abstract concepts. It connects directly to the framework's emphasis on Paideia: if you cannot describe what a wise person looks like, you cannot train one.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
When one, O Pritha's Son!
Abandoning desires which shake the mind--
Finds in his soul full comfort for his soul,
He hath attained the Yog--that man is such!
In sorrows not dejected, and in joys
Not overjoyed; dwelling outside the stress
Of passion, fear, and anger; fixed in calms
Of lofty contemplation;--such an one
Is Muni, is the Sage, the true Recluse!
He who to none and nowhere overbound
By ties of flesh, takes evil things and good
Neither desponding nor exulting, such
Bears wisdom's plainest mark! He who shall draw
As the wise tortoise draws its four feet safe
Under its shield, his five frail senses back
Under the spirit's buckler from the world
Which else assails them, such an one, my Prince!
Hath wisdom's mark! Things that solicit sense
Hold off from the self-governed; nay, it comes,
The appetites of him who lives beyond
Depart,--aroused no more. Yet may it chance,
O Son of Kunti! that a governed mind
Shall some time feel the sense-storms sweep, and wrest
Strong self-control by the roots. Let him regain
His kingdom! let him conquer this, and sit
On Me intent. That man alone is wise
Who keeps the mastery of himself! If one
Ponders on objects of the sense, there springs
Attraction; from attraction grows desire,
Desire flames to fierce passion, passion breeds
Recklessness; then the memory--all betrayed--
Lets noble purpose go, and saps the mind,
Till purpose, mind, and man are all undone.
But, if one deals with objects of the sense
Not loving and not hating, making them
Serve his free soul, which rests serenely lord,
Lo! such a man comes to tranquillity;
And out of that tranquillity shall rise
The end and healing of his earthly pains,
Since the will governed sets the soul at peace.
The soul of the ungoverned is not his,
Nor hath he knowledge of himself; which lacked,
How grows serenity? and, wanting that,
Whence shall he hope for happiness?
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The person of steady wisdom is one who has abandoned all desires that agitate the mind and finds complete satisfaction within themselves.

They are not crushed by sorrow or inflated by joy. They live beyond the pull of passion, fear, and anger. They are settled in calm contemplation.

They are not attached to anything — not to good fortune or bad. When good things come, they do not celebrate excessively. When bad things come, they do not despair. They have withdrawn their senses from their objects the way a tortoise draws its limbs into its shell.

The objects of sense withdraw from the person who does not feed them — but the taste for them may linger. Even that taste disappears for the one who has seen the highest truth.
D: Elegant: The wise person finds satisfaction within, is unshaken by fortune or disaster, and has withdrawn from the pull of the senses the way a tortoise draws its limbs into its shell. The objects of desire fall away — and eventually, even the taste for them dissolves.
D: Distilled: Wisdom looks like stillness in the storm — not because the storm is absent but because the anchor holds.
Notes: The tortoise metaphor (kurma) is one of the Gita's most famous images for sense control (pratyahara). Krishna's description of the sthitaprajna (person of steady wisdom) parallels the Stoic sage: not someone who feels nothing but someone whose feelings do not determine their actions. The distinction between objects of sense withdrawing and the "taste" (rasa) lingering is psychologically precise — you can stop pursuing something and still crave it. Only direct experience of something higher dissolves the craving itself.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
The mind
That gives itself to follow shows of sense
Seeth its helm of wisdom rent away,
And, like a ship in waves of whirlwind, drives
To wreck and death. Only with him, great Prince!
Whose senses are not swayed by things of sense--
Only with him who holds his mastery,
Shows wisdom perfect. What is midnight-gloom
To unenlightened souls shines wakeful day
To his clear gaze; what seems as wakeful day
Is known for night, thick night of ignorance,
To his true-seeing eyes. Such is the Saint!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The mind that chases sensory pleasures loses its capacity for wisdom — like a ship in a storm, driven to wreck. Only the person whose senses are not mastered by their objects possesses true wisdom.

What seems like darkness to the unenlightened is daylight to the wise. What the world considers waking consciousness, the sage sees as sleep.
D: Elegant: The senses are a storm; follow them and they wreck you. What the world calls waking, the sage calls sleep. What the world calls darkness, the sage sees clearly.
D: Distilled: Wisdom and convention see opposite things — what the crowd calls light, the sage calls blindness.
Notes: The inversion — "what is night to all beings is wakeful day to the sage" — is a direct parallel to Plato's Cave allegory: the philosopher who has seen the light perceives reality where others perceive shadow, and vice versa. Both traditions arrived at the same metaphor independently: the wise person's perception is inverted relative to the conventional world.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
And like the ocean, day by day receiving
Floods from all lands, which never overflows
Its boundary-line not leaping, and not leaving,
Fed by the rivers, but unswelled by those;--
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The perfected person is like the ocean: rivers pour into it constantly, yet it never overflows its boundaries.
D: Elegant: Rivers pour endlessly into the ocean, yet the ocean remains the ocean — unchanged by what enters it.
D: Distilled: The wise absorb everything the world sends and remain unchanged.
Notes: The ocean metaphor for equanimity: the sage receives all sensory input, all experiences, all provocations — and none of them alter the fundamental nature. This is not suppression but capacity. A cup overflows; an ocean does not.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
So is the perfect one! to his soul's ocean
The world of sense pours streams of witchery;
They leave him as they find, without commotion,
Taking their tribute, but remaining sea.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
So it is with the perfected soul. The world pours its streams of temptation, its witchery, into the sage — and they leave the sage as they found them, without disturbance. Taking their tribute of experience, but remaining sea.
D: Elegant: The world pours its temptations into the sage and finds them unchanged — experienced but undisturbed, like an ocean that receives every river without rising.
D: Distilled: The sage experiences everything and is mastered by nothing.
Notes: Continuation of the ocean metaphor. Arnold's "witchery" translates maya-related concepts — the enchanting, seductive quality of sensory experience. The sage is not immune to experience but is not controlled by it. This is the Stoic distinction between impression (phantasia) and assent (synkatathesis): you cannot prevent impressions from arriving, but you can choose not to be moved by them.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Yea! whoso, shaking off the yoke of flesh
Lives lord, not servant, of his lusts; set free
From pride, from passion, from the sin of "Self,"
Toucheth tranquillity! O Pritha's Son!
That is the state of Brahm! There rests no dread
When that last step is reached! Live where he will,
Die when he may, such passeth from all 'plaining,
To blest Nirvana, with the Gods, attaining.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The person who shakes off attachment to the body, who lives as master rather than servant of their desires, who is free from pride, passion, and ego — that person touches tranquility.

This is the state of Brahman — the ultimate reality. Once reached, there is no falling back. Whoever attains this, even at the moment of death, passes into union with the infinite.
D: Elegant: Free from desire, free from pride, free from the illusion of self — this is the state of Brahman. Whoever reaches it, even at the last moment, does not fall back.
D: Distilled: Freedom is not the absence of the world but the end of being enslaved by it.
Notes: Brahma-nirvana — union with Brahman, liberation. This closing verse of Chapter 2 names the goal: not escape from life but freedom within it. The "sin of Self" (ahamkara, ego-identification) is the root obstacle. Compare with the framework: wisdom is not withdrawal from the world but correct orientation within it. The chapter began with Arjuna's paralysis and ends with Krishna's vision of perfect action arising from perfect understanding.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
HERE ENDETH CHAPTER II. OF THE BHAGAVAD-GITA,
Entitled "Sankhya-Yog,"
Or "The Book of Doctrines."
Notes: Chapter colophon. Sankhya Yoga — the Yoga of Knowledge/Doctrine. This chapter introduced the Gita's core metaphysics (the eternal soul), its core ethics (act from duty, not desire), and its portrait of the wise person (sthitaprajna). These three correspond roughly to Reality, Right, and Wisdom in the framework.

D: Original: Chapter 3: The Yoga of Action (Karma Yoga)

[Arjuna]
Thou whom all mortals praise, Janardana!
If meditation be a nobler thing
Than action, wherefore, then, great Kesava!
Dost thou impel me to this dreadful fight?
Now am I by thy doubtful speech disturbed!
Tell me one thing, and tell me certainly;
By what road shall I find the better end?
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
If you say that understanding is superior to action, then why are you pushing me toward this terrible fight? Your words seem contradictory and they confuse me. Tell me clearly — which path leads to the better outcome?
D: Elegant: If wisdom matters more than action, why send me into battle? Pick one path and tell me clearly — thinking or fighting?
D: Distilled: If understanding is higher than action, why must I act? The student demands consistency from the teacher.
Notes: Arjuna identifies an apparent contradiction in Krishna's teaching: Chapter 2 praised knowledge and detachment, but also commanded Arjuna to fight. This is the perennial tension between contemplation and action — the same tension the framework addresses through the feedback loop: understanding (Reason/Reality) must produce action (Right), and action must feed back into understanding. They are not alternatives but phases of a cycle.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
I told thee, blameless Lord! there be two paths
Shown to this world; two schools of wisdom.

First
The Sankhya's, which doth save in way of works
Prescribed by reason; next, the Yog, which bids
Attain by meditation, spiritually:
Yet these are one! No man shall 'scape from act
By shunning action; nay, and none shall come
By mere renouncements unto perfectness.
Nay, and no jot of time, at any time,
Rests any actionless; his nature's law
Compels him, even unwilling, into act;
[For thought is act in fancy]. He who sits
Suppressing all the instruments of flesh,
Yet in his idle heart thinking on them,
Plays the inept and guilty hypocrite:
But he who, with strong body serving mind,
Gives up his mortal powers to worthy work,
Not seeking gain, Arjuna! such an one
Is honourable. Do thine allotted task!
Work is more excellent than idleness;
The body's life proceeds not, lacking work.
There is a task of holiness to do,
Unlike world-binding toil, which bindeth not
The faithful soul; such earthly duty do
Free from desire, and thou shalt well perform
Thy heavenly purpose. Spake Prajapati--
In the beginning, when all men were made,
And, with mankind, the sacrifice-- "Do this!
Work! sacrifice! Increase and multiply
With sacrifice! This shall be Kamaduk,
Your 'Cow of Plenty,' giving back her milk
Of all abundance. Worship the gods thereby;
The gods shall yield thee grace. Those meats ye crave
The gods will grant to Labour, when it pays
Tithes in the altar-flame. But if one eats
Fruits of the earth, rendering to kindly Heaven
No gift of toil, that thief steals from his world."
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
I told you there are two paths — the path of knowledge and the path of action. But they are not separate. No one escapes action by refusing to act, and no one reaches perfection through renunciation alone.

No one can sit still even for a moment — nature compels everyone to act. The person who restrains their body but dwells on pleasures in their mind is a fraud. But the one who controls their mind and engages their body in selfless work — that person is superior.

Do your allotted work, for action is better than inaction. Even maintaining your body requires action.

All work binds the doer — except work done as sacrifice, as offering, without attachment to results. Perform your actions in that spirit and you will remain free.
D: Elegant: Two paths — knowledge and action — but they are one. No one escapes action by sitting still; nature compels. The one who controls the body but fantasizes is a fraud. The one who masters the mind and acts without attachment is free. Do your work as an offering, not an investment.
D: Distilled: You cannot escape action — even inaction is a choice. The only freedom is in acting without attachment to the result.
Notes: Chapter 3 resolves the knowledge-vs-action tension: they are two aspects of the same path (jnana-karma-yoga). "Work done as sacrifice" (yajnartha) — the concept of yajna (sacrifice/offering) is being radically reinterpreted from ritual sacrifice to any selfless action performed without personal attachment. This connects to the framework's "Right" pillar: the question is not whether to act but how to act rightly. Inaction is not neutral — it is itself an action with consequences.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Who eat of food after their sacrifice
Are quit of fault, but they that spread a feast
All for themselves, eat sin and drink of sin.
By food the living live; food comes of rain,
And rain comes by the pious sacrifice,
And sacrifice is paid with tithes of toil;
Thus action is of Brahma, who is One,
The Only, All-pervading; at all times
Present in sacrifice. He that abstains
To help the rolling wheels of this great world,
Glutting his idle sense, lives a lost life,
Shameful and vain. Existing for himself,
Self-concentrated, serving self alone,
No part hath he in aught; nothing achieved,
Nought wrought or unwrought toucheth him; no hope
Of help for all the living things of earth
Depends from him. Therefore, thy task prescribed
With spirit unattached gladly perform,
Since in performance of plain duty man
Mounts to his highest bliss. By works alone
Janak and ancient saints reached blessedness!
Moreover, for the upholding of thy kind,
Action thou should'st embrace. What the wise choose
The unwise people take; what best men do
The multitude will follow. Look on me,
Thou Son of Pritha! in the three wide worlds
I am not bound to any toil, no height
Awaits to scale, no gift remains to gain,
Yet I act here! and, if I acted not--
Earnest and watchful--those that look to me
For guidance, sinking back to sloth again
Because I slumbered, would decline from good,
And I should break earth's order and commit
Her offspring unto ruin, Bharata!
Even as the unknowing toil, wedded to sense,
So let the enlightened toil, sense-freed, but set
To bring the world deliverance, and its bliss;
Not sowing in those simple, busy hearts
Seed of despair. Yea! let each play his part
In all he finds to do, with unyoked soul.
All things are everywhere by Nature wrought
In interaction of the qualities.
The fool, cheated by self, thinks, "This I did"
And "That I wrought; "but--ah, thou strong-armed Prince!--
A better-lessoned mind, knowing the play
Of visible things within the world of sense,
And how the qualities must qualify,
Standeth aloof even from his acts. Th' untaught
Live mixed with them, knowing not Nature's way,
Of highest aims unwitting, slow and dull.
Those make thou not to stumble, having the light;
But all thy dues discharging, for My sake,
With meditation centred inwardly,
Seeking no profit, satisfied, serene,
Heedless of issue--fight! They who shall keep
My ordinance thus, the wise and willing hearts,
Have quittance from all issue of their acts;
But those who disregard My ordinance,
Thinking they know, know nought, and fall to loss,
Confused and foolish. 'Sooth, the instructed one
Doth of his kind, following what fits him most:
And lower creatures of their kind; in vain
Contending 'gainst the law. Needs must it be
The objects of the sense will stir the sense
To like and dislike, yet th' enlightened man
Yields not to these, knowing them enemies.
Finally, this is better, that one do
His own task as he may, even though he fail,
Than take tasks not his own, though they seem good.
To die performing duty is no ill;
But who seeks other roads shall wander still.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The cosmos runs on sacrifice — on giving, offering, and cycling. Rain feeds the earth, which feeds the people, who offer back through work and worship, which sustains the cosmic order. Those who eat without offering break this cycle and consume only sin.

But whoever understands this — whoever acts not from desire but from duty, doing what must be done because it must be done — that person is liberated even while acting.

Look at King Janaka and others like him: they achieved perfection through action, not renunciation. Consider what your action means for others — for the world takes its cue from its leaders. Whatever a great person does, others follow. Whatever standards they set, the world adopts.

I myself have nothing to gain in all three worlds. Nothing I need to acquire. Yet I continue to act — because if I stopped, the world would follow my example into chaos. The wise must act, just as the ignorant act — but the wise act without attachment, for the welfare of the world.
D: Elegant: The cosmos is a cycle of offering: rain feeds earth, earth feeds people, people offer back through work. Break the cycle and you consume only sin. Even God acts — not from need but to keep the world from following inaction into chaos. The wise act as the ignorant do, but without attachment and for the welfare of all.
D: Distilled: Even those who have nothing to gain must act — because inaction from the wise teaches the world to stop caring.
Notes: Krishna reveals that he himself continues to act despite having nothing to gain — setting the model for selfless action (nishkama karma). "Whatever a great person does, others follow" (yad yad acarati sresthas) — this is the Grandfather Principle from the framework stated in Hindu terms: leaders and exemplars shape the culture that shapes the next generation. The reference to King Janaka (ruler of Mithila, father of Sita) is an example of a philosopher-king who achieved liberation while remaining active in governance — the same ideal Plato proposed.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Yet tell me, Teacher! by what force doth man
Go to his ill, unwilling; as if one
Pushed him that evil path?
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
But what pushes a person toward evil, even against their own will? It feels as if something forces them down the wrong path.
D: Elegant: What is the force that drives us to do wrong even when we know better — as if pushed by something we cannot see?
D: Distilled: Why do we act against our own understanding? What force overrides what we know to be right?
Notes: Arjuna asks about akrasia — acting against one's better judgment. This is the same problem Socrates wrestled with (Protagoras) and that Aristotle analyzed in detail (Nicomachean Ethics VII). The question is universal: if knowing the right thing were sufficient to do it, ethics would be easy. The gap between knowing and doing is where most of the difficulty of Right lives.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Kama it is!
Passion it is! born of the Darknesses,
Which pusheth him. Mighty of appetite,
Sinful, and strong is this!--man's enemy!
As smoke blots the white fire, as clinging rust
Mars the bright mirror, as the womb surrounds
The babe unborn, so is the world of things
Foiled, soiled, enclosed in this desire of flesh.
The wise fall, caught in it; the unresting foe
It is of wisdom, wearing countless forms,
Fair but deceitful, subtle as a flame.
Sense, mind, and reason--these, O Kunti's Son!
Are booty for it; in its play with these
It maddens man, beguiling, blinding him.
Therefore, thou noblest child of Bharata!
Govern thy heart! Constrain th' entangled sense!
Resist the false, soft sinfulness which saps
Knowledge and judgment! Yea, the world is strong,
But what discerns it stronger, and the mind
Strongest; and high o'er all the ruling Soul.
Wherefore, perceiving Him who reigns supreme,
Put forth full force of Soul in thy own soul!
Fight! vanquish foes and doubts, dear Hero! slay
What haunts thee in fond shapes, and would betray!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
It is desire. It is anger. Both born from rajas — the quality of restless passion. Desire is powerful, consuming, and insatiable. It is the great enemy.

As smoke obscures fire, as rust corrodes a mirror, as the womb encloses the unborn child — so desire wraps around wisdom and hides it. It takes many forms and is never satisfied. It lodges in the senses, the mind, and the intellect, and through these it deludes the embodied soul.

Therefore, master your senses first. Discipline them. Cut off this enemy that destroys knowledge and discrimination.

The senses are powerful — but the mind is above the senses. The intellect is above the mind. And above the intellect is the Self. Knowing this — knowing what is above what — steady yourself with yourself, and destroy this enemy called desire.
D: Elegant: It is desire — born of passion, insatiable, the enemy of wisdom. It hides knowledge the way smoke hides fire, rust corrodes a mirror, the womb encloses the child. Master your senses, then your mind, then your intellect — and above all three, find the Self that governs them.
D: Distilled: Desire does not oppose wisdom directly — it obscures it, the way smoke obscures fire. The fire is still there.
Notes: Kama (desire) and krodha (anger) are identified as the two primary obstacles. The three metaphors for how desire conceals wisdom are graded: smoke on fire (easily cleared), rust on mirror (requires effort), womb around embryo (deeply enclosed, only time reveals). The hierarchy — senses < mind < intellect < Self (atman) — provides a practical map for self-mastery. This connects to the framework: the tools of Reason must be applied not just to external reality but to the internal landscape of desire and bias that distorts our judgment. Compare with the "fight our own minds" passage in the Foundations article.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
HERE ENDETH CHAPTER III. OF THE BHAGAVAD-GITA,
Entitled "Karma-Yog,"
Or "The Book of Virtue in Work."
Notes: Chapter colophon. Karma Yoga — the Yoga of Action. This chapter resolved the knowledge-vs-action paradox: they are not alternatives but aspects of the same path. Act, but without attachment. The key insight for the framework: wisdom is not contemplation separate from action but the quality of understanding that informs right action.

D: Original: Chapter 4: The Yoga of Wisdom (Jnana Yoga)

[Krishna]
This deathless Yoga, this deep union,
I taught Vivaswata, the Lord of Light;
Vivaswata to Manu gave it; he
To Ikshwaku; so passed it down the line
Of all my royal Rishis. Then, with years,
The truth grew dim and perished, noble Prince!
Now once again to thee it is declared--
This ancient lore, this mystery supreme--
Seeing I find thee votary and friend.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
This eternal teaching I first gave to Vivaswata, the Lord of Light, who passed it to Manu, who passed it to Ikshwaku, and so it traveled down through the generations of royal sages. But over time the teaching was lost — the truth grew dim and perished.

Now I give it to you again, this ancient knowledge, this supreme mystery — because you are my devotee and my friend.
D: Elegant: I taught this truth at the beginning of time. It passed through generations of kings and sages — and was lost. Now I teach it again to you, because you are ready and because you are my friend.
D: Distilled: The deepest teachings are not lost because they are wrong but because transmission fails — and must be renewed in each age.
Notes: Vivaswata (the Sun god), Manu (progenitor of humanity), Ikshwaku (first king of the solar dynasty) — Krishna places the Gita's teaching in a lineage stretching to the origin of the cosmos. 'The truth grew dim and perished' (sa kāleneha mahatā yogo naṣṭaḥ) is the Gita's own statement of the erosion problem. The teaching was not refuted — it was lost through time. This is the telephone problem from the Foundations article stated as divine autobiography.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Thy birth, dear Lord, was in these later days,
And bright Vivaswata's preceded time!
How shall I comprehend this thing thou sayest,
"From the beginning it was I who taught?"
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
But you were born recently, and Vivaswata lived at the beginning of time. How can you say that you taught this teaching at the beginning?
D: Elegant: You were born in this age. Vivaswata was born at the dawn of creation. How can you claim to have been the original teacher?
D: Distilled: The student asks: how can someone born now claim to have taught at the beginning of time?
Notes: Arjuna's question sets up Krishna's revelation of his divine nature — that he is not merely a human teacher but an avatar, God incarnate. The question is also rational: Arjuna is applying logos (logical scrutiny) to ethos (the teacher's claims). Good students do this.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Manifold the renewals of my birth
Have been, Arjuna! and of thy births, too!
But mine I know, and thine thou knowest not,
O Slayer of thy Foes! Albeit I be
Unborn, undying, indestructible,
The Lord of all things living; not the less--
By Maya, by my magic which I stamp
On floating Nature-forms, the primal vast--
I come, and go, and come. When Righteousness
Declines, O Bharata! when Wickedness
Is strong, I rise, from age to age, and take
Visible shape, and move a man with men,
Succouring the good, thrusting the evil back,
And setting Virtue on her seat again.
Who knows the truth touching my births on earth
And my divine work, when he quits the flesh
Puts on its load no more, falls no more down
To earthly birth: to Me he comes, dear Prince!
Many there be who come! from fear set free,
From anger, from desire; keeping their hearts
Fixed upon me--my Faithful--purified
By sacred flame of Knowledge. Such as these
Mix with my being. Whoso worship me,
Them I exalt; but all men everywhere
Shall fall into my path; albeit, those souls
Which seek reward for works, make sacrifice
Now, to the lower gods. I say to thee
Here have they their reward. But I am He
Made the Four Castes, and portioned them a place
After their qualities and gifts. Yea, I
Created, the Reposeful; I that live
Immortally, made all those mortal births:
For works soil not my essence, being works
Wrought uninvolved. Who knows me acting thus
Unchained by action, action binds not him;
And, so perceiving, all those saints of old
Worked, seeking for deliverance. Work thou
As, in the days gone by, thy fathers did.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Both you and I have been born many times, Arjuna. The difference is that I remember all my births and you do not.

I am unborn, undying, and indestructible — the lord of all beings. And yet, through my own creative power, I take form and appear in the world. Whenever righteousness declines and wickedness rises, I manifest myself. Age after age I return — to protect the good, to destroy evil, and to reestablish the right order of things.

Whoever truly understands my divine birth and purpose is not reborn at death but comes to me.

Free from passion, fear, and anger, absorbed in me, purified by the fire of knowledge — many have reached my nature. However people approach me, I meet them on their path. All paths lead to me.

Those who desire quick success in this world make offerings to lesser gods — and they get quick results, because results from ritual action come easily in the human world. But I created the four orders of society according to people's qualities and work — though I, the creator, do not act and am not bound by what I create.
D: Elegant: I have been born countless times. You have too — but I remember and you do not. Though I am unborn and eternal, I choose to appear whenever righteousness declines and wickedness rises. Age after age I return to protect the good and reestablish order. However people approach me, I meet them where they are. All paths lead to me.
D: Distilled: Whenever the world loses its way, the teaching returns — in whatever form the age requires.
Notes: This is Krishna's theophany — his revelation that he is not merely a wise teacher but God (Vishnu/Brahman) incarnate. The avatar doctrine (avatāra = 'descent'): God descends into the world periodically to correct the balance. 'Whenever righteousness declines' (yadā yadā hi dharmasya glānir bhavati) is one of the most quoted verses in Hinduism. The framework parallel: wisdom doesn't maintain itself — it requires active intervention to restore it when it erodes. The difference is that the Gita frames restoration as divine action, while the framework frames it as human responsibility. But the diagnosis is identical: the erosion is cyclical and the correction must be deliberate.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Thou sayst, perplexed, It hath been asked before
By singers and by sages, "What is act,
And what inaction? "I will teach thee this,
And, knowing, thou shalt learn which work doth save
Needs must one rightly meditate those three--
Doing,--not doing,--and undoing. Here
Thorny and dark the path is! He who sees
How action may be rest, rest action--he
Is wisest 'mid his kind; he hath the truth!
He doeth well, acting or resting. Freed
In all his works from prickings of desire,
Burned clean in act by the white fire of truth,
The wise call that man wise; and such an one,
Renouncing fruit of deeds, always content.
Always self-satisfying, if he works,
Doth nothing that shall stain his separate soul,
Which--quit of fear and hope--subduing self--
Rejecting outward impulse--yielding up
To body's need nothing save body, dwells
Sinless amid all sin, with equal calm
Taking what may befall, by grief unmoved,
Unmoved by joy, unenvyingly; the same
In good and evil fortunes; nowise bound
By bond of deeds. Nay, but of such an one,
Whose crave is gone, whose soul is liberate,
Whose heart is set on truth--of such an one
What work he does is work of sacrifice,
Which passeth purely into ash and smoke
Consumed upon the altar! All's then God!
The sacrifice is Brahm, the ghee and grain
Are Brahm, the fire is Brahm, the flesh it eats
Is Brahm, and unto Brahm attaineth he
Who, in such office, meditates on Brahm.
Some votaries there be who serve the gods
With flesh and altar-smoke; but other some
Who, lighting subtler fires, make purer rite
With will of worship. Of the which be they
Who, in white flame of continence, consume
Joys of the sense, delights of eye and ear,
Forgoing tender speech and sound of song:
And they who, kindling fires with torch of Truth,
Burn on a hidden altar-stone the bliss
Of youth and love, renouncing happiness:
And they who lay for offering there their wealth,
Their penance, meditation, piety,
Their steadfast reading of the scrolls, their lore
Painfully gained with long austerities:
And they who, making silent sacrifice,
Draw in their breath to feed the flame of thought,
And breathe it forth to waft the heart on high,
Governing the ventage of each entering air
Lest one sigh pass which helpeth not the soul:
And they who, day by day denying needs,
Lay life itself upon the altar-flame,
Burning the body wan. Lo! all these keep
The rite of offering, as if they slew
Victims; and all thereby efface much sin.
Yea! and who feed on the immortal food
Left of such sacrifice, to Brahma pass,
To The Unending. But for him that makes
No sacrifice, he hath nor part nor lot
Even in the present world. How should he share
Another, O thou Glory of thy Line?
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
What is action? What is inaction? Even the wise are confused by this question.

The one who sees inaction in action and action in inaction — that person is wise among all people, and is a true yogi, performing all actions well.

The person whose every undertaking is free from the desire for results, whose actions are burned clean in the fire of knowledge — the wise call such a person learned. Abandoning attachment to the fruits of action, always content, depending on nothing external — such a person acts, yet truly does nothing at all.

Without hope, with mind and body controlled, giving up all possessions, performing only the physical act without claiming ownership of it — such a person incurs no fault. Content with whatever comes unsought, beyond the pairs of opposites, free from envy, steady in success and failure alike — even while acting, such a person is not bound.
D: Elegant: See inaction in action and action in inaction — that is wisdom. The truly learned act without attachment to results. Their work is burned clean in the fire of knowledge. They act, yet they are bound by nothing they do.
D: Distilled: The wise act fully while remaining free from the outcomes of their actions — this is the deepest form of doing.
Notes: The paradox of 'inaction in action' (akarma in karma) is the Gita's most subtle teaching on Karma Yoga. It means: you can be physically active while spiritually detached, or physically still while mentally agitated. The quality of action is determined by the internal state, not the external motion. 'Burned clean in the fire of knowledge' (jñānāgni-dagdha-karmāṇam) — knowledge purifies action the way fire purifies gold. This connects to the framework: Right (action) must be informed by Reason (knowledge) or it becomes either blind action or paralyzed inaction.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
In sight of Brahma all these offerings
Are spread and are accepted! Comprehend
That all proceed by act; for knowing this,
Thou shalt be quit of doubt. The sacrifice
Which Knowledge pays is better than great gifts
Offered by wealth, since gifts' worth--O my Prince!
Lies in the mind which gives, the will that serves:
And these are gained by reverence, by strong search,
By humble heed of those who see the Truth
And teach it. Knowing Truth, thy heart no more
Will ache with error, for the Truth shall show
All things subdued to thee, as thou to Me.
Moreover, Son of Pandu! wert thou worst
Of all wrong-doers, this fair ship of Truth
Should bear thee safe and dry across the sea
Of thy transgressions. As the kindled flame
Feeds on the fuel till it sinks to ash,
So unto ash, Arjuna! unto nought
The flame of Knowledge wastes works' dross away!
There is no purifier like thereto
In all this world, and he who seeketh it
Shall find it--being grown perfect--in himself.
Believing, he receives it when the soul
Masters itself, and cleaves to Truth, and comes--
Possessing knowledge--to the higher peace,
The uttermost repose. But those untaught,
And those without full faith, and those who fear
Are shent; no peace is here or other where,
No hope, nor happiness for whoso doubts.
He that, being self-contained, hath vanquished doubt,
Disparting self from service, soul from works,
Enlightened and emancipate, my Prince!
Works fetter him no more! Cut then atwain
With sword of wisdom, Son of Bharata!
This doubt that binds thy heart-beats! cleave the bond
Born of thy ignorance! Be bold and wise!
Give thyself to the field with me! Arise!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The sacrifice of knowledge is superior to any material offering — because all material sacrifice ultimately resolves into knowledge. Seek that knowledge through reverence, through inquiry, and through humble service to those who have seen the truth and can teach it.

Knowing the truth, you will never again fall into confusion. You will see all beings in yourself and in me. Even if you were the worst of sinners, the boat of knowledge would carry you across all wrongdoing.

As fire reduces wood to ash, so the fire of knowledge reduces all actions to ash. There is nothing in this world as purifying as knowledge. The person who has perfected yoga finds it within themselves, in time.

The faithful attain knowledge. The doubter is destroyed — for the doubting soul has neither this world nor the next, nor any happiness. Therefore, with the sword of knowledge, cut through the doubt in your heart. Stand up, and act.
D: Elegant: The sacrifice of knowledge is greater than any material offering, because all sacrifice resolves into understanding. Seek truth through humility and inquiry. Knowledge is fire — it reduces all accumulated karma to ash. Nothing in this world purifies like understanding. Cut through doubt with the sword of knowledge, and act.
D: Distilled: Knowledge is the fire that burns through everything — confusion, guilt, doubt — and what remains is the freedom to act.
Notes: Chapter 4 culminates in the supremacy of knowledge (jñāna) over ritual action. 'The sacrifice of knowledge' (jñāna-yajña) redefines sacrifice from ritual to intellectual and spiritual practice. 'Those who have seen the truth and can teach it' (tattva-darśinaḥ) — the emphasis on finding genuine teachers (not sophists) echoes the framework's ethos discussion. The chapter ends with 'cut through doubt with the sword of knowledge' (jñānāsinātmanaḥ... chittvaиnam saṁśayam) — a call to decisive action rooted in understanding, not blind faith.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
HERE ENDETH CHAPTER IV. OF THE BHAGAVAD-GITA,
Entitled "Jnana Yog,"
Or "The Book of the Religion of Knowledge,"
Notes: Chapter colophon. Jnana Yoga — the Yoga of Knowledge/Wisdom. This chapter established: (1) the teaching is ancient and periodically lost (the erosion), (2) God incarnates to restore it (the correction), (3) knowledge is the highest form of sacrifice, (4) genuine teachers must be sought with humility. The framework parallel is direct: wisdom erodes, must be deliberately restored, and knowledge (logos) is the supreme tool for that restoration.

D: Original: Chapter 5: The Yoga of Renunciation (Karma Sannyasa Yoga)

[Arjuna]
Yet, Krishna! at the one time thou dost laud
Surcease of works, and, at another time,
Service through work. Of these twain plainly tell
Which is the better way?
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
You praise renunciation of action, and then you praise action itself. Which is actually better? Tell me plainly.
D: Elegant: You praise both doing and not-doing. Which is it? Tell me one thing clearly.
D: Distilled: The student asks the teacher to stop being paradoxical and give a straight answer.
Notes: Arjuna voices the reader's frustration. Krishna has praised both renunciation (sannyasa) and action (karma yoga), and they seem contradictory.
