This is a recurring pattern in the Gita: the student pushes back, forcing the teacher to resolve apparent contradictions rather than leaving them as comfortable ambiguities.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
To cease from works
Is well, and to do works in holiness
Is well; and both conduct to bliss supreme;
But of these twain the better way is his
Who working piously refraineth not.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Both renunciation and selfless action lead to liberation. But of the two, selfless action is better — because it is easier and more natural. The true renouncer is not the one who gives up all activity but the one who acts without attachment and without aversion, free from the pull of opposites.
D: Elegant: Both paths lead to liberation, but selfless action is the better road — because the true renouncer is not the one who stops acting but the one who acts without being moved by the outcome.
D: Distilled: Real renunciation is not giving up action but giving up attachment to what action produces.
Notes: Krishna resolves the paradox: renunciation and action are not opposed. True renunciation (sannyasa) is internal — it means renouncing attachment to results, not renouncing activity itself. This parallels the Stoic distinction between what is 'up to us' (our intentions and judgments) and what is not (outcomes). The framework connection: Right is about orientation and aim, not about withdrawal from the world.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
That is the true Renouncer, firm and fixed,
Who--seeking nought, rejecting nought--dwells proof
Against the "opposites." O valiant Prince!
In doing, such breaks lightly from all deed:
'Tis the new scholar talks as they were two,
This Sankhya and this Yoga: wise men know
Who husbands one plucks golden fruit of both!
The region of high rest which Sankhyans reach
Yogins attain. Who sees these twain as one
Sees with clear eyes! Yet such abstraction, Chief!
Is hard to win without much holiness.
Whoso is fixed in holiness, self-ruled,
Pure-hearted, lord of senses and of self,
Lost in the common life of all which lives--
A "Yogayukt"--he is a Saint who wends
Straightway to Brahm. Such an one is not touched
By taint of deeds. "Nought of myself I do!"
Thus will he think-who holds the truth of truths--
In seeing, hearing, touching, smelling; when
He eats, or goes, or breathes; slumbers or talks,
Holds fast or loosens, opes his eyes or shuts;
Always assured "This is the sense-world plays
With senses."He that acts in thought of Brahm,
Detaching end from act, with act content,
The world of sense can no more stain his soul
Than waters mar th' enamelled lotus-leaf.
With life, with heart, with mind,-nay, with the help
Of all five senses--letting selfhood go--
Yogins toil ever towards their souls' release.
Such votaries, renouncing fruit of deeds,
Gain endless peace: the unvowed, the passion-bound,
Seeking a fruit from works, are fastened down.
The embodied sage, withdrawn within his soul,
At every act sits godlike in "the town
Which hath nine gateways," neither doing aught
Nor causing any deed. This world's Lord makes
Neither the work, nor passion for the work,
Nor lust for fruit of work; the man's own self
Pushes to these! The Master of this World
Takes on himself the good or evil deeds
Of no man--dwelling beyond! Mankind errs here
By folly, darkening knowledge. But, for whom
That darkness of the soul is chased by light,
Splendid and clear shines manifest the Truth
As if a Sun of Wisdom sprang to shed
Its beams of dawn. Him meditating still,
Him seeking, with Him blended, stayed on Him,
The souls illuminated take that road
Which hath no turning back--their sins flung off
By strength of faith. [Who will may have this Light;
Who hath it sees.] To him who wisely sees,
The Brahman with his scrolls and sanctities,
The cow, the elephant, the unclean dog,
The Outcast gorging dog's meat, are all one.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Only the immature think knowledge and action are different paths. The wise know they are one — whoever walks either path fully arrives at the same destination.

The person whose soul is pure, whose self is mastered, whose senses are controlled, whose being is unified with all beings — such a person is not stained by action, even while acting.

The disciplined one, knowing the truth, understands: 'I do nothing at all.' Seeing, hearing, touching, smelling, eating, walking, sleeping, breathing — in all these the wise person recognizes that it is only the senses moving among their objects, and remains uninvolved.

Offering all actions to the infinite, abandoning attachment, such a person moves through the world untouched by sin — like a lotus leaf that water cannot wet.
D: Elegant: The immature separate knowledge from action. The wise see them as one path. Act with mastery and detachment, and you pass through the world like water on a lotus leaf — touching everything, stained by nothing.
D: Distilled: Knowledge and action are not two paths but one — the lotus moves through water without getting wet.
Notes: The lotus leaf metaphor (padma-patram ivāmbhasā) is one of the Gita's most beautiful images: the lotus grows in muddy water but its leaves repel every drop. Engagement without contamination. This is not withdrawal from the world (that would be the ascetic path Krishna has already rejected) but participation without attachment. The framework parallel: the Academy does not withdraw from power, politics, and institutions — it engages with them while maintaining its orientation toward wisdom.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
The world is overcome--aye! even here!
By such as fix their faith on Unity.
The sinless Brahma dwells in Unity,
And they in Brahma. Be not over-glad
Attaining joy, and be not over-sad
Encountering grief, but, stayed on Brahma, still
Constant let each abide! The sage whose soul
Holds off from outer contacts, in himself
Finds bliss; to Brahma joined by piety,
His spirit tastes eternal peace. The joys
Springing from sense-life are but quickening wombs
Which breed sure griefs: those joys begin and end!
The wise mind takes no pleasure, Kunti's Son!
In such as those! But if a man shall learn,
Even while he lives and bears his body's chain,
To master lust and anger, he is blest!
He is the Yukta; he hath happiness,
Contentment, light, within: his life is merged
In Brahma's life; he doth Nirvana touch!
Thus go the Rishis unto rest, who dwell
With sins effaced, with doubts at end, with hearts
Governed and calm. Glad in all good they live,
Nigh to the peace of God; and all those live
Who pass their days exempt from greed and wrath,
Subduing self and senses, knowing the Soul!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The world is overcome — even here, in this life — by those who fix their minds on unity. Brahman is without flaw and dwells in unity, and those who rest in Brahman share that quality.

Do not be elated by pleasure or depressed by pain. Remain steady. The sage whose soul is turned away from external contacts finds joy within. United with Brahman through discipline, such a spirit tastes eternal peace.

The pleasures born of sensory contact are wombs of suffering — they come and go, and the wise do not seek their shelter. The person who can withstand the force of desire and anger before leaving this body — that person is disciplined, that person is happy.
D: Elegant: Fix your mind on unity and the world is overcome — here, in this life. Pleasures born of the senses are wombs of suffering: they come and go. The one who withstands desire and anger before death — that one is free.
D: Distilled: Sensory pleasures are borrowed happiness — they must be returned with interest.
Notes: 'Pleasures born of contact are wombs of suffering' (ye hi saṁsparśajā bhogā duḥkha-yonaya eva te) — one of the Gita's sharpest formulations. Compare with the Sneaky Candy concept: short-term pleasure that produces long-term harm, with the harm not readily apparent. The Gita's framework for this predates the modern concept by millennia but describes the same mechanism.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
The Saint who shuts outside his placid soul
All touch of sense, letting no contact through;
Whose quiet eyes gaze straight from fixed brows,
Whose outward breath and inward breath are drawn
Equal and slow through nostrils still and close;
That one-with organs, heart, and mind constrained,
Bent on deliverance, having put away
Passion, and fear, and rage;--hath, even now,
Obtained deliverance, ever and ever freed.
Yea! for he knows Me Who am He that heeds
The sacrifice and worship, God revealed;
And He who heeds not, being Lord of Worlds,
Lover of all that lives, God unrevealed,
Wherein who will shall find surety and shield!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The sage who shuts out all external contact, fixing their gaze between the brows, equalizing the inward and outward breaths, with senses, mind, and intellect controlled, bent entirely on liberation, having put away desire, fear, and anger — that person is forever free.

Knowing me as the enjoyer of all sacrifices and austerities, the great lord of all worlds, the friend of all beings — knowing this, one attains peace.
D: Elegant: Master the senses, steady the breath, release desire and fear and anger — and know me as the friend of all beings. That knowledge is peace.
D: Distilled: Peace comes not from controlling the world but from knowing what the world rests upon.
Notes: The meditation posture described here (gaze fixed between brows, breath equalized) is a practical yogic technique (dhyana) still practiced today. 'Friend of all beings' (suhṛdaṁ sarva-bhūtānām) — Krishna's self-description as universal friend, not judge or ruler. This frames the divine relationship as philia (reciprocal care) rather than authority. Chapter 5's conclusion connects all three framework pillars: Reality (knowing what is), Reason (disciplined practice), Right (peace through correct understanding).

D: Original:
[Krishna]
HERE ENDS CHAPTER V. OF THE BHAGAVAD-GITA,
Entitled "Karmasanyasayog,"
Or "The Book of Religion by Renouncing Fruit of Works."
Notes: Chapter colophon. Karma Sannyasa Yoga — the Yoga of Renunciation of Action. The paradox resolved: true renunciation is not the cessation of action but the purification of motive. Act fully, without attachment. The lotus leaf principle.

D: Original: Chapter 6: The Yoga of Meditation (Dhyana Yoga)

[Krishna]
Therefore, who doeth work rightful to do,
Not seeking gain from work, that man, O Prince!
Is Sanyasi and Yogi--both in one
And he is neither who lights not the flame
Of sacrifice, nor setteth hand to task.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The true renouncer — the true yogi — is the one who does the work that duty requires, without seeking personal gain from it. Not the one who lights no fire of sacrifice, nor the one who does no work at all.
D: Elegant: The real yogi is not the one who stops working but the one who works without seeking gain. Renunciation without action is not renunciation — it is laziness.
D: Distilled: Renunciation is not the absence of work but the absence of self-interest in work.
Notes: Chapter 6 opens by redefining both 'sannyasi' (renouncer) and 'yogi' (practitioner) as the same thing: someone who acts from duty without attachment. This directly counters the common misunderstanding that spiritual practice requires withdrawal from worldly activity.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Regard as true Renouncer him that makes
Worship by work, for who renounceth not
Works not as Yogin. So is that well said:
"By works the votary doth rise to faith,
And saintship is the ceasing from all works;
Because the perfect Yogin acts--but acts
Unmoved by passions and unbound by deeds,
Setting result aside.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Regard the person who performs work as worship — that is the true renouncer. No one becomes a yogi without first doing the work. For the one who is climbing toward yoga, action is the means. For the one who has reached the summit, stillness is the means. The perfect yogi acts, but acts unmoved by passion and unbound by results.
D: Elegant: Action is the path up. Stillness is the summit. But the perfected yogi does both — acting without being moved, achieving without being bound.
D: Distilled: You climb by doing; you arrive by letting go of what the doing was for.
Notes: The mountain metaphor: action (karma) is the ascent, meditation (dhyana) is the peak. But the summit doesn't mean you stop — it means you act from a different place. This maps to the framework's progression: you start by doing (building institutions, writing, teaching), and the mature form is acting from settled wisdom rather than anxious effort.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Let each man raise
The Self by Soul, not trample down his Self,
Since Soul that is Self's friend may grow Self's foe.
Soul is Self's friend when Self doth rule o'er Self,
But Self turns enemy if Soul's own self
Hates Self as not itself.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Let each person raise themselves by their own effort — do not degrade yourself. The Self can be its own friend or its own enemy. It is your friend when you have mastered yourself, and your enemy when you have not.
D: Elegant: Raise yourself by your own effort. The Self is its own friend when mastered, its own enemy when not.
D: Distilled: You are the only person who can save you, and the only person who can destroy you.
Notes: 'The Self is Self's friend when Self rules over Self' (bandhur ātmātmanas tasya yenātmaivātmanā jitaḥ) — one of the Gita's most psychologically precise statements. The internal battle (mastering your own mind) is presented as more fundamental than the external battle (the war on Kurukshetra). This connects to the framework's emphasis on Reason as self-discipline: the capacity to think clearly requires first mastering the forces within that resist clarity.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
The sovereign soul
Of him who lives self-governed and at peace
Is centred in itself, taking alike
Pleasure and pain; heat, cold; glory and shame.
He is the Yogi, he is Yukta, glad
With joy of light and truth; dwelling apart
Upon a peak, with senses subjugate
Whereto the clod, the rock, the glistering gold
Show all as one. By this sign is he known
Being of equal grace to comrades, friends,
Chance-comers, strangers, lovers, enemies,
Aliens and kinsmen; loving all alike,
Evil or good.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The self-governed soul, at peace, centered in itself, taking pleasure and pain alike — heat and cold, honor and disgrace — such a person is the true yogi. Content with knowledge and direct experience, steady on the heights, senses subdued, seeing gold and clay as the same — such a person treats friends, enemies, strangers, allies, saints, and sinners with equal regard.
D: Elegant: The yogi sees gold and clay as the same. Friends and enemies receive equal regard. This is not indifference — it is the clarity of someone who has stopped being pushed around by preferences.
D: Distilled: Equanimity is not caring about nothing — it is caring about everything without being captured by anything.
Notes: The description of the yogi who sees 'gold and clay as one' (sama-loṣṭrāśma-kāñcanaḥ) can be misread as nihilism. It is actually about the cessation of reactive bias — seeing things as they are rather than as sources of personal gain or loss. This is the same capacity the framework describes as essential for Right: the ability to assess situations without being distorted by self-interest.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Sequestered should he sit,
Steadfastly meditating, solitary,
His thoughts controlled, his passions laid away,
Quit of belongings. In a fair, still spot
Having his fixed abode,--not too much raised,
Nor yet too low,--let him abide, his goods
A cloth, a deerskin, and the Kusa-grass.
There, setting hard his mind upon The One,
Restraining heart and senses, silent, calm,
Let him accomplish Yoga, and achieve
Pureness of soul, holding immovable
Body and neck and head, his gaze absorbed
Upon his nose-end, rapt from all around,
Tranquil in spirit, free of fear, intent
Upon his Brahmacharya vow, devout,
Musing on Me, lost in the thought of Me.
That Yojin, so devoted, so controlled,
Comes to the peace beyond,--My peace, the peace
Of high Nirvana!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The yogi should sit in solitude, meditating steadily, thoughts controlled, desires put away. In a clean, still place — neither too high nor too low — seated on cloth and grass, fixing the mind on one point, restraining heart and senses, still and calm. Holding body, neck, and head aligned, gazing at the tip of the nose, serene, fearless, firm in the vow of celibacy, the mind restrained, thinking of me, sitting devoted to me — the yogi attains the peace that culminates in nirvana, the peace that abides in me.
D: Elegant: Sit alone. Be still. Fix the mind on one point. Hold the body straight, the gaze steady, the senses quiet. Think of nothing but the highest — and the peace that follows is the peace that ends all seeking.
D: Distilled: Meditation is not escape from the world but the practice of becoming still enough to perceive what the world rests on.
Notes: This is the Gita's most detailed meditation instruction (dhyana). The physical posture described — spine straight, gaze fixed, breath controlled — is the foundation of classical yoga practice (ashtanga yoga). 'Nirvana' (nirvāṇa) appears in the Gita — predating its more famous use in Buddhism. Here it means extinction of suffering through union with Brahman, not annihilation.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
But for earthly needs
Religion is not his who too much fasts
Or too much feasts, nor his who sleeps away
An idle mind; nor his who wears to waste
His strength in vigils. Nay, Arjuna! call
That the true piety which most removes
Earth-aches and ills, where one is moderate
In eating and in resting, and in sport;
Measured in wish and act; sleeping betimes,
Waking betimes for duty.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
But yoga is not for the extremist. It is not for the one who fasts too much or feasts too much. Not for the one who sleeps too long or stays awake too long. True discipline is moderate — moderate in eating, resting, working, sleeping, and waking. This balanced practice is what removes suffering.
D: Elegant: Yoga is not for extremists. Not too much fasting, not too much feasting. Not too much sleep, not too much wakefulness. The path that removes suffering is the path of measure.
D: Distilled: The disciplined life is not asceticism — it is the art of balance.
Notes: This is the Gita's version of the Golden Mean (Aristotle's mesotes). Neither extreme asceticism nor indulgence — but measured, balanced living. The Buddha's 'Middle Way' (majjhima paṭipadā) teaches the same principle. The convergence across traditions is striking: Greek, Hindu, and Buddhist ethics all arrive at moderation as the foundation of wisdom. This connects directly to the framework's discussion of the Golden Mean as the correct position on any spectrum given current circumstances.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
When the man,
So living, centres on his soul the thought
Straitly restrained--untouched internally
By stress of sense--then is he Yukta. See!
Steadfast a lamp burns sheltered from the wind;
Such is the likeness of the Yogi's mind
Shut from sense-storms and burning bright to Heaven.
When mind broods placid, soothed with holy wont;
When Self contemplates self, and in itself
Hath comfort; when it knows the nameless joy
Beyond all scope of sense, revealed to soul--
Only to soul! and, knowing, wavers not,
True to the farther Truth; when, holding this,
It deems no other treasure comparable,
But, harboured there, cannot be stirred or shook
By any gravest grief, call that state "peace,"
That happy severance Yoga; call that man
The perfect Yogin!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
When the disciplined mind rests in the self, untouched by any desire — that is yoga. A lamp that does not flicker in a windless place — such is the image of the yogi's mind, absorbed in meditation on the self.

When thought becomes still, quieted by the practice of discipline; when the self beholds the self and is satisfied; when it knows the boundless joy that is beyond the senses, grasped only by the understanding; when, established there, it does not waver from the truth — then one has found what is worth finding, and having found it, counts no other gain as greater. Established in that state, even heavy sorrow cannot shake you.
D: Elegant: A lamp in a windless room — steady, unflickering — that is the yogi's mind. When thought becomes still and the self sees itself, it finds a joy beyond the senses that nothing can take away.
D: Distilled: The steadiest flame is the one no wind can reach — and the deepest joy is the one no loss can touch.
Notes: The lamp metaphor (yathā dīpo nivātastho neṅgate) is one of the Gita's most famous images for concentrated consciousness. 'Joy beyond the senses, grasped only by the understanding' (sukham ātyantikam yat tad buddhi-grāhyam atīndriyam) — this is the beauty concept from the framework: the sensation when truth or excellence lands, accessible only through disciplined understanding, not through sensory experience.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Steadfastly the will
Must toil thereto, till efforts end in ease,
And thought has passed from thinking. Shaking off
All longings bred by dreams of fame and gain,
Shutting the doorways of the senses close
With watchful ward; so, step by step, it comes
To gift of peace assured and heart assuaged,
When the mind dwells self-wrapped, and the soul broods
Cumberless. But, as often as the heart
Breaks--wild and wavering--from control, so oft
Let him re-curb it, let him rein it back
To the soul's governance; for perfect bliss
Grows only in the bosom tranquillised,
The spirit passionless, purged from offence,
Vowed to the Infinite. He who thus vows
His soul to the Supreme Soul, quitting sin,
Passes unhindered to the endless bliss
Of unity with Brahma. He so vowed,
So blended, sees the Life-Soul resident
In all things living, and all living things
In that Life-Soul contained. And whoso thus
Discerneth Me in all, and all in Me,
I never let him go; nor looseneth he
Hold upon Me; but, dwell he where he may,
Whate'er his life, in Me he dwells and lives,
Because he knows and worships Me, Who dwell
In all which lives, and cleaves to Me in all.
Arjuna! if a man sees everywhere--
Taught by his own similitude--one Life,
One Essence in the Evil and the Good,
Hold him a Yogi, yea! well-perfected!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The will must work at this steadily, step by step, until effort gives way to ease and thinking passes beyond thought. Let go of all desires bred by ambition and fantasy. Draw the senses inward with patient discipline. Gradually, peace comes — the mind rests in the self.

But as often as the mind breaks free — wild and wandering — bring it back. Again and again, return it to the self. The yogi whose mind is calm, whose passions are stilled, who is free from fault and identified with the infinite — such a person has reached the highest happiness.

The yogi who sees the self in all beings and all beings in the self — that one sees truly. Whoever sees me everywhere and sees everything in me — I am never lost to that person, and that person is never lost to me.

The yogi who worships me as dwelling in all beings, however that yogi lives, lives in me.
D: Elegant: The mind will break free — wild, wandering, restless. Bring it back. Again and again, return it to the center. The one who sees the self in all beings and all beings in the self — that one sees truly, and is never lost.
D: Distilled: The practice is not achieving stillness once but returning to it every time the mind escapes.
Notes: The instruction to bring the wandering mind back 'again and again' (yato yato niścarati manas cañcalam asthiram, tatas tato niyamyaitad) is strikingly practical and modern — it is essentially the instruction given in contemporary mindfulness meditation. The universalist vision ('see me in all beings') reaches toward what the framework calls Wisdom: the integration of understanding (seeing truly) with ethical orientation (treating all beings as manifestations of the same reality).

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Slayer of Madhu! yet again, this Yog,
This Peace, derived from equanimity,
Made known by thee--I see no fixity
Therein, no rest, because the heart of men
Is unfixed, Krishna! rash, tumultuous,
Wilful and strong. It were all one, I think,
To hold the wayward wind, as tame man's heart.
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
This yoga you describe — this peace through equanimity — I cannot see how it can last. The human mind is restless, turbulent, powerful, and stubborn. Trying to control it seems as hopeless as trying to hold the wind.
D: Elegant: The mind is restless, turbulent, and stubborn. Trying to control it is like trying to hold the wind.
D: Distilled: Arjuna names what every meditator discovers: the mind does not want to be still.
Notes: Arjuna's objection is honest and psychologically accurate: 'The mind is restless' (cañcalaṁ hi manaḥ). This is not a philosophical objection but an experiential one — he has tried what Krishna is describing and found it impossibly difficult. Krishna's answer (next verse) does not deny the difficulty but reframes it as solvable through persistent practice. This maps to the framework's Spirit component: the willingness to pursue wisdom despite the difficulty is itself the thing being developed.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Hero long-armed! beyond denial, hard
Man's heart is to restrain, and wavering;
Yet may it grow restrained by habit, Prince!
By wont of self-command. This Yog, I say,
Cometh not lightly to th' ungoverned ones;
But he who will be master of himself
Shall win it, if he stoutly strive thereto.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
You are right — the mind is extremely difficult to restrain. It is wild and restless. But it can be mastered through practice and detachment.

Yoga is hard to attain for the undisciplined. But whoever strives earnestly and has self-control can achieve it.
D: Elegant: The mind is wild — you are right. But it can be tamed by practice and detachment. This path is not for the undisciplined, but it is open to anyone willing to strive.
D: Distilled: The mind is wild, but it can be trained — by anyone willing to do the work.
Notes: Krishna's answer (abhyāsena tu kaunteya vairāgyeṇa ca gṛhyate) — 'by practice (abhyasa) and detachment (vairagya).' These two words are the entire method. Patanjali's Yoga Sutras (1.12) will later use exactly the same pair as the foundation of yoga practice. The framework parallel: building the capacity for wisdom requires both sustained effort (practice) and freedom from attachment to outcomes (detachment).

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
And what road goeth he who, having faith,
Fails, Krishna! in the striving; falling back
From holiness, missing the perfect rule?
Is he not lost, straying from Brahma's light,
Like the vain cloud, which floats 'twixt earth and heaven
When lightning splits it, and it vanisheth?
Fain would I hear thee answer me herein,
Since, Krishna! none save thou can clear the doubt.
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
But what about the person who has faith and tries sincerely — and still fails? Who starts the path but cannot complete it? Is that person lost — like a cloud that breaks apart between earth and heaven, belonging to neither? Please clear this doubt for me, Krishna. No one else can.
D: Elegant: What about the sincere person who tries and fails? Is all that effort lost — scattered like a cloud between earth and heaven?
D: Distilled: The deepest fear of the seeker: what if I try with everything I have and still fall short?
Notes: The cloud metaphor (vināśyaty ubhaya-vibhraṣṭaḥ chinnābhram iva naśyati) — torn apart, belonging to neither world. This is a universal fear: the person who leaves conventional life for spiritual pursuit but doesn't reach the goal. Am I worse off than if I'd never tried? This question matters for the framework too: what about the person who commits to building the Academy and fails? Is partial progress worth anything?

D: Original:
[Krishna]
He is not lost, thou Son of Pritha! No!
Nor earth, nor heaven is forfeit, even for him,
Because no heart that holds one right desire
Treadeth the road of loss! He who should fail,
Desiring righteousness, cometh at death
Unto the Region of the Just; dwells there
Measureless years, and being born anew,
Beginneth life again in some fair home
Amid the mild and happy. It may chance
He doth descend into a Yogin house
On Virtue's breast; but that is rare! Such birth
Is hard to be obtained on this earth, Chief!
So hath he back again what heights of heart
He did achieve, and so he strives anew
To perfectness, with better hope, dear Prince!
For by the old desire he is drawn on
Unwittingly; and only to desire
The purity of Yog is to pass
Beyond the Sabdabrahm, the spoken Ved.
But, being Yogi, striving strong and long,
Purged from transgressions, perfected by births
Following on births, he plants his feet at last
Upon the farther path. Such as one ranks
Above ascetics, higher than the wise,
Beyond achievers of vast deeds! Be thou
Yogi Arjuna! And of such believe,
Truest and best is he who worships Me
With inmost soul, stayed on My Mystery!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
No — that person is not lost. Not on earth and not beyond it. No one who does any good is ever destroyed.

The one who falls from the path of yoga is reborn in favorable circumstances — among the virtuous and prosperous, or even in a family of wise yogis (though that is rare). There, the wisdom gained in the previous life reawakens, and the person strives again from where they left off. Even the mere desire to know yoga carries a person beyond the scriptures.

The yogi who strives earnestly, purified over many lifetimes, eventually reaches the supreme goal. The yogi is greater than the ascetic, greater than the scholar, greater than the ritualist. Therefore be a yogi, Arjuna. And of all yogis, the one who worships me with faith and love — that one I hold dearest.
D: Elegant: No one who does good is ever lost. Even failed effort carries forward — into the next life, into the next attempt. The desire to know is itself progress. Strive earnestly, and eventually you arrive. Be a yogi — and of all yogis, the one who acts from love is the greatest.
D: Distilled: No sincere effort toward wisdom is wasted — not in this life and not across lifetimes.
Notes: Krishna's answer to the 'what if I fail' fear: spiritual progress is cumulative and cannot be lost. Even incomplete effort carries forward (the mechanism is reincarnation in the Hindu framework, but the principle applies more broadly: partial progress builds on itself). 'The yogi is greater than the ascetic, greater than the scholar, greater than the ritualist' (tapasvibhyo 'dhiko yogī jñānibhyo 'pi mato 'dhikaḥ) — practice integrated with understanding is higher than any single discipline. This is the framework's 'skill + spirit + worldview must be built together' stated as a ranking.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
HERE ENDETH CHAPTER VI. OF THE BHAGAVAD-GITA,
Entitled "Atmasanyamayog,"
Or "The Book of Religion by Self-Restraint."
Notes: Chapter colophon. Dhyana Yoga — the Yoga of Meditation. This chapter provided: practical meditation instruction, the Golden Mean of discipline (neither extreme asceticism nor indulgence), the mind-as-wind problem and its solution (practice + detachment), and the assurance that no sincere effort is wasted. The framework parallel: the Spirit component (willingness to pursue wisdom) is not a one-time commitment but an ongoing practice of returning the wandering mind to its purpose.

D: Original: Chapter 7: The Yoga of Knowledge and Realization (Jnana Vijnana Yoga)

[Krishna]
Learn now, dear Prince! how, if thy soul be set
Ever on Me--still exercising Yog,
Still making Me thy Refuge--thou shalt come
Most surely unto perfect hold of Me.
I will declare to thee that utmost lore,
Whole and particular, which, when thou knowest,
Leaveth no more to know here in this world.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Listen carefully, Arjuna. If you fix your mind on me and practice discipline, making me your refuge, I will show you the complete truth — the knowledge that, once grasped, leaves nothing more to know.
D: Elegant: Fix your mind on me. Practice. Take refuge. I will show you everything — and once you see it, there is nothing left to seek.
D: Distilled: The highest knowledge is not a collection of facts but a single understanding that makes all other questions dissolve.
Notes: Chapter 7 opens Krishna's deepest theological teaching. 'Leaves nothing more to know' (jnana + vijnana = theoretical + experiential knowledge). The Gita distinguishes between knowing about something (jnana) and knowing it directly (vijnana). The framework parallel: the difference between studying wisdom and possessing it.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Of many thousand mortals, one, perchance,
Striveth for Truth; and of those few that strive--
Nay, and rise high--one only--here and there--
Knoweth Me, as I am, the very Truth.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Out of thousands of people, perhaps one strives for truth. And of those rare few who strive and even rise high — only one, here and there, knows me as I actually am.
D: Elegant: Of thousands, one strives. Of the strivers, one arrives. The truth is not hidden — but almost no one looks.
D: Distilled: The rarity of wisdom is not because truth is hidden but because almost no one does the work to reach it.
Notes: The funnel of seekers: many called, few strive, fewer arrive. This maps to the framework's observation that wisdom has always been the province of a minority — not by gatekeeping but by the demands of the pursuit itself. The erosion problem is compounded: even among those who seek, most do not persist.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Earth, water, flame, air, ether, life, and mind,
And individuality--those eight
Make up the showing of Me, Manifest.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Earth, water, fire, air, ether, life-force, mind, and individuality — these eight elements are my lower nature, my manifest form.
D: Elegant: Eight elements compose my visible form: earth, water, fire, air, ether, life, mind, and the sense of self.
D: Distilled: The material world — including mind and ego — is the lower face of something higher.
Notes: The eight elements (ashtadha prakriti): the five gross elements (pancha mahabhuta: earth, water, fire, air, ether/space) plus three subtle elements (mind/manas, intellect/buddhi, ego/ahamkara). Note that mind and individuality are classified as material — part of prakriti, not spirit. This is a radical claim: what you think of as 'you' (your mind, your sense of self) belongs to nature, not to the soul. The framework's Reality pillar asks the same question: what is actually real versus what merely feels real?

D: Original:
[Krishna]
These be my lower Nature; learn the higher,
Whereby, thou Valiant One! this Universe
Is, by its principle of life, produced;
Whereby the worlds of visible things are born
As from a Yoni. Know! I am that womb:
I make and I unmake this Universe:
Than me there is no other Master, Prince!
No other Maker! All these hang on me
As hangs a row of pearls upon its string.
I am the fresh taste of the water; I
The silver of the moon, the gold o' the sun,
The word of worship in the Veds, the thrill
That passeth in the ether, and the strength
Of man's shed seed. I am the good sweet smell
Of the moistened earth, I am the fire's red light,
The vital air moving in all which moves,
The holiness of hallowed souls, the root
Undying, whence hath sprung whatever is;
The wisdom of the wise, the intellect
Of the informed, the greatness of the great.
The splendour of the splendid. Kunti's Son!
These am I, free from passion and desire;
Yet am I right desire in all who yearn,
Chief of the Bharatas! for all those moods,
Soothfast, or passionate, or ignorant,
Which Nature frames, deduce from me; but all
Are merged in me--not I in them! The world--
Deceived by those three qualities of being--
Wotteth not Me Who am outside them all,
Above them all, Eternal! Hard it is
To pierce that veil divine of various shows
Which hideth Me; yet they who worship Me
Pierce it and pass beyond.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
But beyond this lower nature, learn my higher one — the living principle by which the entire universe is produced and sustained. I am the womb of all creation. I make and unmake this universe. Nothing exists beyond me. Everything hangs on me as pearls hang on a string.

I am the taste in water, the light of sun and moon, the sacred syllable in the scriptures, the sound that moves through space, the vitality in human seed. I am the sweet smell of the earth, the brightness of fire, the life in all that lives, the discipline of the disciplined. I am the root of everything that exists — the wisdom of the wise, the brilliance of the brilliant, the splendor of the splendid.

I am desire itself — when desire is not contrary to righteousness.

All three qualities of nature — clarity, passion, and inertia — come from me, but I am not in them. They are in me. The whole world, deceived by these three qualities, does not recognize me — the one who stands beyond them, eternal and unchanging. My divine illusion is difficult to penetrate. But those who devote themselves to me pass through it.
D: Elegant: Everything hangs on me as pearls hang on a string. I am the taste in water, the light in the sun, the life in all that lives. The three qualities of nature come from me — but I am not in them. The world, deceived by clarity and passion and inertia, does not see the one who stands beyond all three. My illusion is hard to pierce — but those who worship me pass through.
D: Distilled: The divine is not one thing among many but the thread on which every bead of existence is strung.
Notes: The pearl-string metaphor (mayi sarvam idam protam sutre mani-gana iva) is one of the Gita's most important images: God is not a separate entity but the connecting principle that holds all of reality together. 'I am desire when not contrary to dharma' (dharmaviruddho bhutesu kamo 'smi) — desire itself is not the enemy; only desire that conflicts with right order. This is significant: the Gita does not advocate the elimination of desire but its alignment with dharma. The three gunas (sattva/clarity, rajas/passion, tamas/inertia) will be explored fully in Chapter 14. Maya (divine illusion) — the veil that makes the created world appear to be all there is. The framework parallel: what most people take for reality is a surface manifestation; the deeper structure requires effort to perceive.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
I am not known
To evil-doers, nor to foolish ones,
Nor to the base and churlish; nor to those
Whose mind is cheated by the show of things,
Nor those that take the way of Asuras.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
I am not known to evildoers, to the foolish, to the base, or to those whose minds are stolen by illusion. They follow the way of demons.
D: Elegant: The foolish, the base, and those whose minds are captured by appearances — none of these see me. They follow the demonic path without knowing it.
D: Distilled: The divine is invisible to minds occupied by lesser things.
Notes: Asuras (demons) represent not supernatural beings but a quality of consciousness — the orientation toward power, domination, and self-aggrandizement without reference to truth. The 'demonic' path in the Gita is a psychological category, not a mythological one. Those 'whose mind is cheated by the show of things' (maya-apahrita-jnana) describes anyone whose understanding is captured by surface appearances.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Four sorts of mortals know me: he who weeps,
Arjuna! and the man who yearns to know;
And he who toils to help; and he who sits
Certain of me, enlightened.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Four kinds of people turn to me: the one in distress, the one who seeks knowledge, the one who seeks achievement, and the one who has already found wisdom.
D: Elegant: Four seekers approach me: the suffering, the curious, the ambitious, and the wise. All four are valid starting points.
D: Distilled: People come to truth through pain, curiosity, ambition, or insight — and every door leads to the same room.
Notes: The four types of devotees (chatur-vidha bhajante mam): arta (distressed), jijnasu (knowledge-seeker), artharthi (wealth/achievement-seeker), jnani (wise). This taxonomy is psychologically useful: it recognizes that people begin the spiritual path for different reasons, and none of the starting motivations are disqualified. The framework parallel: people come to the pursuit of wisdom through different doors — suffering, intellectual curiosity, practical ambition, or direct insight — and the tradition must accommodate all of them.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Of these four,
O Prince of India! highest, nearest, best
That last is, the devout soul, wise, intent
Upon "The One." Dear, above all, am I
To him; and he is dearest unto me!
All four are good, and seek me; but mine own,
The true of heart, the faithful--stayed on me,
Taking me as their utmost blessedness,
They are not "mine,"but I--even I myself!
At end of many births to Me they come!
Yet hard the wise Mahatma is to find,
That man who sayeth, "All is Vasudev!"
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Of these four, the highest is the wise one — the person of steady devotion who sees everything as one. I am supremely dear to such a person, and that person is dear to me. All four are good seekers, but the wise one is my very self — for at the end of many lifetimes, the great soul arrives at the understanding that everything is God. Such a person is rare.
D: Elegant: All four seekers are worthy. But the wise one — who sees 'all is God' — is not merely close to me. That one is me. Such a soul is the rarest thing in the world.
D: Distilled: The rarest insight is also the simplest: everything is one thing, and that thing is sacred.
Notes: 'All is Vasudeva' (vasudevah sarvam iti sa mahatma su-durlabhah) — the culmination of Hindu monotheism: everything that exists is a manifestation of the divine. The 'many lifetimes' required suggests this is not a sudden insight but the product of sustained effort across time. Mahatma (great soul) — the term later used for Gandhi. The framework parallel: the highest form of wisdom is not mastery of specific domains but the integrated understanding that connects all knowledge into a unified whole.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
There be those, too, whose knowledge, turned aside
By this desire or that, gives them to serve
Some lower gods, with various rites, constrained
By that which mouldeth them. Unto all such--
Worship what shrine they will, what shapes, in faith--
'Tis I who give them faith! I am content!
The heart thus asking favour from its God,
Darkened but ardent, hath the end it craves,
The lesser blessing--but 'tis I who give!
Yet soon is withered what small fruit they reap:
Those men of little minds, who worship so,
Go where they worship, passing with their gods.
But Mine come unto me! Blind are the eyes
Which deem th' Unmanifested manifest,
Not comprehending Me in my true Self!
Imperishable, viewless, undeclared,
Hidden behind my magic veil of shows,
I am not seen by all; I am not known--
Unborn and changeless--to the idle world.
But I, Arjuna! know all things which were,
And all which are, and all which are to be,
Albeit not one among them knoweth Me!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
There are those whose desires pull them toward lesser gods — worshipping this form or that, constrained by their own nature. I grant them the faith they need for whatever path they choose, and they receive the results they seek. But those results are small and temporary: those who worship lesser gods go to lesser gods. Those who worship me come to me.

The blind mistake me for something that has merely taken form, not comprehending my true nature — imperishable, undeclared, hidden behind the veil of appearances. I am not visible to all. The unborn, the unchanging — I know everything that was, is, and will be. But no one among all these beings knows me.
D: Elegant: I grant faith to every seeker, whatever shrine they choose. But the rewards of lesser worship are lesser rewards — temporary, limited. Those who worship smaller gods arrive at smaller destinations. Those who seek me find the imperishable. I know all that was, is, and will be — yet almost no one knows me.
D: Distilled: You arrive at whatever you worship — so the question is not whether to worship but what is worthy of worship.
Notes: Krishna's radical inclusivism: he does not condemn the worship of other gods but places it in a hierarchy. All worship ultimately reaches him, but the results match the aim — small gods yield small rewards. 'You go where you worship' is the Gita's version of 'you become what you practice.' Maya (illusion/appearances) conceals Krishna's true nature — the unmanifest behind all manifestation. The framework parallel: pursuing the Fundamental Questions (unanswerable but generative) produces deeper results than pursuing proximate, answerable questions — even though the proximate ones feel more productive.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
By passion for the "pairs of opposites,"
By those twain snares of Like and Dislike, Prince!
All creatures live bewildered, save some few
Who, quit of sins, holy in act, informed,
Freed from the "opposites,"and fixed in faith,
Cleave unto Me.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
All beings are bewildered by the paired opposites — attraction and aversion, pleasure and pain. Only the few who are free from sin, firm in faith, and released from this pull, cleave to me.
D: Elegant: The paired opposites — pleasure and pain, attraction and aversion — bewilder every creature. Only those who have freed themselves from the pull can see clearly enough to find me.
D: Distilled: The obstacle to truth is not complexity but the constant pull of like and dislike.
Notes: Dvandva-moha (delusion by pairs of opposites) — the fundamental psychological mechanism that obscures wisdom. Every experience is immediately sorted into pleasant/unpleasant, wanted/unwanted, and this sorting prevents clear perception. The Stoics identified the same mechanism (prohairesis — the faculty of choice distorted by passions). The framework parallel: the fight against your own mind, where bias and preference distort judgment before reasoning even begins.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Who cleave, who seek in Me
Refuge from birth and death, those have the Truth!
Those know Me BRAHMA; know Me Soul of Souls,
The ADHYATMAN; know KARMA, my work;
Know I am ADHIBHUTA, Lord of Life,
And ADHIDAIVA, Lord of all the Gods,
And ADHIYAJNA, Lord of Sacrifice;
Worship Me well, with hearts of love and faith,
And find and hold me in the hour of death.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Those who take refuge in me and seek liberation from birth and death — they come to know Brahman, the soul, and the nature of action. They know me as the lord of beings, the lord of gods, and the lord of sacrifice. Even at the hour of death, they know me with minds fixed in discipline.
D: Elegant: Those who seek me find everything: Brahman, the soul, the nature of action, the lord of all worlds. Even at death, the disciplined mind holds me and does not let go.
D: Distilled: What you hold in your mind at the moment of death reveals what you held throughout your life.
Notes: This closing verse introduces the key terms that Chapter 8 will define: Brahman (absolute reality), Adhyatman (the soul), Karma (action), Adhibhuta (lord of beings), Adhidaiva (lord of gods), Adhiyajna (lord of sacrifice). The phrase 'even at the hour of death' (prayana-kale 'pi) sets up Chapter 8's teaching on death and dying — the idea that the content of consciousness at death determines what follows.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
HERE ENDETH CHAPTER VII. OF THE BHAGAVAD-GITA,
Entitled "Vijnanayog,"
Or "The Book of Religion by Discernment."
Notes: Chapter colophon. Vijnanayoga — the Yoga of Knowledge and Discernment. This chapter established Krishna's dual nature (lower/material and higher/spiritual), the pearl-string metaphor for divine unity, the four types of seekers, and the hierarchy of worship. The framework parallel: Reality has a visible surface (the eight elements, the manifest world) and an invisible structure (the connecting principle, Brahman). Understanding the surface is jnana; experiencing the structure is vijnana.

D: Original: Chapter 8: The Yoga of the Imperishable Brahman (Aksara Brahma Yoga)

[Arjuna]
Who is that BRAHMA? What that Soul of Souls,
The ADHYATMAN? What, Thou Best of All!
Thy work, the KARMA? Tell me what it is
Thou namest ADHIBHUTA? What again
Means ADHIDAIVA? Yea, and how it comes
Thou canst be ADHIYAJNA in thy flesh?
Slayer of Madhu! Further, make me know
How good men find thee in the hour of death?
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
What is Brahman? What is the soul? What is karma? What is the lord of beings, the lord of gods, the lord of sacrifice? And how can you be found at the hour of death?
D: Elegant: Arjuna asks: define everything. What is the absolute? The soul? Action? The lord of beings and gods? And how do the dying find you?
D: Distilled: The student asks the teacher to define the terms — the first step from awe to understanding.
Notes: Arjuna's question takes the theological terms from 7.70 and demands precise definitions. This is the student's proper role: not to accept terminology passively but to require clarity. The Gita models good pedagogy: abstract claims must be made specific before they can be acted upon.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
I BRAHMA am! the One Eternal GOD,
And ADHYATMAN is My Being's name,
The Soul of Souls! What goeth forth from Me,
Causing all life to live, is KARMA called:
And, Manifested in divided forms,
I am the ADHIBHUTA, Lord of Lives;
And ADHIDAIVA, Lord of all the Gods,
Because I am PURUSHA, who begets.
And ADHIYAJNA, Lord of Sacrifice,
I--speaking with thee in this body here--
Am, thou embodied one! (for all the shrines
Flame unto Me!) And, at the hour of death,
He that hath meditated Me alone,
In putting off his flesh, comes forth to Me,
Enters into My Being--doubt thou not!
But, if he meditated otherwise
At hour of death, in putting off the flesh,
He goes to what he looked for, Kunti's Son!
Because the Soul is fashioned to its like.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
I am Brahman — the one eternal God. My nature as the soul of all souls is Adhyatman. The creative force that brings beings into existence is karma. My manifestation in all living forms is Adhibhuta. My presence as the spirit behind the gods is Adhidaiva. And as the one who receives all sacrifice — speaking to you here in this body — I am Adhiyajna.

At the hour of death, whoever meditates on me alone, putting off the flesh, comes to me without doubt. But whoever thinks of something else at death goes to whatever they were thinking of — because the soul is shaped by what it dwells upon.
D: Elegant: I am Brahman. My nature as the soul is Adhyatman. My creative force is karma. I manifest in every living form and receive every sacrifice. At death, whoever thinks of me comes to me. Whoever thinks of something else goes to that instead — because the soul becomes what it contemplates.
D: Distilled: The soul is shaped by what it dwells upon — at the hour of death and at every other hour.
Notes: Krishna provides precise definitions for each term Arjuna asked about. The key teaching: 'the soul is fashioned to its like' (yam yam vapi smaran bhavam tyajaty ante kalevaram, tam tam evaiti) — you become what you habitually contemplate. This is not just a death-and-dying doctrine; it is a psychological principle. The framework parallel: what you pay attention to, you become. The Grandfather Principle operates here too: the culture you steep yourself in shapes the person you become.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Have Me, then, in thy heart always! and fight!
Thou too, when heart and mind are fixed on Me,
Shalt surely come to Me! All come who cleave
With never-wavering will of firmest faith,
Owning none other Gods: all come to Me,
The Uttermost, Purusha, Holiest!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Therefore keep me in your heart at all times — and fight. If your mind and heart are fixed on me, you will come to me without question. The one whose will never wavers, whose faith is firm, who looks to no other — that one reaches the highest.
D: Elegant: Hold me in your heart and fight. A mind that never wavers, fixed on the highest, arrives at the highest. It is that simple — and that difficult.
D: Distilled: Constant devotion is not a mood but a discipline — hold the aim steady and the arrival is certain.
Notes: The instruction to 'hold me in your heart and fight' (mam anusmara yudhya ca) encapsulates the entire Gita in five words: contemplation and action, simultaneously. This is the resolution of the knowledge-vs-action paradox: do both, at the same time. The framework parallel: the Academy does not withdraw from the world to contemplate — it contemplates while engaged.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Whoso hath known Me, Lord of sage and singer,
Ancient of days; of all the Three Worlds Stay,
Boundless,--but unto every atom Bringer
Of that which quickens it: whoso, I say,
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Whoever has known me — the lord of sages and singers, ancient of days, sustainer of all three worlds, boundless yet present in every atom —
Notes: Verses 8.75-8.78 form a single continuous passage in Arnold's translation, describing the qualities of the person who knows Krishna and the manner of their death. They are separated here to match the source indexing but should be read as one movement.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Hath known My form, which passeth mortal knowing;
Seen my effulgence--which no eye hath seen--
Than the sun's burning gold more brightly glowing,
Dispersing darkness,--unto him hath been
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
— whoever has known my form that surpasses mortal understanding, my radiance brighter than the sun's gold, dispersing all darkness —
Notes: Continuation of 8.75. The imagery of light surpassing the sun recurs in Chapter 11's cosmic vision.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Right life! And, in the hour when life is ending,
With mind set fast and trustful piety,
Drawing still breath beneath calm brows unbending,
In happy peace that faithful one doth die,--
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
— that person, having lived rightly, at the hour of death draws the breath inward with calm concentration, steady beneath unbending brows, in trustful peace —
Notes: Continuation. The description of dying well: calm, concentrated, steady. The Gita treats death not as an event that happens to you but as a final act of discipline.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
In glad peace passeth to Purusha's heaven.
The place which they who read the Vedas name
AKSHARAM, "Ultimate;" whereto have striven
Saints and ascetics--their road is the same.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
— and passes in glad peace to the supreme, the place that the Vedas call the Imperishable, the goal that saints and ascetics strive to reach.

[Combined 8.75-8.78]: Whoever has known me — ancient, boundless, yet present in every atom, radiant beyond the sun — that person, having lived rightly, dies with calm concentration, breath drawn inward, brows steady, mind fixed in trustful peace, and passes to the Imperishable: the destination that saints and ascetics have always sought.
D: Elegant: Whoever has truly known me — boundless yet intimate, brighter than the sun — dies calmly, breath drawn inward, mind fixed, and passes to the place the Vedas call Imperishable.
D: Distilled: The person who has lived in truth dies in peace — and death is not an end but an arrival.
Notes: The combined passage (8.75-8.78) describes the ars moriendi — the art of dying well. The physical technique (drawing breath inward, fixing the mind) is yogic, but the principle is universal: the quality of your death reflects the quality of your life. Akshara (imperishable) — the destination beyond the cycle of birth and death. Compare with Socrates' calm acceptance of death in the Phaedo: the philosopher who has lived examining reality does not fear its transition.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
That way--the highest way--goes he who shuts
The gates of all his senses, locks desire
Safe in his heart, centres the vital airs
Upon his parting thought, steadfastly set;
And, murmuring OM, the sacred syllable--
Emblem of BRAHM--dies, meditating Me.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The highest path is this: shut the gates of the senses, lock desire within the heart, center the vital breath, fix the mind steadily — and, uttering OM, the sacred syllable that is the emblem of Brahman, die meditating on me.
D: Elegant: Close the senses, still the breath, center the mind, speak OM, and die meditating on the highest. That is the way.
D: Distilled: The art of dying is the final practice of the art of living: concentration, surrender, release.
Notes: OM (pranava) — the sacred syllable considered the sound-form of Brahman itself. In Mandukya Upanishad, OM's three phonemes (A-U-M) represent waking, dreaming, and deep sleep states, with the silence after representing the transcendent fourth state (turiya). The instruction to die while uttering OM is both literal yogic technique and metaphor: align your last breath with the ultimate reality.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
For who, none other Gods regarding, looks
Ever to Me, easily am I gained
By such a Yogi; and, attaining Me,
They fall not--those Mahatmas--back to birth,
To life, which is the place of pain, which ends,
But take the way of utmost blessedness.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The one who looks to me alone, with undivided attention — that yogi reaches me easily. And having reached me, such great souls are not reborn into this place of pain and impermanence. They take the way of highest blessing.
D: Elegant: Undivided attention, directed at the highest — that is the whole method. Those who reach me do not return to a world of suffering.
D: Distilled: The reward of reaching the highest is that you never again have to settle for less.
Notes: Anityam asukham lokam — 'this impermanent, unhappy world.' The Gita does not romanticize earthly existence; it calls it what it is: temporary and painful. Liberation (moksha) means exit from the cycle of rebirth (samsara). The framework parallel: the pursuit of wisdom is not optional comfort but necessary escape from the default condition of confusion and suffering.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
The worlds, Arjuna!--even Brahma's world--
Roll back again from Death to Life's unrest;
But they, O Kunti's Son! that reach to Me,
Taste birth no more. If ye know Brahma's Day
Which is a thousand Yugas; if ye know
The thousand Yugas making Brahma's Night,
Then know ye Day and Night as He doth know!
When that vast Dawn doth break, th' Invisible
Is brought anew into the Visible;
When that deep Night doth darken, all which is
Fades back again to Him Who sent it forth;
Yea! this vast company of living things--
Again and yet again produced--expires
At Brahma's Nightfall; and, at Brahma's Dawn,
Riseth, without its will, to life new-born.
But--higher, deeper, innermost--abides
Another Life, not like the life of sense,
Escaping sight, unchanging. This endures
When all created things have passed away:
This is that Life named the Unmanifest,
The Infinite! the All! the Uttermost.
Thither arriving none return. That Life
Is Mine, and I am there! And, Prince! by faith
Which wanders not, there is a way to come
Thither. I, the PURUSHA, I Who spread
The Universe around me--in Whom dwell
All living Things--may so be reached and seen!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
All the worlds — even Brahma's heaven — are subject to the cycle of creation and dissolution. But those who reach me are freed from rebirth.

If you understand the scale: one day of Brahma lasts a thousand yugas. One night of Brahma lasts a thousand yugas. When his day dawns, the unmanifest becomes manifest — all beings pour forth into existence. When his night falls, they dissolve back into the unmanifest. This vast company of living things is produced and destroyed again and again, without choice, at the rhythm of cosmic day and night.

But beyond this — higher, deeper, innermost — there exists another reality. Not like the life of sense. Unchanging. When all created things pass away, this endures. This is the Unmanifest, the Infinite, the Uttermost. Those who arrive there do not return.

That life is mine, and I am there. By unwavering faith, it can be reached. I, who spread the universe around me, in whom all living things dwell — I can be seen.
D: Elegant: All worlds cycle — even heaven. Brahma's day is a thousand ages; his night, a thousand more. All beings pour forth at dawn and dissolve at nightfall, endlessly. But beyond this cycle — higher, deeper, innermost — there is a reality that does not change. Those who reach it never return.
D: Distilled: Beyond the cycle of creation and destruction, there is something that does not cycle — and reaching it is the point of everything.
Notes: Brahma's day and night (kalpa): one day of Brahma = 4.32 billion human years (1,000 maha-yugas). The scale is designed to dwarf human conceptions of permanence — even the gods exist within cycles. The 'higher, deeper, innermost' reality (avyakto 'vyaktat sanatanah) is beyond all cycles, beyond all manifestation. This is the Gita's version of the framework's distinction between contingent truth (things that are true within a context) and fundamental truth (what remains when all contexts are stripped away). The Fundamental Questions point toward this deeper layer.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
.    .    .    .    .    .    .    .    .    .    .    .    .    .

Richer than holy fruit on Vedas growing,
Greater than gifts, better than prayer or fast,
Such wisdom is! The Yogi, this way knowing,
Comes to the Utmost Perfect Peace at last.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
This wisdom surpasses all scriptural study, all charitable giving, all ascetic practice, all prayer. The yogi who knows this reaches the ultimate, perfect peace.
D: Elegant: Greater than scripture, charity, austerity, or prayer — this knowing. The yogi who possesses it arrives at the end of all seeking.
D: Distilled: Direct knowledge of reality outweighs every form of religious practice combined.
Notes: The superiority of direct knowledge (vijnana) over ritual and scriptural practice (karma-kanda). This parallels the framework's hierarchy: logos (understanding why) is higher than mythos (following the forms), even though both are necessary. The Gita does not dismiss ritual — it ranks it below understanding.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
HERE ENDETH CHAPTER VIII. OF THE  BHAGAVAD-GITA,
Entitled  "Aksharaparabrahmayog,"
Or "The book of Religion by Devotion to the One Supreme God."
Notes: Chapter colophon. Aksharaparabrahmayoga — the Yoga of the Imperishable Supreme. This chapter taught: the soul becomes what it contemplates, the art of dying well, the cosmic scale of creation and destruction, and the existence of a reality beyond all cycles. The framework parallel: the Fundamental Questions point beyond the contingent world toward what is permanent — and the value of pursuing them lies in the orientation they produce, not in arriving at final answers.

D: Original: Chapter 9: The Yoga of Royal Knowledge (Raja Vidya Raja Guhya Yoga)

[Krishna]
Now will I open unto thee--whose heart
Rejects not--that last lore, deepest-concealed,
That farthest secret of My Heavens and Earths,
Which but to know shall set thee free from ills,--
A royal lore! a Kingly mystery!
Yea! for the soul such light as purgeth it
From every sin; a light of holiness
With inmost splendour shining; plain to see;
Easy to walk by, inexhaustible!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Now I will reveal to you the deepest secret — the supreme knowledge, the kingly mystery. To know it is to be purified of all wrongdoing. It is a light of holiness, radiant from within. It is easy to practice, plain to see, and inexhaustible.
D: Elegant: The deepest secret is also the simplest. To know it purifies everything. It is radiant, inexhaustible, and plain — for those willing to see.
D: Distilled: The highest knowledge is not obscure but overlooked — it hides in plain sight, waiting for attention.
Notes: Rajavidya (royal knowledge) and rajaguhya (royal secret) — 'royal' not because it belongs to kings but because it is sovereign: the knowledge that governs all other knowledge. The Gita calls it pratyakshavagamam (directly perceivable) and susukham kartum (easy to practice) — a striking claim that the highest truth is accessible, not esoteric. The framework parallel: wisdom is not hidden behind credentials or institutions. It is available to anyone willing to attend to the Fundamental Questions.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
They that receive not this, failing in faith
To grasp the greater wisdom, reach not Me,
Destroyer of thy foes! They sink anew
Into the realm of Flesh, where all things change!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Those who lack faith in this teaching do not reach me. They fall back into the cycle of death and rebirth, the realm where everything changes.
D: Elegant: Without faith in this, there is no arrival — only the endless cycle of change and death.
D: Distilled: The obstacle to liberation is not ignorance but the refusal to trust what you have already begun to see.
Notes: Ashraddadhanah — lacking shraddha (faith/trust). In the Gita, faith is not blind belief but willingness to act on understanding before it is complete. The person who demands certainty before commitment never begins. The framework parallel: the pursuit of wisdom requires committing to the process before you can see the destination.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
By Me the whole vast Universe of things
Is spread abroad;--by Me, the Unmanifest!
In Me are all existences contained;
Not I in them!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The entire universe is spread out by me — by the unmanifest. All things exist within me. I am not contained in them.
D: Elegant: I spread the universe. All things exist in me — yet I am not contained by any of them.
D: Distilled: The source contains everything it produces but is not limited to what it has produced.
Notes: The asymmetry: all things are in God, but God is not in all things — God exceeds the creation. This is the distinction between pantheism (God = nature) and panentheism (God includes but transcends nature). The Gita is panentheistic: the world is real and divine, but the divine exceeds the world. The framework parallel: the Fundamental Questions generate all specific inquiries but are not reducible to any of them.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Yet they are not contained,
Those visible things! Receive and strive to embrace
The mystery majestical! My Being--
Creating all, sustaining all--still dwells
Outside of all!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
And yet — they are not truly contained in me either. Behold this mystery: my being creates and sustains all things, yet dwells outside of all.
D: Elegant: My being creates and sustains all things — yet dwells outside of all. This is the mystery: the source is not captured by what it sources.
D: Distilled: The deepest paradox: what creates everything is not trapped within anything.
Notes: Krishna introduces a paradox he will not resolve: all things are in me, yet they are not in me, yet I sustain them. This is apophatic theology — describing the divine by what it is not, because positive descriptions always fall short. The framework parallel: the Fundamental Questions are productive precisely because they cannot be answered. The attempt to contain truth in a final statement is the error.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
See! as the shoreless airs
Move in the measureless space, but are not space,
[And space were space without the moving airs];
So all things are in Me, but are not I.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
As the wind moves through limitless space — dwelling in it but not being it — so all things move through me. They are in me but are not me.
D: Elegant: As wind moves through space — within it, part of it, but not the space itself — so all things move through me.
D: Distilled: Everything exists within the infinite the way wind exists within space: present, moving, but never the same thing as the space that holds it.
Notes: The wind-in-space metaphor (yathakaso sthito nityam vayuh sarvatra-go mahan) — wind cannot exist without space, but space exists without wind. The relationship is asymmetric dependence. This is one of the Gita's clearest illustrations of the Creator-creation relationship: creation depends on the source, but the source does not depend on creation.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
At closing of each Kalpa, Indian Prince!
All things which be back to My Being come:
At the beginning of each Kalpa, all
Issue new-born from Me.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
At the end of each cosmic cycle, all beings return to my unmanifest nature. At the beginning of the next cycle, they pour forth again from me.
D: Elegant: At each cycle's end, all beings dissolve back into me. At each new dawn, they are born again from me.
D: Distilled: Creation is not a single event but an eternal rhythm — dissolution and emergence, forever.
Notes: Kalpa — a cosmic cycle (one day of Brahma). The Gita's cosmology is cyclical, not linear. There is no single creation event and no final apocalypse — only an endless rhythm of manifestation and dissolution. This contrasts with the Abrahamic linear model (creation, history, judgment). The framework's erosion pattern is a small-scale version of the same insight: wisdom is not built once and preserved but must be rebuilt in every cycle.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
By Energy
And help of Prakriti my outer Self,
Again, and yet again, I make go forth
The realms of visible things--without their will--
All of them--by the power of Prakriti.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
By the power of my creative energy — Prakriti — I send forth these realms of beings again and again, without their choosing. They are brought into existence by the force of nature itself.
D: Elegant: Through Prakriti — my creative energy — I send forth all beings again and again, without their consent. Nature compels what nature creates.
D: Distilled: Existence is not chosen by the one who exists — it is compelled by the force that creates.
Notes: Prakriti (nature/material energy) as the mechanism of creation. Beings are created 'without their will' (avasham) — existence is not voluntary. This has implications for the framework: you did not choose to be born into a world where wisdom has eroded. But you can choose how to respond. The involuntary nature of birth makes the voluntary pursuit of wisdom all the more significant.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Yet these great makings, Prince! involve Me not
Enchain Me not! I sit apart from them,
Other, and Higher, and Free; nowise attached!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Yet none of this binds me. I sit apart — other, higher, free. The creation does not enchain the creator.
D: Elegant: I create — yet creation does not bind me. I sit apart, higher and free.
D: Distilled: The creator is not bound by what is created.
Notes: Udasinavad asinam (sitting as if indifferent) — Krishna's non-attachment to creation is not indifference but sovereignty. He is engaged without being controlled. This models the ideal the Gita teaches to humans: act fully but without attachment. The framework parallel: the wise person participates in the world without being enslaved by outcomes.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Thus doth the stuff of worlds, moulded by Me,
Bring forth all that which is, moving or still,
Living or lifeless! Thus the worlds go on!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Through this process, nature brings forth everything — moving and still, living and lifeless. This is how the worlds go on.
D: Elegant: Nature, under my direction, brings forth everything that moves and everything that is still. Thus the worlds continue.
D: Distilled: The visible world is the product of an invisible process — nature acting under direction.
Notes: Prakriti as the executive mechanism of divine will. The Gita's model is not deism (God creates and withdraws) but active sustenance — God directs nature continuously. The distinction between the director (purusha/spirit) and the mechanism (prakriti/nature) will be fully explored in Chapter 13.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
The minds untaught mistake Me, veiled in form;--
Naught see they of My secret Presence, nought
Of My hid Nature, ruling all which lives.
Vain hopes pursuing, vain deeds doing; fed
On vainest knowledge, senselessly they seek
An evil way, the way of brutes and fiends.
But My Mahatmas, those of noble soul
Who tread the path celestial, worship Me
With hearts unwandering,--knowing Me the Source,
Th' Eternal Source, of Life. Unendingly
They glorify Me; seek Me; keep their vows
Of reverence and love, with changeless faith
Adoring Me. Yea, and those too adore,
Who, offering sacrifice of wakened hearts,
Have sense of one pervading Spirit's stress,
One Force in every place, though manifold!
I am the Sacrifice! I am the Prayer!
I am the Funeral-Cake set for the dead!
I am the healing herb! I am the ghee,
The Mantra, and the flame, and that which burns!
I am-of all this boundless Universe-
The Father, Mother, Ancestor, and Guard!
The end of Learning! That which purifies
In lustral water! I am OM! I am
Rig-Veda, Sama-Veda, Yajur-Ved;
The Way, the Fosterer, the Lord, the Judge,
The Witness; the Abode, the Refuge-House,
The Friend, the Fountain and the Sea of Life
Which sends, and swallows up; Treasure of Worlds
And Treasure-Chamber! Seed and Seed-Sower,
Whence endless harvests spring! Sun's heat is mine;
Heaven's rain is mine to grant or to withhold;
Death am I, and Immortal Life I am,
Arjuna! SAT and ASAT, Visible Life,
And Life Invisible!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The foolish mistake me for something ordinary — they see the human form and miss the divine reality behind it. Pursuing vain hopes, vain deeds, and vain knowledge, they follow the way of delusion.

But the great souls who walk the divine path worship me with unwavering hearts, knowing me as the eternal source of all. They glorify me endlessly, strive toward me, keep their vows of devotion with changeless faith. And those who offer the sacrifice of knowledge perceive one pervading spirit, one force manifesting in every place.

I am the sacrifice. I am the prayer. I am the offering to the dead. I am the healing herb, the ghee, the mantra, the flame, and what the flame consumes. I am the father, mother, ancestor, and guardian of this universe. I am the goal of knowledge, the purifier, the sacred syllable OM. I am the three Vedas. I am the way, the sustainer, the lord, the witness, the dwelling-place, the refuge, the friend, the origin, the dissolution, the foundation, the seed, and the imperishable store. I am the sun's heat. I hold back the rain and release it. I am death and immortal life. I am being and non-being, the visible and the invisible.
D: Elegant: The foolish see my human form and miss what stands behind it. The great souls see through — and worship with changeless faith, knowing me as the source of everything. I am the sacrifice, the prayer, the flame, and what the flame consumes. Father and mother and ancestor. The way, the witness, the refuge, the friend. Death and immortal life. Being and non-being. Everything.
D: Distilled: The divine is not one role but every role — creator, sustainer, destroyer, witness, refuge, and friend, simultaneously.
Notes: This is one of the Gita's most expansive self-declarations. Krishna claims identity with every aspect of reality — ritual, natural, cosmic, familial. The list format is deliberate: it overwhelms the listener's categories. 'I am SAT and ASAT' (being and non-being) — the divine encompasses both existence and non-existence, transcending the most fundamental duality. The Gita's framework is more comprehensive than any single philosophical category. The framework parallel: the Fundamental Questions ('What is real? How do we know? What should we do?') are themselves manifestations of a single underlying orientation — the pursuit of wisdom.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Yea! those who learn
The threefold Veds, who drink the Soma-wine,
Purge sins, pay sacrifice--from Me they earn
Passage to Swarga; where the meats divine
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Those who study the three Vedas, who drink the sacred soma, who purge their sins through ritual sacrifice — they earn passage to heaven, where they feast with the gods —
Notes: Verses 9.94-9.96 form a single argument about the limitations of ritual worship. Soma: a sacred plant-based drink used in Vedic rituals, believed to confer divine vision. Swarga: heaven/paradise in Hindu cosmology.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Of great gods feed them in high Indra's heaven.
Yet they, when that prodigious joy is o'er,
Paradise spent, and wage for merits given,
Come to the world of death and change once more.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
— yet when that enormous joy is spent and the merit is used up, they return to the world of death and change.

[Combined 9.94-9.95]: Those who follow the Vedic rites, who purify themselves through sacrifice, earn passage to heaven and feast with the gods. But when the merit is exhausted, they return to the mortal world.
D: Elegant: Ritual earns heaven — but heaven has an expiration date. When the merit runs out, you fall back to earth.
D: Distilled: Even paradise is temporary if it is earned rather than understood.
Notes: The Gita's critique of ritual religion (karma-kanda): heaven (Swarga/Indra's paradise) is real but impermanent. It is earned through merit (punya) and expires when the merit is exhausted. This is a devastating critique of transactional religion — the idea that you can 'earn' a permanent reward through temporary effort. The framework parallel: institutional religion that focuses on ritual compliance rather than genuine understanding produces temporary results at best.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
They had their recompense! they stored their treasure,
Following the threefold Scripture and its writ;
Who seeketh such gaineth the fleeting pleasure
Of joy which comes and goes! I grant them it!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
They received their reward, stored their treasure, followed the scriptures — and what they gained was the fleeting pleasure of joy that comes and goes. I give them what they seek. But it does not last.
D: Elegant: They sought pleasure and got it. I gave them what they asked for. But what comes and goes is not what they needed.
D: Distilled: Getting what you want is not the same as getting what you need — and the divine gives you whichever one you ask for.
Notes: Alpa-medhasam (of small intellect) — Krishna does not condemn these seekers but identifies their limitation: they asked for too little. The divine grants what is requested, but the quality of the request determines the quality of the result. The framework parallel: the difference between pursuing proximate goods (wealth, status, comfort) and pursuing wisdom — both are available, but one is permanent and the other is not.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
But to those blessed ones who worship Me,
Turning not otherwhere, with minds set fast,
I bring assurance of full bliss beyond.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
But those who worship me with undivided hearts, turning nowhere else, with minds fixed — to them I bring the assurance of full and lasting peace.
D: Elegant: To those who turn to me with undivided hearts, I bring what ritual cannot: a peace that does not expire.
D: Distilled: Undivided attention produces undivided peace — the reward matches the quality of the seeking.
