Notes: Ananya-cetah (undivided mind) — single-pointed devotion. Yoga-kshemam vahamy aham (I carry what they need and protect what they have) — Krishna's promise to those who surrender fully: he assumes responsibility for their welfare. This is the bhakti (devotion) path stated in its purest form.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Nay, and of hearts which follow other gods
In simple faith, their prayers arise to me,
O Kunti's Son! though they pray wrongfully;
For I am the Receiver and the Lord
Of every sacrifice, which these know not
Rightfully; so they fall to earth again!
Who follow gods go to their gods; who vow
Their souls to Pitris go to Pitris; minds
To evil Bhuts given o'er sink to the Bhuts;
And whoso loveth Me cometh to Me.
Whoso shall offer Me in faith and love
A leaf, a flower, a fruit, water poured forth,
That offering I accept, lovingly made
With pious will. Whate'er thou doest, Prince!
Eating or sacrificing, giving gifts,
Praying or fasting, let it all be done
For Me, as Mine. So shalt thou free thyself
From Karmabandh, the chain which holdeth men
To good and evil issue, so shalt come
Safe unto Me-when thou art quit of flesh--
By faith and abdication joined to Me!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Even those who worship other gods with sincere faith — their prayers reach me too, though they do not know the proper way. For I am the receiver and the lord of every sacrifice, though they do not recognize this.

Those who worship gods go to gods. Those who worship ancestors go to ancestors. Those who worship spirits go to spirits. And those who worship me come to me.

Whoever offers me even a leaf, a flower, a fruit, or water — if offered with love and genuine devotion — I accept it. Whatever you do, whatever you eat, whatever you sacrifice, whatever you give, whatever austerity you practice — do it as an offering to me. That way you will be freed from the chain of karma — the bondage to good and bad consequences — and, liberated, you will come to me.
D: Elegant: Even a leaf, a flower, water — offered with love, I accept. Whatever you do, do it as an offering. Those who worship gods reach gods; those who worship ancestors reach ancestors. But those who offer everything to me — they reach me and are freed from every chain.
D: Distilled: The offering does not need to be grand — a leaf, a flower, water. What matters is not the gift but the love behind it.
Notes: Patram pushpam phalam toyam (a leaf, a flower, a fruit, water) — one of the Gita's most democratic teachings: no expensive ritual is required. Any offering made with genuine devotion is accepted. This dismantles the priestly monopoly on worship. 'You go where you worship' (yanti deva-vrata devan, pitrin yanti pitr-vratah) — the destination matches the orientation. Karmabandha (bondage of action) — the chain of consequences that binds the soul to the cycle of rebirth. The framework parallel: the Academy is not for elites — wisdom is accessible to anyone willing to offer genuine attention, regardless of resources or credentials.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
I am alike for all! I know not hate,
I know not favour! What is made is Mine!
But them that worship Me with love, I love;
They are in Me, and I in them!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
I am equal to all beings. I do not hate anyone and I do not favor anyone. But those who worship me with love — they are in me, and I am in them.
D: Elegant: I play no favorites. I know no hate. But those who love me live in me, and I in them — not by my preference but by theirs.
D: Distilled: The divine does not discriminate — but it responds to those who turn toward it.
Notes: Samo 'ham sarva-bhutesu (I am equal to all beings) — divine impartiality. Krishna does not choose favorites; the relationship is determined by the devotee's orientation. This is like gravity: the force is universal, but only objects that move within its field experience its pull. The framework parallel: wisdom is equally available but not equally pursued. The inequality is in the seeker, not the sought.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Nay, Prince!
If one of evil life turn in his thought
Straightly to Me, count him amidst the good;
He hath the high way chosen; he shall grow
Righteous ere long; he shall attain that peace
Which changes not. Thou Prince of India!
Be certain none can perish, trusting Me!
O Pritha's Son! whoso will turn to Me,
Though they be born from the very womb of Sin,
Woman or man; sprung of the Vaisya caste
Or lowly disregarded Sudra,--all
Plant foot upon the highest path; how then
The holy Brahmans and My Royal Saints?
Ah! ye who into this ill world are come--
Fleeting and false--set your faith fast on Me!
Fix heart and thought on Me! Adore Me! Bring
Offerings to Me! Make Me prostrations! Make
Me your supremest joy! and, undivided,
Unto My rest your spirits shall be guided.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Even a person of the most wicked life, if they turn to me with wholehearted devotion — count them among the good. They have chosen the right path and will quickly become righteous. They will find lasting peace.

Be certain of this, Arjuna: no one who trusts in me is ever destroyed.

Anyone who takes refuge in me — regardless of birth, caste, gender, or station — sets foot on the highest path. How much more, then, the holy and the royal? You have come into this fleeting, sorrowful world. Fix your faith on me. Set your heart and mind on me. Worship me. Make me your supreme joy. Surrender yourself to me entirely — and you will come to me.
D: Elegant: Even the worst sinner, turning to me with a whole heart, is counted among the good — and swiftly becomes righteous. No one who trusts in me is destroyed. Woman or man, high-born or low — anyone who takes refuge in me walks the highest path. Set your heart on me. Surrender everything. Come.
D: Distilled: The door to the divine does not check your past — it only asks whether you are willing to walk through it now.
Notes: The most radically inclusive passage in the Gita. Krishna explicitly names women, Vaishyas (merchants), and Shudras (laborers) as equally eligible for the highest path — a revolutionary statement in the context of Brahmanical Hinduism's caste hierarchy. 'No one who trusts in me is ever destroyed' (na me bhaktah pranasyati) — an unconditional guarantee. The framework parallel: the pursuit of wisdom is not gated by birth, credentials, or past failures. It requires only genuine commitment. This is the anti-elitist foundation of Paideia.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
HERE ENDS CHAPTER IX. OF THE BHAGAVAD-GITA,
Entitled "Rajavidyarajaguhyayog,"
Or "The Book of Religion by the Kingly Knowledge and the Kingly
Mystery."
Notes: Chapter colophon. Rajavidyarajaguhyayoga — the Yoga of Royal Knowledge and Royal Mystery. This chapter delivered the Gita's most inclusive and devotional teaching: the divine is equally available to all, regardless of caste, gender, or past sins. A leaf offered with love is worth more than elaborate ritual. You arrive at what you worship — so worship the highest. The framework parallel: wisdom is sovereign knowledge (rajavidya) not because it belongs to rulers but because it rules over all other forms of knowledge.

D: Original: Chapter 10: The Yoga of Divine Glories (Vibhuti Yoga)

Krishna.
Hear farther yet, thou Long-Armed Lord! these latest words I say--
Uttered to bring thee bliss and peace, who lovest Me alway--
Not the great company of gods nor kingly Rishis know
My Nature, Who have made the gods and Rishis long ago;
He only knoweth-only he is free of sin, and wise,
Who seeth Me, Lord of the Worlds, with faith-enlightened eyes,
Unborn, undying, unbegun. Whatever Natures be
To mortal men distributed, those natures spring from Me!
Intellect, skill, enlightenment, endurance, self-control,
Truthfulness, equability, and grief or joy of soul,
And birth and death, and fearfulness, and fearlessness, and shame,
And honour, and sweet harmlessness, and peace which is the
same
Whate'er befalls, and mirth, and tears, and piety, and thrift,
And wish to give, and will to help,--all cometh of My gift!
The Seven Chief Saints, the Elders Four, the Lordly Manus set--
Sharing My work--to rule the worlds, these too did I beget;
And Rishis, Pitris, Manus, all, by one thought of My mind;
Thence did arise, to fill this world, the races of mankind;
Wherefrom who comprehends My Reign of mystic Majesty--
That truth of truths--is thenceforth linked in faultless faith to Me:
Yea! knowing Me the source of all, by Me all creatures wrought,
The wise in spirit cleave to Me, into My Being brought;
Hearts fixed on Me; breaths breathed to Me; praising Me, each to each,
So have they happiness and peace, with pious thought and speech;
And unto these--thus serving well, thus loving ceaselessly--
I give a mind of perfect mood, whereby they draw to Me;
And, all for love of them, within their darkened souls I dwell,
And, with bright rays of wisdom's lamp, their ignorance dispel.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Hear me again, Arjuna — you who love me. I speak these words for your bliss and peace.

Neither the great gods nor the primordial sages know my origin, for I am the source from which gods and sages alike arose. Whoever knows me as unborn, beginningless, and lord of all worlds — that person alone is free from delusion and cleansed of all wrongdoing.

Every quality distributed among mortal beings springs from me: intellect, skill, enlightenment, endurance, self-control, truthfulness, equanimity, joy and grief, birth and death, fear and fearlessness, honor and shame, harmlessness, contentment, austerity, generosity, and the will to help — all are my gifts.

The seven great sages, the four ancient elders, and the cosmic progenitors were all born from my thought. From them arose all the races of humanity.

Whoever comprehends this truth — the full scope of my power and presence — is united with me through unwavering faith. The wise know me as the source of everything, and with hearts and breaths devoted to me, they teach each other and find happiness and peace. To those who serve with love, I give the insight that draws them to me. And out of compassion, dwelling within their souls, I dispel their darkness with the bright lamp of wisdom.
D: Elegant: I am the source from which gods and sages arose — yet none of them know my origin. Every human quality springs from me: intellect, courage, truthfulness, joy, grief, fear, and fearlessness. Whoever comprehends this is united with me beyond all doubt. To those who serve with love, I light the lamp of wisdom within their own darkness.
D: Distilled: Every quality you admire in human beings — courage, truthfulness, intellect, compassion — is a fragment of the divine, distributed across mortal lives.
Notes: Krishna's self-description expands to encompass all human qualities — including negative ones (grief, fear, death). The divine is not only the source of the admirable but of everything. The Seven Sages (Saptarishi) and Four Kumaras (Sanaka, Sanandana, Sanatana, Sanatkumara) are cosmic progenitors in Hindu mythology. 'I dispel their darkness with the bright lamp of wisdom' (tesham evanukampartham aham ajnana-jam tamah, nashayamy atma-bhava-stho jnana-dipena bhasvata) — God as inner teacher, not external authority. The framework parallel: the capacity for wisdom is not something imposed from outside but awakened from within — though it often requires a teacher or tradition to trigger the awakening.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Yes! Thou art Parabrahm! The High Abode!
The Great Purification! Thou art God
Eternal, All-creating, Holy, First,
Without beginning! Lord of Lords and Gods!
Declared by all the Saints--by Narada,
Vyasa Asita, and Devalas;
And here Thyself declaring unto me!
What Thou hast said now know I to be truth,
O Kesava! that neither gods nor men
Nor demons comprehend Thy mystery
Made manifest, Divinest! Thou Thyself
Thyself alone dost know, Maker Supreme!
Master of all the living! Lord of Gods!
King of the Universe! To Thee alone
Belongs to tell the heavenly excellence
Of those perfections wherewith Thou dost fill
These worlds of Thine; Pervading, Immanent!
How shall I learn, Supremest Mystery!
To know Thee, though I muse continually?
Under what form of Thine unnumbered forms
Mayst Thou be grasped? Ah! yet again recount,
Clear and complete, Thy great appearances,
The secrets of Thy Majesty and Might,
Thou High Delight of Men! Never enough
Can mine ears drink the Amrit of such words!
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
You are the Supreme Brahman, the great purification, the eternal God — unborn, beginningless, lord of lords. All the sages confirm this, and now you declare it yourself.

I accept everything you have told me as truth, Krishna. Neither gods nor demons comprehend your mystery. You alone know yourself.

You alone can tell the full measure of your divine powers — the forms through which you pervade these worlds. How can I know you? Under which of your countless forms should I contemplate you? Tell me again, in full, of your powers and manifestations — for I can never hear enough of your words.
D: Elegant: The sages confirm it. Now you confirm it yourself. I believe you — but I cannot comprehend you. You alone know what you are. Tell me again: under which of your infinite forms should I seek you? I can never hear enough.
D: Distilled: The student says: I believe, but I do not yet understand. Tell me more — I am not finished listening.
Notes: Arjuna's response models the ideal student: acceptance without passivity, belief without the pretense of full comprehension, and the hunger for more. 'Neither gods nor demons comprehend thy mystery' — even the divine beings operate within a framework they cannot fully see. Only the source knows itself completely. The request for specific vibhutis (divine manifestations) is pedagogically important: abstract theology becomes graspable only through concrete examples.

D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Very well — I will tell you some of my chief manifestations, for there is no end to my extent.

I am the Self seated in every creature's heart. I am the beginning, middle, and end of all beings.

Among the sun-gods, I am Vishnu. Among the storms, I am Marichi. Among the stars, I am the moon. Among the Vedas, I am the Sama Veda. Among the gods, I am Indra. Among the senses, I am the mind. Among living beings, I am consciousness.

Among the destroyers, I am Shankara. Among the nature-spirits, I am Kubera, lord of wealth. Among the elemental gods, I am fire. Among mountains, I am Meru.

Among priests, I am Brihaspati. Among warriors, I am Skanda. Among waters, I am the ocean. Among sages, I am Bhrigu. Among sounds, I am OM.

Among prayers, I am the whispered chant. Among mountains, I am the Himalayas. Among trees, I am the sacred fig. Among divine sages, I am Narada. Among horses, I am Uchchaishravas, born from the churning of the cosmic ocean. Among elephants, Airavata. Among men, the king.

Among weapons, I am the thunderbolt. Among cows, I am the wish-fulfilling Kamadhuk. Among serpents, I am Vasuki. Among the thousand-headed, Ananta, on whose coils Vishnu reclines. Among water-lords, Varuna. Among judges, Yama.

Among demons, I am Prahlada. Among measures, I am time itself. Among beasts, the tiger. Among birds, Garuda. Among winds, the whirlwind. Among warriors, Rama. Among fish, the great sea-creature. Among rivers, the Ganges.

I am the beginning, middle, and end of all creation. Among sciences, I am the science of the Self. Among arguments, I am logic itself. Among letters, I am the letter A. Among compound words, I am the pairing. I am endless time. I am the creator facing every direction. I am death that seizes all and birth that brings all forth.

Among feminine virtues, I am fame, fortune, speech, memory, intelligence, constancy, and patience. Among hymns, the Brihat-Saman. Among meters, Gayatri. Among months, the harvest month. Among seasons, spring. Among gamblers' throws, I am the winning cast. I am the splendor of the splendid and the greatness of the great. I am victory, effort, and the goodness of the good.

Among the Vrishnis, I am Vasudeva. Among the Pandavas — I am you, Arjuna.

Among poets, I am Ushana. Among lawgivers, Vyasa. Among rulers, I am wise governance. Among secrets, I am silence. Among the learned, I am the knowledge they possess.

Whatever exists with power, beauty, or glory — know that it springs from a fraction of my radiance. But what use is all this detail? I, who am everything, who made everything, stand apart as its lord.
D: Elegant: I am the Self in every heart. I am the beginning, middle, and end. Among suns, I am the brightest. Among sounds, OM. Among mountains, Himalayas. Among rivers, Ganges. Among letters, A. Among seasons, spring. Among secrets, silence. Among the Pandavas — I am you, Arjuna. Whatever exists with power, beauty, or glory springs from a fraction of my radiance.
D: Distilled: The divine is not somewhere else — it is the excellence within everything: the silence in secrets, the spring among seasons, the courage in warriors.
Notes: The vibhuti (divine manifestation) catalogue is the Gita's most elaborate literary device: by identifying the divine with the best of every category, Krishna makes abstract theology tangible. Key entries: 'Among arguments, I am logic' (vadah pravadatam aham) — even the capacity for rational argument is divine. 'Among secrets, I am silence' — the deepest knowledge is held, not spoken. 'Among the Pandavas, I am you' — the most intimate vibhuti: Arjuna himself is a manifestation of the divine. The framework parallel: every domain of human excellence — science, art, governance, argument, memory — is a manifestation of the same underlying principle. The pursuit of excellence in any domain, done with proper orientation, is a form of worship.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
HERE ENDETH CHAPTER X. OF THE BHAGAVAD-GITA,
Entitled "Vibhuti Yog,"
Or "The Book of Religion by the Heavenly Perfections."
Notes: Chapter colophon. Vibhuti Yoga — the Yoga of Divine Manifestations. This chapter made abstract theology concrete by cataloguing divine excellence across every domain of existence. The pattern: in every category, the divine is the highest instance. The framework parallel: 'the splendor of the splendid and the greatness of the great' — the best of anything is a window into the nature of everything. Excellence, wherever it appears, points toward the same source.

D: Original: Chapter 11: The Vision of the Universal Form (Visvarupa Darshana Yoga)

[Arjuna]
This, for my soul's peace, have I heard from Thee,
The unfolding of the Mystery Supreme
Named Adhyatman; comprehending which,
My darkness is dispelled; for now I know--
O Lotus-eyed!--whence is the birth of men,
And whence their death, and what the majesties
Of Thine immortal rule. Fain would I see,
As thou Thyself declar'st it, Sovereign Lord!
The likeness of that glory of Thy Form
Wholly revealed. O Thou Divinest One!
If this can be, if I may bear the sight,
Make Thyself visible, Lord of all prayers!
Show me Thy very self, the Eternal God!
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
What you have told me about the supreme mystery of the Self has dispelled my confusion. Now I understand the origin and dissolution of all beings, and the majesty of your eternal nature.

But I want to see it — not just hear about it. If you think I can bear the sight, reveal yourself to me. Show me your true form, your eternal being.
D: Elegant: I have heard the teaching and my confusion is gone. But hearing is not seeing. If I can bear it — show me your true form.
D: Distilled: Understanding the truth about reality is not the same as seeing it — and the student asks to see.
Notes: The transition from jnana (knowledge through teaching) to darshana (direct vision). Arjuna asks for what every genuine seeker eventually asks: not more explanation but direct experience. 'If I may bear the sight' — Arjuna intuits that seeing the truth might be overwhelming. This anticipation proves accurate. The framework parallel: there is a difference between understanding the erosion intellectually and seeing it clearly enough to be moved to action.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Gaze, then, thou Son of Pritha! I manifest for thee
Those hundred thousand thousand shapes that clothe my Mystery:
I show thee all my semblances, infinite, rich, divine,
My changeful hues, my countless forms. See! in this face of mine,
Adityas, Vasus, Rudras, Aswins, and Maruts; see
Wonders unnumbered, Indian Prince! revealed to none save thee.
Behold! this is the Universe!--Look! what is live and dead
I gather all in one--in Me! Gaze, as thy lips have said,
On GOD ETERNAL, VERY GOD! See Me! see what thou prayest!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Then look, Arjuna — I reveal to you my hundreds of thousands of divine forms. Every shape, every color, every manifestation — infinite and rich. See in my face the Adityas, Vasus, Rudras, the Ashvins and Maruts. See wonders never shown to any being before you.

Behold: the entire universe — everything that lives and everything that has died — gathered into one. Gaze upon the Eternal God, as you asked.
D: Elegant: Then look — at a hundred thousand shapes clothed in divine light. See the entire universe gathered in one form. Everything alive and everything dead, held together. This is what you asked for.
D: Distilled: When the divine reveals itself, it is not one thing but everything — the entire universe compressed into a single face.
Notes: Krishna grants Arjuna's request — the Vishvarupa (universal form). The catalogue of divine beings (Adityas, Vasus, Rudras, Ashvins, Maruts) populates the vision with the full pantheon of Vedic mythology. 'Wonders revealed to none save thee' — this vision is unique, granted only to Arjuna. The framework parallel: certain forms of understanding cannot be transmitted through teaching alone; they require direct encounter.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Thou canst not!--nor, with human eyes, Arjuna! ever mayest!
Therefore I give thee sense divine. Have other eyes, new light!
And, look! This is My glory, unveiled to mortal sight!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
But you cannot see this with human eyes. Therefore I give you divine sight — a new way of seeing. Now look upon my glory.
D: Elegant: Human eyes cannot bear this. I give you divine sight. Now look.
D: Distilled: Seeing the truth requires a different kind of sight — one that must be given, not earned.
Notes: Divyam dadami te cakshuh (I give you divine eyes) — the vision requires a faculty that Arjuna does not naturally possess. Krishna must upgrade his perception. This is significant: the highest truth is not accessible through ordinary cognition. It requires a transformed mode of awareness. The framework parallel: the shift from conventional thinking to genuine philosophical understanding is not incremental improvement but a qualitative change in how one sees.

D: Original:
[Sanjaya]
Then, O King! the God, so saying,
Stood, to Pritha's Son displaying
All the splendour, wonder, dread
Of His vast Almighty-head.
Out of countless eyes beholding,
Out of countless mouths commanding,
Countless mystic forms enfolding
In one Form: supremely standing
Countless radiant glories wearing,
Countless heavenly weapons bearing,
Crowned with garlands of star-clusters,
Robed in garb of woven lustres,
Breathing from His perfect Presence
Breaths of every subtle essence
Of all heavenly odours; shedding
Blinding brilliance; overspreading--
Boundless, beautiful--all spaces
With His all-regarding faces;
So He showed! If there should rise
Suddenly within the skies
Sunburst of a thousand suns
Flooding earth with beams undeemed-of,
Then might be that Holy One's
Majesty and radiance dreamed of!
D: Refashioned:
[Sanjaya]
Then the great God revealed himself to Arjuna — all splendor, all wonder, all dread. Countless eyes seeing. Countless mouths commanding. Countless forms folded into one supreme form. Radiant with garlands of stars, robed in light, breathing the essence of every heavenly fragrance. Blazing, blinding, beautiful, boundless — filling all of space with his all-regarding faces.

If a thousand suns were to burst into the sky at once, that might approach the radiance of that supreme being.
D: Elegant: Countless eyes. Countless mouths. Countless forms folded into one — crowned with stars, robed in light, breathing heaven's perfume, filling all of space. If a thousand suns rose at once, they might begin to approach that radiance.
D: Distilled: The vision of the divine is a thousand suns rising at once — too bright to look at, too vast to comprehend, impossible to look away from.
Notes: The 'thousand suns' simile (divi surya-sahasrasya bhaved yugapad utthita) is the most famous image in the Gita. Robert Oppenheimer quoted this passage when witnessing the first nuclear detonation at Trinity, 1945: 'Now I am become Death, the destroyer of worlds' (from 11.134). The simile captures the specific quality of encountering something that exceeds your capacity to process it — the sublime in its original philosophical sense (Burke, Kant). The framework parallel: genuine encounter with a Fundamental Question produces not comfortable understanding but overwhelming awe.

D: Original:
[Sanjaya]
So did Pandu's Son behold
All this universe enfold
All its huge diversity
Into one vast shape, and be
Visible, and viewed, and blended
In one Body--subtle, splendid,
Nameless--th' All-comprehending
God of Gods, the Never-Ending
Deity!
D: Refashioned:
[Sanjaya]
Arjuna saw the entire universe — all its vast diversity — folded into one body, one nameless form: the all-comprehending God of Gods, the Never-Ending.
D: Elegant: The whole universe — every separation, every distinction — folded into one body. One nameless, all-comprehending form.
D: Distilled: All diversity gathered into one — the vision is not of something new but of the unity behind everything that already exists.
Notes: The Vishvarupa is not a new form added to the universe but the perception of the universe's underlying unity. Every separate thing is revealed as a facet of one thing. This is the experiential version of the philosophical claim made in Chapters 7-10: all is Vasudeva.

D: Original:
[Sanjaya]
But, sore amazed,
Thrilled, o'erfilled, dazzled, and dazed,
Arjuna knelt; and bowed his head,
And clasped his palms; and cried, and said:
D: Refashioned:
[Sanjaya]
Overwhelmed, dazzled, trembling, Arjuna fell to his knees, bowed his head, clasped his palms together, and spoke:
D: Elegant: Arjuna falls to his knees, clasps his hands, and speaks — not from understanding but from awe.
D: Distilled: The first response to the infinite is not words but the body's surrender: knees bend, head bows, hands clasp.
Notes: The physical response precedes verbal response — the body recognizes what the mind cannot yet process. Compare with Arjuna's physical collapse in Chapter 1: there, his body refused to fight. Here, his body refuses to stand. Both are responses to encounters that exceed the capacity of the rational mind.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Yea! I have seen! I see!
Lord! all is wrapped in Thee!
The gods are in Thy glorious frame! the creatures
Of earth, and heaven, and hell
In Thy Divine form dwell,
And in Thy countenance shine all the features
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
I see it — everything is wrapped in you! The gods, the creatures of earth and heaven and the underworld, all dwelling in your divine form —
Notes: Verses 11.112-11.133 comprise Arjuna's extended response to the cosmic vision. Many are short devotional verses that together form one continuous outpouring of awe, praise, fear, and supplication. They are grouped thematically below rather than treated individually, as the vision is meant to overwhelm, not to be analyzed line by line.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Of Brahma, sitting lone
Upon His lotus-throne;
Of saints and sages, and the serpent races
Ananta, Vasuki;
Yea! mightiest Lord! I see
Thy thousand thousand arms, and breasts, and faces,
And eyes,--on every side
Perfect, diversified;
And nowhere end of Thee, nowhere beginning,
Nowhere a centre! Shifts--
Wherever soul's gaze lifts--
Thy central Self, all-wielding, and all-winning!
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
I see Brahma on his lotus throne, the sages, the cosmic serpents. I see your thousand arms, breasts, faces, and eyes — infinite, without beginning, middle, or end. Wherever I look, your central Self shifts — all-wielding, all-winning.
Notes: Arjuna sees specific divine beings within the universal form: Brahma (the creator), Ananta and Vasuki (cosmic serpents who support the world). The detail 'no beginning, no middle, no end' (anadi-madhyantam) captures the quality of infinity — the mind searches for boundaries and finds none.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Infinite King! I see
The anadem on Thee,
The club, the shell, the discus; see Thee burning
In beams insufferable,
Lighting earth, heaven, and hell
With brilliance blazing, glowing, flashing; turning
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
I see your crown, your mace, your discus — blazing with unbearable brilliance, lighting earth, heaven, and hell —
Notes: The divine attributes: the crown (kirita), mace (gada), and discus (chakra) are Vishnu's traditional symbols. The brilliance is 'insufferable' — diptam anala-arka-dyutim — light beyond what vision can process.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Darkness to dazzling day,
Look I whichever way;
Ah, Lord! I worship Thee, the Undivided,
The Uttermost of thought,
The Treasure-Palace wrought
To hold the wealth of the worlds; the Shield provided
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
— turning darkness to dazzling day in every direction. You are the Undivided, the Uttermost, the treasure-house of all worlds, the shield that protects righteousness —
Notes: Dharma-sya goptrr (protector of dharma/righteousness) — Krishna's cosmic role is not just creator but guardian of moral order.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
To shelter Virtue's laws;
The Fount whence Life's stream draws
All waters of all rivers of all being:
The One Unborn, Unending:
Unchanging and Unblending!
With might and majesty, past thought, past seeing!
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
— the source from which every river of being flows. Unborn, unending, unchanging — with might and majesty beyond thought or sight.

[Combined 11.112-11.116 — The Vision of Glory]: I see everything in you — gods and creatures, heaven and hell. Brahma on his lotus, the cosmic serpents, sages beyond number. Your thousand arms, faces, and eyes have no beginning, middle, or end. Your crown, your mace, your discus blaze with light that turns darkness to day. You are the Undivided, the Uttermost of thought, the treasure-house of worlds, the protector of righteousness, the source of every stream of being — unborn, unending, unchanging, beyond sight and beyond thought.
D: Elegant: Everything is in you — gods, creatures, heavens, hells. A thousand arms, a thousand faces, no beginning, no end. Light turns darkness to day. You are the source of every stream of being — unborn, unending, beyond thought.
D: Distilled: The vision reveals not a god among other things but the reality within which all things exist.
Notes: The combined passage establishes the first movement of Arjuna's response: pure awe at the beauty and majesty of the universal form. The imagery shifts from catalogue (specific divine beings) to quality (boundlessness, brilliance, timelessness). The framework parallel: the first encounter with a genuinely Fundamental Question produces not answers but awe — the recognition that you are facing something larger than your categories can hold.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Silver of moon and gold
Of sun are glories rolled
From Thy great eyes; Thy visage, beaming tender
Throughout the stars and skies,
Doth to warm life surprise
Thy Universe. The worlds are filled with wonder
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
The silver of the moon and the gold of the sun radiate from your eyes. Your gaze warms the universe to life. The worlds are filled with wonder —
Notes: Continuation of Arjuna's hymn. The sun and moon as Krishna's eyes — the daily cycle of light is reimagined as the divine gaze.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Of Thy perfections! Space
Star-sprinkled, and void place
From pole to pole of the Blue, from bound to bound,
Hath Thee in every spot,
Thee, Thee!--Where Thou art not,
O Holy, Marvellous Form! is nowhere found!
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
— at your perfections. From pole to pole, from every boundary to every boundary, there is no place where you are not.
Notes: Omnipresence: 'where thou art not is nowhere found.' The vision confirms the philosophical claim of Chapter 7 (the pearl-string) and Chapter 9 (I pervade everything).

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
O Mystic, Awful One!
At sight of Thee, made known,
The Three Worlds quake; the lower gods draw nigh Thee;
They fold their palms, and bow
Body, and breast, and brow,
And, whispering worship, laud and magnify Thee!
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
At the sight of you, the three worlds tremble. The lesser gods draw near and worship, folding their palms, bowing, whispering praise.
Notes: Even the gods worship when they see the universal form — establishing a hierarchy: lesser gods are themselves devotees before the supreme.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Rishis and Siddhas cry
"Hail! Highest Majesty!"
From sage and singer breaks the hymn of glory
In dulcet harmony,
Sounding the praise of Thee;
While countless companies take up the story,
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
Sages and celestial singers cry 'Hail! Highest Majesty!' — hymns of glory rising in harmony from countless companies.
Notes: Continuation. Rishis (sages) and Siddhas (perfected beings) respond to the vision with spontaneous worship.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Rudras, who ride the storms,
Th' Adityas' shining forms,
Vasus and Sadhyas, Viswas, Ushmapas;
Maruts, and those great Twins
The heavenly, fair, Aswins,
Gandharvas, Rakshasas, Siddhas, and Asuras,--
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
The Rudras, the Adityas, the Vasus, the Sadhyas, the celestial twins, the Gandharvas, the Rakshasas, the Siddhas, and the Asuras —
Notes: A catalogue of divine and semi-divine beings, all witnessing the vision. The list includes both benevolent (Gandharvas, Siddhas) and malevolent (Rakshasas, Asuras) beings — all are equally awed.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
These see Thee, and revere
In sudden-stricken fear;
Yea! the Worlds,--seeing Thee with form stupendous,
With faces manifold,
With eyes which all behold,
Unnumbered eyes, vast arms, members tremendous,
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
— all see you with sudden-stricken fear. The worlds behold your stupendous form — countless faces, countless eyes, vast arms, tremendous limbs —
Notes: The tone shifts from awe to fear. The vision is no longer only beautiful — it is terrifying. The word bhita (frightened) marks the transition.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Flanks, lit with sun and star,
Feet planted near and far,
Tushes of terror, mouths wrathful and tender;--
The Three wide Worlds before Thee
Adore, as I adore Thee,
Quake, as I quake, to witness so much splendour!
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
— flanks lit by sun and star, feet spanning near and far, mouths with terrible tusks, expressions both wrathful and tender. The three worlds quake at the sight, as I quake.

[Combined 11.117-11.123 — Cosmic Awe and Trembling]: Moon-silver and sun-gold shine from your eyes. Your perfections fill space from boundary to boundary — there is no place where you are not. At the sight of you, the three worlds tremble. Gods and sages draw near with folded palms, crying 'Hail!' The storm-gods, sun-gods, spirits, singers, and demons all stare in sudden-stricken fear at your form — stupendous, countless-faced, terrible and tender. The worlds adore as I adore, and quake as I quake.
D: Elegant: The worlds quake. Gods and sages fold their palms. Celestial singers cry 'Hail!' Even demons stare in terror. Your form is both terrible and tender — and everything that exists trembles before it, as I tremble.
D: Distilled: The vision of ultimate reality is both beautiful and terrifying — and everything in creation responds with the same trembling.
Notes: The vision now holds beauty and terror simultaneously — tusks alongside tender expressions. Rudolf Otto's concept of the numinous (mysterium tremendum et fascinans) describes exactly this experience: the holy as simultaneously attractive and terrifying. The Gita's vision anticipates Otto by millennia. The framework parallel: genuine encounter with the Fundamental Questions is not comfortable. It disrupts settled categories and demands a response that involves the whole person — intellect, emotion, and body.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
I mark Thee strike the skies
With front, in wondrous wise
Huge, rainbow-painted, glittering; and thy mouth
Opened, and orbs which see
All things, whatever be
In all Thy worlds, east, west, and north and south.
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
I see you striking the skies — enormous, rainbow-painted, glittering — your mouth open, your eyes seeing everything in every direction.
Notes: The imagery becomes more ominous: the open mouth will become the central terrifying image of the vision.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
O Eyes of God! O Head!
My strength of soul is fled,
Gone is heart's force, rebuked is mind's desire!
When I behold Thee so,
With awful brows a-glow,
With burning glance, and lips lighted by fire
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
My strength fails. My courage is gone. My mind recoils. Your brows glow with awful light. Your lips are lit with fire —
Notes: Arjuna's physical response mirrors his collapse in Chapter 1 (trembling, strength failing) — but the cause is different. In Chapter 1, he saw the horror of killing his family. Here, he sees the horror of ultimate reality. Both are encounters with truths too large for the ordinary mind to process.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Fierce as those flames which shall
Consume, at close of all,
Earth, Heaven! Ah me! I see no Earth and Heaven!
Thee, Lord of Lords! I see,
Thee only-only Thee!
Now let Thy mercy unto me be given,
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
— fierce as the flames that will consume everything at the end of time. I see no earth, no heaven — only you, Lord of Lords, only you. Let your mercy be upon me.
Notes: Kala-anala (time-fire) — the fire that consumes the cosmos at the end of each cycle (pralaya). Arjuna sees the destructive aspect of the divine: not just the creator and sustainer but the annihilator.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Thou Refuge of the World!
Lo! to the cavern hurled
Of Thy wide-opened throat, and lips white-tushed,
I see our noblest ones,
Great Dhritarashtra's sons,
Bhishma, Drona, and Karna, caught and crushed!
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
I see our noblest warriors — Dhritirashtra's sons, Bhishma, Drona, Karna — rushing into the cavern of your terrible mouth, caught and crushed between your white-tusked jaws.
Notes: The vision becomes specific and personal: Arjuna sees the very warriors he was afraid to fight in Chapter 1 being devoured by the universal form. Their death is not something Arjuna will cause — it is something that has already been determined. This reframes the entire ethical dilemma: Arjuna is not the killer but the instrument.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
The Kings and Chiefs drawn in,
That gaping gorge within;
The best of both these armies torn and riven!
Between Thy jaws they lie
Mangled full bloodily,
Ground into dust and death! Like streams down-driven
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
Kings and chiefs drawn into that gaping maw — the best of both armies torn, mangled, ground to dust between your jaws.
Notes: Both armies — not just the Kauravas but also the Pandavas' allies — are being consumed. The destruction is universal, not partisan.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
With helpless haste, which go
In headlong furious flow
Straight to the gulfing deeps of th' unfilled ocean,
So to that flaming cave
Those heroes great and brave
Pour, in unending streams, with helpless motion!
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
Like rivers in helpless flood pouring straight into the ocean's depths, these heroes stream into your flaming mouth with unstoppable motion.
Notes: The river metaphor: warriors flowing to their death as inevitably as rivers flowing to the sea. The 'helpless haste' (vegena) emphasizes that this is not a choice — it is the nature of time.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Like moths which in the night
Flutter towards a light,
Drawn to their fiery doom, flying and dying,
So to their death still throng,
Blind, dazzled, borne along
Ceaselessly, all those multitudes, wild flying!
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
Like moths flying toward a flame — drawn, dazzled, dying — so these multitudes rush to their destruction, ceaselessly, blindly.

[Combined 11.124-11.130 — The Devouring]: Your mouth gapes open across the sky — rainbow-painted, glittering, terrible. My strength fails and my mind recoils. Your lips burn with fire fierce as the end of time. I see no earth, no heaven — only you.

And into your terrible mouth I see them pouring — Bhishma, Drona, Karna, the sons of Dhritirashtra, kings and chiefs of both armies — caught between your tusked jaws, mangled, ground to dust. Like rivers in helpless flood rushing to the ocean. Like moths drawn to a flame, dazzled and dying. Ceaselessly, blindly, they pour into your fire.
D: Elegant: Your mouth gapes across the sky. Into it pour the warriors of both armies — Bhishma, Drona, Karna — like rivers to the ocean, like moths to a flame. Crushed between your jaws. Ceaselessly. Blindly. My strength fails. I see no earth, no heaven — only you, devouring.
D: Distilled: The vision of ultimate reality includes the sight of everything you love being consumed — and recognizing that the consumption was always inevitable.
Notes: The moth metaphor (yatha pradiptam jvalanam patanga) is the vision's most devastating image: the warriors are not merely killed but drawn helplessly toward their own destruction, unable to resist. This is the Gita's most direct confrontation with the nature of time (kala): time does not merely permit death; it attracts beings toward it. The framework parallel: the erosion pattern is not accidental but gravitational — civilizations do not merely happen to decay; they are drawn toward decay by the same forces that created them. Recognition of this pattern is the first step toward resisting it.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Thou, that hast fashioned men,
Devourest them again,
One with another, great and small, alike!
The creatures whom Thou mak'st,
With flaming jaws Thou tak'st,
Lapping them up! Lord God! Thy terrors strike
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
You who fashioned all creatures devour them again — great and small alike — lapping them up with flaming jaws. Your terrors strike from end to end of the earth.
Notes: The creator is also the destroyer: the same power that makes also unmakes. This is not dualism (a good creator vs. an evil destroyer) but unity — creation and destruction are two phases of one process.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
From end to end of earth,
Filling life full, from birth
To death, with deadly, burning, lurid dread!
Ah, Vishnu! make me know
Why is Thy visage so?
Who art Thou, feasting thus upon Thy dead?
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
The world from birth to death is filled with your burning, lurid dread. Who are you? Why is your face so terrible? Who are you, feasting on the dead?
Notes: Arjuna demands to know: who is this terrifying being? The question 'Who art thou, feasting thus upon thy dead?' is one of the most powerful lines in the poem.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Who? awful Deity!
I bow myself to Thee,
Namostu Te, Devavara! Prasid!
O Mightiest Lord! rehearse
Why hast Thou face so fierce?
Whence doth this aspect horrible proceed?
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
Awful deity — I bow to you. Tell me who you are. Tell me why your face is so fierce. Where does this horrible aspect come from?

[Combined 11.131-11.133 — The Question]: You devour your own creation — great and small, lapped up in flaming jaws. The earth is filled with dread from end to end. I bow — but I must know: who are you? Why this terrible face? What is this horrible form?
D: Elegant: You made them. Now you eat them — great and small. I bow, but I must ask: who are you? Why this face? Why this dread?
D: Distilled: The deepest question facing any theology: if the same power creates and destroys, what does that make the power?
Notes: Arjuna's question reaches the theological crisis at the heart of the vision: if God is both creator and destroyer, if God devours what God made — what is God? This is not atheism but the deepest form of theism: confronting the full nature of the divine, including the parts that terrify. The framework parallel: any honest account of Reality must include the destructive forces, not just the creative ones. A framework that only addresses what builds and never what erodes is incomplete.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Thou seest Me as Time who kills,
Time who brings all to doom,
The Slayer Time, Ancient of Days, come hither to consume;
Excepting thee, of all these hosts of hostile chiefs arrayed,
There stands not one shall leave alive the battlefield! Dismayed
No longer be! Arise! obtain renown! destroy thy foes!
Fight for the kingdom waiting thee when thou hast vanquished those.
By Me they fall--not thee! the stroke of death is dealt them now,
Even as they show thus gallantly; My instrument art thou!
Strike, strong-armed Prince, at Drona! at Bhishma strike! deal death
On Karna, Jyadratha; stay all their warlike breath!
'Tis I who bid them perish! Thou wilt but slay the slain;
Fight! they must fall, and thou must live, victor upon this plain!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
I am Time — the destroyer of worlds, come here to consume. With or without you, none of these warriors arrayed for battle will survive.

So do not hesitate. Rise. Win glory. Conquer your enemies. Fight for the kingdom that waits for you. These men are already dead — struck down by me, not by you. You are only the instrument.

Strike at Drona, at Bhishma, at Karna. They must fall. It is I who bid them perish. You will only slay the slain. Fight — and you will be victorious.
D: Elegant: I am Time — the destroyer. These warriors are already dead. I killed them, not you. You are the instrument, not the cause. Rise. Fight. You will only slay the slain.
D: Distilled: You are not the cause of what happens — you are the instrument through which what was always going to happen comes to pass.
Notes: Kalo 'smi lokakshaya-krit pravriddho — 'I am Time, the mighty destroyer of worlds.' This is the verse Oppenheimer famously recalled at Trinity (July 16, 1945), though he paraphrased it as 'Now I am become Death, the destroyer of worlds.' Krishna's answer to 'who are you?' is not a name but a function: Time. The reframing is radical: Arjuna's ethical dilemma dissolves because the warriors are 'already slain' (mayaivaite nihatah purvam eva) — their death is determined by cosmic necessity, not by Arjuna's choice. He is nimitta-matram (merely the instrument). This raises profound questions about free will, moral responsibility, and the nature of agency. The framework parallel: individuals do not single-handedly cause cultural erosion or restoration — they participate in patterns larger than themselves. But participation is still meaningful, and the quality of one's participation still matters.

D: Original:
[Sanjaya]
Hearing mighty Keshav's word,
Tremblingly that helmed Lord
Clasped his lifted palms, and--praying
Grace of Krishna--stood there, saying,
With bowed brow and accents broken,
These words, timorously spoken:
D: Refashioned:
[Sanjaya]
Hearing Krishna's words, Arjuna trembled. Clasping his palms together, bowing, he spoke with broken voice:
D: Elegant: Arjuna trembles, clasps his palms, bows, and speaks in a broken voice — not the voice of a warrior but of a man who has seen too much.
D: Distilled: After the vision of ultimate reality, the warrior speaks not with strength but with the broken humility of someone who finally understands the scale of what he faces.
Notes: Krita-anjalih (with palms joined) — the gesture of supplication and reverence. Arjuna's voice is 'broken' (gadgadaya) — not from weakness but from the impossibility of putting the vision into words. The body's response to the numinous: trembling, bowing, stammering.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Worthily, Lord of Might!
The whole world hath delight
In Thy surpassing power, obeying Thee;
The Rakshasas, in dread
At sight of Thee, are sped
To all four quarters; and the company
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
It is right that the world delights in your power. The demons flee in terror to every direction, and the perfected beings bow before you —
Notes: Arjuna begins his second hymn: a prayer of reconciliation after the terrifying vision. The tone shifts from terror to devotional submission.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Of Siddhas sound Thy name.
How should they not proclaim
Thy Majesties, Divinest, Mightiest?
Thou Brahm, than Brahma greater!
Thou Infinite Creator!
Thou God of gods, Life's Dwelling-place and Rest!
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
How could they not praise you? You are Brahma, greater than Brahma — infinite creator, God of gods, dwelling-place of the universe, the foundation of all life.
Notes: Adi-karta (original creator) — Krishna is identified as prior to Brahma, the creator-god of the Hindu trinity. The hierarchy: Brahma creates, but Krishna is the source from which Brahma's creative power itself derives.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Thou, of all souls the Soul!
The Comprehending Whole!
Of being formed, and formless being the Framer;
O Utmost One! O Lord!
Older than eld, Who stored
The worlds with wealth of life! O Treasure-Claimer,
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
You are the soul of all souls, the comprehending whole — the framer of both form and formlessness, older than the oldest, the storer of worlds.
Notes: Adi-deva (primal god), purana-purusha (ancient person) — terms emphasizing the primordial nature of the divine.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Who wottest all, and art
Wisdom Thyself! O Part
In all, and All; for all from Thee have risen
Numberless now I see
The aspects are of Thee!
Vayu Thou art, and He who keeps the prison
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
You know everything and are everything. You are Vayu, the wind. You are Yama, the lord of death. You are Agni, fire. You are Varuna, the waters. You are Prajapati, the first father, and the grandfather of the first father. I bow to you a thousand thousand times.
Notes: Arjuna identifies Krishna with the major Vedic deities: Vayu (wind), Yama (death/justice), Agni (fire), Varuna (cosmic waters/law), Prajapati (lord of creatures). Each god is an aspect of the one supreme being.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Of Narak, Yama dark;
And Agni's shining spark;
Varuna's waves are Thy waves. Moon and starlight
Are Thine! Prajapati
Art Thou, and 'tis to Thee
They knelt in worshipping the old world's far light,
Notes: Continuation of Arjuna's identification of Krishna with all deities, including the celestial lights and the ancient worship of the first mortals.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
The first of mortal men.
Again, Thou God! again
A thousand thousand times be magnified!
Honour and worship be--
Glory and praise,--to Thee
Namo, Namaste, cried on every side;
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
Honor and glory and praise — Namo, Namaste — cried from every side, a thousand thousand times!

[Combined 11.136-11.141 — Arjuna's Hymn of Praise]: It is right that the world rejoices in your power. Demons flee, the perfected bow. How could they not? You are greater than Brahma — infinite creator, God of gods, soul of souls. You are wind and death and fire and water and the first father. You know everything and are everything. A thousand thousand times, from every direction — Namaste!
D: Elegant: You are greater than Brahma. You are wind, fire, death, and water. You are the first father and the grandfather of the first father. From every direction, from every being: Namaste — a thousand thousand times.
D: Distilled: When you see the source of everything, the only response is to bow from every direction at once.
Notes: Namo, Namaste (I bow) — the quintessential gesture of Hindu devotion, repeated with the urgency of someone who has seen the truth and cannot bow enough. The thousand-fold repetition is not rhetoric but an attempt to match an infinite reality with finite words.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Cried here, above, below,
Uttered when Thou dost go,
Uttered where Thou dost come! Namo! we call;
Namostu! God adored!
Namostu! Nameless Lord!
Hail to Thee! Praise to Thee! Thou One in all;
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
Namaste — cried above, below, and on all sides. Wherever you go, wherever you come — praise to you, the One in All!
Notes: Omnidirectional praise: the divine is everywhere, so worship must come from everywhere.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
For Thou art All! Yea, Thou!
Ah! if in anger now
Thou shouldst remember I did think Thee Friend,
Speaking with easy speech,
As men use each to each;
Did call Thee "Krishna," "Prince," nor comprehend
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
You are everything. If in my ignorance I treated you as a friend — calling you 'Krishna,' calling you 'Prince,' speaking casually, as people speak to equals —
Notes: The social horror of retrospection: Arjuna realizes he has been treating the supreme being as a friend and equal — joking, arguing, traveling together. Sakha (friend) — the Gita's deepest irony: Krishna IS Arjuna's friend, but he is also the creator and destroyer of worlds.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Thy hidden majesty,
The might, the awe of Thee;
Did, in my heedlessness, or in my love,
On journey, or in jest,
Or when we lay at rest,
Sitting at council, straying in the grove,
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
— not understanding your hidden majesty, not comprehending your might — whether in heedlessness or in love, on the road, at rest, at meals, sitting together, alone or among others —
Notes: Arjuna catalogs all the casual moments of their friendship — traveling, eating, resting, joking — each one now recontextualized as an encounter with the infinite. The intimacy makes the revelation more devastating, not less.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Alone, or in the throng,
Do Thee, most Holy! wrong,
Be Thy grace granted for that witless sin!
For Thou art, now I know,
Father of all below,
Of all above, of all the worlds within
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
— forgive me for whatever wrong I did in my ignorance. For you are now revealed as the father of all that is below, above, and within —

[Combined 11.142-11.145 — The Apology]: Praise to you from every direction! But now I must ask — if I have wronged you by treating you as a mere friend, calling you 'Krishna' and 'Prince,' speaking casually on the road, at meals, at rest, alone or in company — forgive me. I did not know. I did not comprehend what you are. You are the father of everything above, below, and within.
D: Elegant: Forgive me. I called you 'friend.' I called you 'Krishna.' I joked with you on the road and at meals. I did not know I was speaking casually to the creator of worlds. Forgive my ignorance — and my love.
D: Distilled: The most intimate relationships can also be the most blind — and the revelation of what was always there changes everything retroactively.
Notes: This passage contains one of the Gita's most psychologically complex moments: Arjuna apologizes for having loved Krishna as a friend rather than worshipping him as God. But the love was not wrong — it was incomplete. Krishna will later confirm (11.154) that love and devotion are the highest path, and friendship with the divine is not an error but an achievement. The framework parallel: the discovery that what you treated as ordinary was actually sacred does not invalidate the relationship — it deepens it.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Guru of Gurus; more
To reverence and adore
Than all which is adorable and high!
How, in the wide worlds three
Should any equal be?
Should any other share Thy Majesty?
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
You are the guru of gurus — more worthy of reverence than anything that exists. Nothing in the three worlds can equal you. How could anything surpass you?
D: Elegant: Teacher of teachers, greater than everything — how could anything in three worlds equal you, let alone surpass you?
D: Distilled: The highest authority is not one option among many but the standard against which all other authority is measured.
Notes: Guru of gurus (guror gariyase) — the ultimate teacher, the source from which all teaching derives. The framework parallel: the Fundamental Questions are the 'guru of gurus' in the intellectual domain — the questions from which all other questions derive their importance.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Therefore, with body bent
And reverent intent,
I praise, and serve, and seek Thee, asking grace.
As father to a son,
As friend to friend, as one
Who loveth to his lover, turn Thy face
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
Therefore, with body bent and heart full of reverence, I bow and ask your grace. Bear with me as a father with a son, as a friend with a friend, as a lover with the beloved.
Notes: Three models for the divine-human relationship: father-son (authority + nurture), friend-friend (equality + trust), lover-beloved (intimacy + passion). Arjuna asks for all three simultaneously.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
In gentleness on me!
Good is it I did see
This unknown marvel of Thy Form! But fear
Mingles with joy! Retake,
Dear Lord! for pity's sake
Thine earthly shape, which earthly eyes may bear!
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
I am glad I saw this — your unknown, marvelous form. But fear mingles with my joy. Please, out of pity, take back your earthly shape — the one that human eyes can bear.
Notes: The request to return to the familiar form — Arjuna has seen enough. The human psyche cannot sustain encounter with the infinite. This is not weakness but honest acknowledgment of limitation.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Be merciful, and show
The visage that I know;
Let me regard Thee, as of yore, arrayed
With disc and forehead-gem,
With mace and anadem,
Thou that sustainest all things! Undismayed
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
Be merciful. Show me the face I know — with the discus, the crown, the mace. Let me see the form I loved before this overwhelming vision.
Notes: Chaturbhuja (four-armed form) — Arjuna asks for the familiar Vishnu form: four arms holding conch, discus, mace, and lotus. This is still divine but comprehensible — a step between the cosmic vision and the human disguise.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Let me once more behold
The form I loved of old,
Thou of the thousand arms and countless eyes!
This frightened heart is fain
To see restored again
My Charioteer, in Krishna's kind disguise.
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
Let me once more see what I loved — you, of the thousand arms and countless eyes, yes, but in the kind disguise of my charioteer, my Krishna.

[Combined 11.146-11.150 — The Plea to Return]: You are the guru of gurus — nothing equals you. I bow with everything I am. Bear with me as a father bears a son, a friend a friend, a lover the beloved. I am glad I saw your true form — but fear mingles with joy. Please, Lord — take back your earthly shape. Show me the face I know. Let me see my charioteer again, my Krishna, in his kind disguise.
D: Elegant: I saw your true form and I am glad. But I am also afraid. Bear with me — as father, friend, and lover — and become my Krishna again. Let me see the kind face of my charioteer.
D: Distilled: The deepest request after encountering the infinite: please become small enough for me to love you again.
Notes: Arjuna's plea reveals the essential tension of all mystical experience: the vision is true but unsustainable. Human beings cannot live in constant encounter with the infinite — they need the divine mediated through familiar forms. 'My charioteer, in Krishna's kind disguise' — the disguise is itself an act of grace, not deception. The framework parallel: the Academy cannot operate at the level of the Fundamental Questions all the time. It must translate cosmic concerns into practical, human-scale forms — curricula, institutions, mentoring relationships — without losing awareness of what those forms point toward.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Yea! thou hast seen, Arjuna! because I loved thee well,
The secret countenance of Me, revealed by mystic spell,
Shining, and wonderful, and vast, majestic, manifold,
Which none save thou in all the years had favour to behold;
For not by Vedas cometh this, nor sacrifice, nor alms,
Nor works well-done, nor penance long, nor prayers, nor chaunted
psalms,
That mortal eyes should bear to view the Immortal Soul unclad,
Prince of the Kurus! This was kept for thee alone! Be glad!
Let no more trouble shake thy heart, because thine eyes have seen
My terror with My glory. As I before have been
So will I be again for thee; with lightened heart behold!
Once more I am thy Krishna, the form thou knew'st of old!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
You have seen this because I love you — this secret form, shining and vast and manifold, which no one else in all of history has been shown. It cannot be seen through scriptures or sacrifices, through charity or rituals or penance. It was kept for you alone.

Let your heart be troubled no more. Do not be afraid. Look — I am your Krishna again, the form you knew before.
D: Elegant: This vision was for you alone — not earned by scripture, sacrifice, or penance, but given through love. Let your heart be still. Look — I am Krishna again. The form you knew. The friend you loved.
D: Distilled: The deepest revelation is not earned but given — and the gift is followed by the return to the familiar, so that love can breathe.
Notes: Krishna confirms what Arjuna suspected: this vision cannot be accessed through any conventional religious practice. It is given by grace (prasada) through love (bhakti). The return to the familiar form is itself an act of compassion — Krishna puts the infinite back into the container Arjuna can bear. 'As I before have been, so will I be again for thee' — the infinite does not resent being made small. The framework parallel: the movement between the cosmic and the personal, between the Fundamental Questions and daily practice, is not a failure of nerve but the natural rhythm of wisdom.

D: Original:
[Sanjaya]
These words to Arjuna spake
Vasudev, and straight did take
Back again the semblance dear
Of the well-loved charioteer;
Peace and joy it did restore
When the Prince beheld once more
Mighty BRAHMA's form and face
Clothed in Krishna's gentle grace.
D: Refashioned:
[Sanjaya]
Having spoken, Krishna took back his gentle, human form — the beloved charioteer. Arjuna's heart was restored to peace.
D: Elegant: Krishna becomes Krishna again. The cosmic vision folds back into the face of a friend. Peace returns.
D: Distilled: After the infinite reveals itself, grace is the decision to become small again — so that the one who saw can go on living.
Notes: Saumya-vapu (gentle form) — the return to the human shape. The narrative structure mirrors the meditative cycle: expansion to the infinite, then return to the particular. Both movements are necessary. The vision without the return would be madness; the return without the vision would be ignorance.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Now that I see come back, Janardana!
This friendly human frame, my mind can think
Calm thoughts once more; my heart beats still again!
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
Now that I see your friendly human form again, my mind can think clearly. My heart beats calmly. I am restored.
D: Elegant: The familiar face returns, and with it — calm. The mind can think again. The heart beats steady.
D: Distilled: After the overwhelming vision, the return to the ordinary is itself a kind of salvation.
Notes: Arjuna's relief is genuine and human. He does not pretend to sustain the cosmic vision — he admits he needed the return. This honesty is part of what makes Arjuna a good student and a good model: he does not perform spiritual attainment he has not achieved.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Yea! it was wonderful and terrible
To view me as thou didst, dear Prince! The gods
Dread and desire continually to view!
Yet not by Vedas, nor from sacrifice,
Nor penance, nor gift-giving, nor with prayer
Shall any so behold, as thou hast seen!
Only by fullest service, perfect faith,
And uttermost surrender am I known
And seen, and entered into, Indian Prince!
Who doeth all for Me; who findeth Me
In all; adoreth always; loveth all
Which I have made, and Me, for Love's sole end
That man, Arjuna! unto Me doth wend.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
What you saw is something the gods themselves continually desire to see — yet even they do not see it easily. It cannot be reached through scriptures, sacrifice, study, charity, rituals, or severe penance.

Only through undivided devotion — complete faith, full service, utter surrender — can I be known, and seen, and entered into.

The one who does everything for me, who finds me in everything, who worships always, who loves all that I have made, and who loves me for love's own sake — that person comes to me.
D: Elegant: Even the gods long to see what you saw and cannot. Not scripture, not sacrifice, not penance reveals it. Only undivided devotion — doing everything for me, finding me in everything, loving all I have made, and loving me for love alone.
D: Distilled: The ultimate truth is not reached by effort or merit but by love that holds nothing back.
Notes: The chapter's climactic teaching: bhakti (devotion/love) is the supreme path — higher than knowledge (jnana), action (karma), or meditation (dhyana). Mat-karma-krt mat-paramo mad-bhaktah sanga-varjitah, nirvairah sarva-bhutesu yah sa mam eti (who does my work, who is devoted to me, who is free from attachment, who bears enmity to no creature — that one comes to me). This is the Gita's most complete single-sentence description of the ideal person. The framework parallel: wisdom without love is sterile analysis; love without wisdom is sentimental attachment. The highest form integrates both — understanding that serves, knowledge that cares, truth pursued for love's own sake.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
HERE ENDETH CHAPTER XI. OF THE BHAGAVAD-GITA,
Entitled "Viswarupadarsanam,"
Or "The Book of the Manifesting of the One and Manifold."
Notes: Chapter colophon. Viswarupa-darsana Yoga — the Yoga of the Vision of the Universal Form. The most dramatic chapter of the Gita: Arjuna asks to see the truth, receives divine eyes, beholds the cosmic form (beautiful and terrifying), sees all warriors already dead in the mouth of Time, hears Krishna declare 'I am Time, the destroyer,' begs for the familiar form to return, and learns that only devotion — not scripture, ritual, or penance — grants access to the deepest reality. The framework parallel: the progression from intellectual understanding to direct encounter to integration is the arc of genuine education (Paideia). You study, you experience, you return changed — and the return to ordinary life is not a retreat but the beginning of applied wisdom.

D: Original: Chapter 12: The Yoga of Devotion (Bhakti Yoga)

[Arjuna]
Lord! of the men who serve Thee--true in heart--
As God revealed; and of the men who serve,
Worshipping Thee Unrevealed, Unbodied, Far,
Which take the better way of faith and life?
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
Of two kinds of devotees — those who worship you in your revealed form, and those who worship the formless, invisible, unmanifest absolute — which has chosen the better path?
D: Elegant: Which is better: to worship God as a person with a face, or to worship the formless, invisible absolute?
D: Distilled: The essential question of all religion: do you seek the divine through form or beyond form?
Notes: Arjuna asks the question that divides most religious traditions: is it better to worship a personal God (saguna Brahman — with attributes) or the impersonal absolute (nirguna Brahman — without attributes)? This maps to the split between devotional/theistic traditions and philosophical/mystical ones. The framework parallel: is it better to pursue wisdom through concrete practices and institutions (forms) or through pure philosophical inquiry (the formless)?

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Whoever serve Me--as I show Myself--
Constantly true, in full devotion fixed,
Those hold I very holy. But who serve--
Worshipping Me The One, The Invisible,
The Unrevealed, Unnamed, Unthinkable,
Uttermost, All-pervading, Highest, Sure--
Who thus adore Me, mastering their sense,
Of one set mind to all, glad in all good,
These blessed souls come unto Me.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Those who serve me with constant devotion, fixing their hearts on my revealed form with supreme faith — I consider them the best practitioners. But those who worship the formless — the invisible, the unnameable, the unthinkable, the all-pervading, the unchanging — mastering their senses, treating all beings equally, rejoicing in the good of all — they also reach me.
D: Elegant: Both paths arrive. But I hold the devoted heart — fixed on my form with unwavering faith — as the higher way. The formless path reaches me too, but through greater difficulty.
D: Distilled: Both paths reach the same destination — but the path through love is more natural than the path through abstraction.
Notes: Krishna's answer favors the personal, devotional path (bhakti) while affirming the validity of the impersonal path (jnana). The reason: the formless path is harder for embodied beings (Chapter 12 will make this explicit in the next verse). Humans are wired for relationship, not abstraction. The framework parallel: the Academy serves wisdom best through concrete relationships (teacher-student, mentor-apprentice) rather than through abstract systems alone. Forms are necessary even if what they point to is formless.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Yet, hard
The travail is for such as bend their minds
To reach th' Unmanifest That viewless path
Shall scarce be trod by man bearing the flesh!
But whereso any doeth all his deeds
Renouncing self for Me, full of Me, fixed
To serve only the Highest, night and day
Musing on Me--him will I swiftly lift
Forth from life's ocean of distress and death,
Whose soul clings fast to Me. Cling thou to Me!
Clasp Me with heart and mind! so shalt thou dwell
Surely with Me on high. But if thy thought
Droops from such height; if thou be'st weak to set
Body and soul upon Me constantly,
Despair not! give Me lower service! seek
To reach Me, worshipping with steadfast will;
And, if thou canst not worship steadfastly,
Work for Me, toil in works pleasing to Me!
For he that laboureth right for love of Me
Shall finally attain! But, if in this
Thy faint heart fails, bring Me thy failure! find
Refuge in Me! let fruits of labour go,
Renouncing hope for Me, with lowliest heart,
So shalt thou come; for, though to know is more
Than diligence, yet worship better is
Than knowing, and renouncing better still.
Near to renunciation--very near--
Dwelleth Eternal Peace!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The path to the unmanifest is hard — extremely hard for those who bear a body. The formless way is nearly impossible to walk in human flesh.

But whoever dedicates all actions to me, with mind fixed on me, serving only the highest, meditating on me day and night — I will swiftly lift that person out of the ocean of death.

Cling to me. Fix your heart and mind on me. If you cannot do that, then reach for me through steady practice. If even that is too much, then work for me — do what pleases me. If even that fails, then bring me your failure — renounce the fruits of all your labor and find refuge in me with a humble heart.

For knowledge is better than mechanical practice. Meditation is better than mere knowledge. Renunciation of results is better than meditation. And close to renunciation — very close — dwells eternal peace.
D: Elegant: The formless path is nearly impossible in human flesh. But there is a ladder: fix your heart on me. If you cannot — practice. If you cannot practice — work for me. If even that fails — bring me your failure and let go of results. Renunciation of outcomes brings the peace that every other practice is reaching toward.
D: Distilled: There is always a next step, even when the highest step is out of reach — and the willingness to take whatever step you can is itself the path.
Notes: Krishna provides a hierarchy of practice — a ladder of accessibility: (1) Fix heart on me (highest), (2) Practice steadily, (3) Work for me, (4) Renounce results. This is pedagogically brilliant: it meets every seeker where they are. You cannot meditate? Then practice. You cannot practice? Then work. You cannot even work with proper motive? Then just let go of results. Each step is lower than the last, but each is still valid and still moves toward the goal. The framework parallel: Paideia must be accessible at multiple levels. Not everyone can be a philosopher, but everyone can practice. Not everyone can practice, but everyone can work with good intention. The system must accommodate the entire range.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Who hateth nought
Of all which lives, living himself benign,
Compassionate, from arrogance exempt,
Exempt from love of self, unchangeable
By good or ill; patient, contented, firm
In faith, mastering himself, true to his word,
Seeking Me, heart and soul; vowed unto Me,--
That man I love! Who troubleth not his kind,
And is not troubled by them; clear of wrath,
Living too high for gladness, grief, or fear,
That man I love! Who, dwelling quiet-eyed,
Stainless, serene, well-balanced, unperplexed,
Working with Me, yet from all works detached,
That man I love! Who, fixed in faith on Me,
Dotes upon none, scorns none; rejoices not,
And grieves not, letting good or evil hap
Light when it will, and when it will depart,
That man I love! Who, unto friend and foe
Keeping an equal heart, with equal mind
Bears shame and glory; with an equal peace
Takes heat and cold, pleasure and pain; abides
Quit of desires, hears praise or calumny
In passionless restraint, unmoved by each;
Linked by no ties to earth, steadfast in Me,
That man I love! But most of all I love
Those happy ones to whom 'tis life to live
In single fervid faith and love unseeing,
Drinking the blessed Amrit of my Being!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The person I love: one who hates no living thing, who is compassionate and kind, free from arrogance and self-importance, steady through pleasure and pain, patient, content, self-controlled, firm in faith, with mind and heart devoted to me.

I love the one who does not disturb others and is not disturbed by them — who lives above gladness, grief, and fear.

I love the quiet-eyed one — stainless, serene, balanced, unperplexed — who works with me yet remains detached from the work's results.

I love the one who does not dote on anyone or scorn anyone, who neither celebrates when good fortune comes nor grieves when it departs.

I love the one who treats friend and foe with the same heart, who bears honor and shame equally, who takes heat and cold, pleasure and pain without being moved, who has shed all desires, who hears praise and blame with equal calm, who is linked to nothing on earth, who is steadfast in me.

But most of all — I love those for whom it is life itself to live in faith and love, drinking the blessed nectar of my being.
D: Elegant: I love the one who hates nothing, craves nothing, is shaken by nothing — steady through pleasure and pain, friend and foe, praise and blame. Who works without attachment, who lives above fear and grief. But most of all, I love those for whom it is life itself to live in love and faith — drinking the nectar of my being.
D: Distilled: The divine portrait of the beloved: not the one who achieves the most but the one who is shaken by the least — and who loves without condition.
Notes: This passage is the Gita's most complete portrait of the ideal devotee — the bhakta. It parallels the sthitaprajna (person of steady wisdom) described in Chapter 2, but with a crucial addition: love. The Chapter 2 description was stoic and philosophical; this one is warm and devotional. The qualities listed form a composite: non-hatred, compassion, humility, equanimity, contentment, self-mastery, faith, serenity, detachment, impartiality. 'Most of all I love those to whom it is life to live in love' — the highest quality is not any specific virtue but the integration of all virtues into a life animated by love. The framework parallel: the ideal graduate of the Academy would embody exactly this — not just competence or knowledge but the quality of character that makes wisdom livable. Reality + Reason + Right, held together by love.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
HERE ENDETH CHAPTER XII. OF THE BHAGAVAD-GITA,
Entitled "Bhaktiyog,"
Or"The Book of the Religion of Faith."
Notes: Chapter colophon. Bhakti Yoga — the Yoga of Devotion/Faith. The shortest and most personal of the Gita's chapters. It answered: personal devotion is higher than abstract worship (but both arrive). It provided: a ladder of practice accessible to everyone. And it painted: the most complete portrait of the person the divine loves. The framework parallel: Chapter 12 is the Gita's answer to the question 'what does the graduate of Paideia look like?' — not an intellectual but a whole person: compassionate, unshaken, devoted, and animated by love.

D: Original: Chapter 13: The Yoga of the Field and its Knower (Kshetra Kshetrajna Vibhaga Yoga)

[Arjuna]
Now would I hear, O gracious Kesava!
Of Life which seems, and Soul beyond, which sees,
And what it is we know-or think to know.
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
Krishna, I want to understand the difference between the Field and the Knower of the Field — between what we experience and who experiences it. What is matter, and what is the soul that perceives it?
D: Elegant: What is this body — and who is the one inside it, watching? Tell me the difference between the seen and the seer.
D: Distilled: The deepest question is not 'what do I experience?' but 'who is doing the experiencing?'
Notes: Arjuna asks about Kshetra (the Field — body/matter/the experienced) and Kshetrajna (the Knower of the Field — soul/consciousness/the experiencer). This is the subject-object distinction at the root of all epistemology. Kesava is an epithet of Krishna meaning 'one with beautiful hair' or 'slayer of the demon Keshi.' The framework parallel: this maps directly to the Reality pillar — you cannot act rightly without first understanding what is real, and the first step is distinguishing the instrument of perception from what is perceived.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Yea! Son of Kunti! for this flesh ye see
Is Kshetra, is the field where Life disports;
And that which views and knows it is the Soul,
Kshetrajna. In all "fields," thou Indian prince!
I am Kshetrajna. I am what surveys!
Only that knowledge knows which knows the known
By the knower! What it is, that "field" of life,
What qualities it hath, and whence it is,
And why it changeth, and the faculty
That wotteth it, the mightiness of this,
And how it wotteth-hear these things from Me!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
This body is the Field — the ground where life plays out. The one who sees and knows the Field is the Soul, the Knower of the Field. In every field, in every body, I am the Knower. True knowledge is understanding both the Field and its Knower — what it is, what qualities it has, where it comes from, why it changes, and what faculty perceives it. Hear this from me.
D: Elegant: The body is the field. The soul is the one who knows the field. In every body, I am the knower. True knowledge is understanding both the stage and the one who watches the play.
D: Distilled: The body is the field of experience; the soul is the one who experiences — and knowing the difference between them is the beginning of wisdom.
Notes: Kshetra (field/body) and Kshetrajna (knower of the field/soul).
