Krishna makes a radical claim: 'In all fields, I am the Knower' — not merely that each person has an individual soul, but that the consciousness in every being is ultimately divine. This is the Upanishadic doctrine of Atman = Brahman (individual soul = universal soul). The framework parallel: the distinction between the instrument of perception and the perceiver maps to the Reason pillar — reason is the faculty that perceives reality, but reason itself must be understood as distinct from the data it processes.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
.     .     .     .     .     .     .     .     .     .     .     .

The elements, the conscious life, the mind,
The unseen vital force, the nine strange gates
Of the body, and the five domains of sense;
Desire, dislike, pleasure and pain, and thought
Deep-woven, and persistency of being;
These all are wrought on Matter by the Soul!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The Field consists of: the elements, conscious life, the mind, the unseen vital force, the nine gates of the body, the five senses, desire, dislike, pleasure, pain, thought, and the will to persist. All of these are matter — shaped by the Soul but belonging to the material world.
D: Elegant: The Field is everything the body is made of: elements, senses, desires, thoughts, pain, pleasure, and the will to go on living. All of it — matter. None of it — you.
D: Distilled: Everything you experience — sensation, desire, thought, even the drive to keep living — belongs to the body, not to the one who watches from within.
Notes: The 'nine gates' (nava-dvare) are the nine openings of the body: two eyes, two ears, two nostrils, mouth, and two lower openings. The 'five domains of sense' (pancha indriya-gocharah) are sight, sound, smell, taste, and touch. The list deliberately includes thought and desire as part of the Field (matter), not as properties of the soul — a radical claim. Most people identify with their thoughts and desires. The Gita says: those are part of the machinery, not part of you. The framework parallel: Reason requires distinguishing between what you think (which can be distorted) and the capacity that does the thinking (which must be trained to perceive clearly).

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Humbleness, truthfulness, and harmlessness,
Patience and honour, reverence for the wise.
Purity, constancy, control of self,
Contempt of sense-delights, self-sacrifice,
Perception of the certitude of ill
In birth, death, age, disease, suffering, and sin;
Detachment, lightly holding unto home,
Children, and wife, and all that bindeth men;
An ever-tranquil heart in fortunes good
And fortunes evil, with a will set firm
To worship Me--Me only! ceasing not;
Loving all solitudes, and shunning noise
Of foolish crowds; endeavours resolute
To reach perception of the Utmost Soul,
And grace to understand what gain it were
So to attain,--this is true Wisdom, Prince!
And what is otherwise is ignorance!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
True wisdom looks like this: humility, truthfulness, harmlessness, patience, honor, reverence for genuine teachers, purity, constancy, self-control, indifference to sensory pleasures, absence of ego, clear perception of the suffering inherent in birth, death, age, and disease. Detachment from home, family, and possessions. A heart undisturbed by good fortune or bad. Unwavering devotion to me. Love of solitude over the noise of foolish crowds. Persistent effort to perceive the ultimate Soul. And the understanding of what that perception is worth.

This is true wisdom. Everything else is ignorance.
D: Elegant: Wisdom is humility, truthfulness, patience, self-control, detachment, steadiness in fortune and misfortune, devotion, love of solitude, and the relentless pursuit of the Soul. Everything else is ignorance.
D: Distilled: Wisdom is not what you know but how you live — and the test is whether you can remain steady when everything around you shifts.
Notes: This is the Gita's most comprehensive catalog of wisdom-qualities (jnana-lakshana). The list includes both inner dispositions (humility, patience, equanimity) and practical choices (solitude over crowds, self-control over indulgence). 'Perception of the certitude of ill in birth, death, age, disease' — wisdom requires seeing suffering clearly, not as occasional misfortune but as structural to embodied existence. The closing binary — 'this is wisdom; everything else is ignorance' — is deliberately stark. The framework parallel: this list maps closely to the portrait of the sthitaprajna (person of steady wisdom) from Chapter 2 and the beloved devotee from Chapter 12, now expanded to include the full range of cognitive, ethical, and dispositional qualities that constitute wisdom in the FU framework.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Now will I speak of knowledge best to know-
That Truth which giveth man Amrit to drink,
The Truth of HIM, the Para-Brahm, the All,
The Uncreated;; not Asat, not Sat,
Not Form, nor the Unformed; yet both, and more;--
Whose hands are everywhere, and everywhere
Planted His feet, and everywhere His eyes
Beholding, and His ears in every place
Hearing, and all His faces everywhere
Enlightening and encompassing His worlds.
Glorified in the senses He hath given,
Yet beyond sense He is; sustaining all,
Yet dwells He unattached: of forms and modes
Master, yet neither form nor mode hath He;
He is within all beings--and without--
Motionless, yet still moving; not discerned
For subtlety of instant presence; close
To all, to each; yet measurelessly far!
Not manifold, and yet subsisting still
In all which lives; for ever to be known
As the Sustainer, yet, at the End of Times,
He maketh all to end--and re-creates.
The Light of Lights He is, in the heart of the Dark
Shining eternally. Wisdom He is
And Wisdom's way, and Guide of all the wise,
Planted in every heart.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Now I will tell you the highest knowledge — the truth that gives immortality. It is the truth of the Supreme Being, the Absolute — beyond existence and non-existence, beyond form and formlessness, yet encompassing both.

His hands are everywhere. His feet are everywhere. His eyes see in every direction. His ears hear in every place. His faces shine and encompass all worlds. Beyond the senses yet glorified through them. Sustaining all yet attached to nothing. Master of all forms and qualities yet possessing neither form nor quality. Within all beings and outside them. Motionless yet always moving. Too subtle to perceive yet intimately close. Not divided yet appearing as the many.

He sustains all beings. At the end of time, he dissolves them. Then he creates them anew. He is the Light of Lights, shining in the heart of darkness. He is Wisdom itself, the Way of wisdom, and the Guide of the wise — planted in every heart.
D: Elegant: Beyond form and formlessness. Within all beings and outside them. Motionless yet moving. Too subtle to see yet closer than close. He is the Light of Lights shining in the heart of darkness — Wisdom itself, and the Way of wisdom, planted in every heart.
D: Distilled: The divine is not somewhere else — it is the light inside the darkness, the wisdom inside the seeker, planted in every heart before the search begins.
Notes: This passage is one of the Gita's highest theological statements, echoing the Upanishads (especially Isha Upanishad and Mundaka Upanishad). 'Not Sat, not Asat' — beyond existence and non-existence, transcending even the most fundamental categories. 'The Light of Lights in the heart of the Dark' (jyotisham api taj jyotis tamasah param ucyate) — compare with the Gospel of John ('the light shines in the darkness'). The convergence is striking: multiple traditions arrive at the same metaphor for the divine as light within darkness. 'Planted in every heart' — wisdom is not external knowledge to be acquired but an internal capacity to be awakened. The framework parallel: the Fundamental Questions are not imposed from outside but arise naturally from the human condition. The Academy's role is to cultivate what is already there.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
So have I told
Of Life's stuff, and the moulding, and the lore
To comprehend. Whoso, adoring Me,
Perceiveth this, shall surely come to Me!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
So I have told you about the Field, the moulding of matter, and the knowledge needed to comprehend it. Whoever understands this and devotes themselves to me will come to me.
D: Elegant: The field is explained. The knowledge is given. Whoever grasps this and turns toward me — arrives.
D: Distilled: Understanding what you are made of and who is watching from within — that understanding, fully embraced, is the path itself.
Notes: A transitional summary. Krishna has covered the three elements: Kshetra (the Field/matter), Kshetrajna (the Knower), and Jnana (the knowledge that distinguishes them). The promise is direct: comprehension of this distinction, combined with devotion, leads to liberation. The framework parallel: Reality (what is the Field?), Reason (how do we know the Knower?), and Right (what do we do with this understanding?) — the three pillars applied to the deepest metaphysical question.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Know thou that Nature and the Spirit both
Have no beginning! Know that qualities
And changes of them are by Nature wrought;
That Nature puts to work the acting frame,
But Spirit doth inform it, and so cause
Feeling of pain and pleasure. Spirit, linked
To moulded matter, entereth into bond
With qualities by Nature framed, and, thus
Married to matter, breeds the birth again
In good or evil yonis.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Both Nature and Spirit are without beginning. The qualities of nature — and all their changes — are produced by Nature itself. Nature provides the body, the instrument of action. But Spirit animates the body, giving rise to the experience of pleasure and pain.

The Spirit, linked to matter, enters into a bond with nature's qualities. This marriage of spirit and matter determines the kind of birth the soul receives — good or evil, according to the qualities it has cultivated.
D: Elegant: Nature and Spirit are both eternal. Nature builds the body and drives its actions. Spirit enters the body and experiences what the body does. Married to matter, the soul is reborn according to the qualities it has gathered — good or evil, light or dark.
D: Distilled: The soul does not create its circumstances — it enters them, experiences them, and is reborn according to what it cultivated while inside them.
Notes: Prakriti (Nature) and Purusha (Spirit/Soul) — the two eternal principles in Samkhya philosophy. Prakriti is the source of all material forms, qualities, and actions. Purusha is pure consciousness that does not act but experiences. The gunas (qualities — sattva, rajas, tamas) belong to Prakriti, not Purusha. But through association, the soul 'marries' matter and becomes conditioned by the qualities. The mechanism of rebirth: the soul takes on a new body determined by the qualities it cultivated in the previous life. The framework parallel: you are shaped by your environment (the Field) but you are not your environment. The capacity for wisdom (Spirit) is always present, but it can be obscured by the qualities you allow to dominate.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Yet is this
Yea! in its bodily prison!--Spirit pure,
Spirit supreme; surveying, governing,
Guarding, possessing; Lord and Master still
PURUSHA, Ultimate, One Soul with Me.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Yet even within the body's prison, the Spirit remains pure — supreme, sovereign, governing, guarding. This is the Purusha, the Ultimate Soul, one with me.
D: Elegant: Even in the body's prison, the Spirit remains pure — sovereign, untouched, one with me.
D: Distilled: The soul is not contaminated by the body it inhabits — it governs from within, pure and unchanged.
Notes: Purusha (the Supreme Person/Soul) — here identified with Krishna himself. The key claim: even when entangled with matter, the soul's essential nature is not degraded. It remains 'spirit pure, spirit supreme' — surveying, governing, possessing. The prison metaphor is important: the body constrains but does not corrupt the soul. The framework parallel: the capacity for wisdom can be obscured by circumstances, habits, and cultural erosion, but it cannot be destroyed. It persists, waiting to be reawakened.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Whoso thus knows himself, and knows his soul
PURUSHA, working through the qualities
With Nature's modes, the light hath come for him!
Whatever flesh he bears, never again
Shall he take on its load. Some few there be
By meditation find the Soul in Self
Self-schooled; and some by long philosophy
And holy life reach thither; some by works:
Some, never so attaining, hear of light
From other lips, and seize, and cleave to it
Worshipping; yea! and those--to teaching true--
Overpass Death!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Whoever truly knows themselves — knows the Purusha working through the qualities of Nature — that person has found the light. They will never again take on the burden of a body.

Some find the soul through meditation. Some through philosophical inquiry and disciplined living. Some through the path of selfless action. And others, who cannot walk any of these paths themselves, hear the truth from a teacher and hold fast to it — and they too cross beyond death.
D: Elegant: Some find the soul through meditation, some through inquiry, some through selfless work. And some — hearing truth from another — simply hold fast to it and cross beyond death. All paths arrive.
D: Distilled: There is not one path to the truth but many — and even those who can only listen and hold on will arrive.
Notes: Krishna offers four paths to self-knowledge: (1) Dhyana — meditation, (2) Sankhya — philosophical analysis and holy living, (3) Karma Yoga — selfless action, (4) Shruti — hearing from a teacher and holding to it through faith. The fourth is the most accessible: you do not need to meditate, analyze, or even act perfectly — if you listen to someone who has seen the truth and trust what they say, that is enough. The framework parallel: Paideia must accommodate all levels of capacity. The Academy is not only for philosophers. Some people learn through practice, some through study, some through work, and some simply by listening to those who have already understood. All are valid.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Wherever, Indian Prince!
Life is--of moving things, or things unmoved,
Plant or still seed--know, what is there hath grown
By bond of Matter and of Spirit: Know
He sees indeed who sees in all alike
The living, lordly Soul; the Soul Supreme,
Imperishable amid the Perishing:
For, whoso thus beholds, in every place,
In every form, the same, one, Living Life,
Doth no more wrongfulness unto himself,
But goes the highest road which brings to bliss.
Seeing, he sees, indeed, who sees that works
Are Nature's wont, for Soul to practise by
Acting, yet not the agent; sees the mass
Of separate living things--each of its kind--
Issue from One, and blend again to One:
Then hath he BRAHMA, he attains!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Wherever life exists — in moving things or still ones, in plant or seed — know that it has grown from the union of Field and Knower, matter and spirit.

The one who truly sees perceives the same living Soul — supreme and imperishable — dwelling equally in all things that perish. Seeing the same life in all, that person does no wrong to themselves but walks the highest road to bliss.

The one who truly sees recognizes that all separate beings issue from one source and will blend back into one — and in that recognition, attains Brahman.
D: Elegant: Wherever life exists — moving or still — it grew from the union of matter and spirit. See the same Soul in all things that perish, and you walk the highest road. See that all separation issues from One and returns to One — and you have attained.
D: Distilled: The highest perception is seeing one life in all living things — and recognizing that every separation is temporary.
Notes: The vision of unity: the same soul (Purusha) dwells in all beings. 'Seeing equally' (samam pashyan) is the ethical foundation — if the same divine consciousness animates every creature, then harming another is harming yourself. 'Issues from One, blends again to One' — the cycle of manifestation and dissolution. All diversity is temporary; unity is the ground state. The framework parallel: the recognition that all beings share the same fundamental nature — the same capacity for wisdom, the same orientation toward the Fundamental Questions — is the basis for universal ethical concern. This is not sentimentality but metaphysical insight applied to ethics: Reality informing Right.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
O Prince!
That Ultimate, High Spirit, Uncreate,
Unqualified, even when it entereth flesh
Taketh no stain of acts, worketh in nought!
Like to the ethereal air, pervading all,
Which, for sheer subtlety, avoideth taint,
The subtle Soul sits everywhere, unstained:
Like to the light of the all-piercing sun
[Which is not changed by aught it shines upon,]
The Soul's light shineth pure in every place;
And they who, by such eye of wisdom, see
How Matter, and what deals with it, divide;
And how the Spirit and the flesh have strife,
Those wise ones go the way which leads to Life!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The Ultimate Spirit — uncreated, unqualified — even when it enters flesh, takes no stain from action and works in nothing.

Like air, which pervades everything yet avoids contamination because of its subtlety, the Soul sits everywhere, unstained. Like sunlight, which shines on everything without being changed by what it illuminates, the Soul's light shines pure in every place.

Those who see with the eye of wisdom how matter and spirit divide — and how they struggle — those wise ones go the way that leads to life.
D: Elegant: Like air that pervades everything without being stained. Like sunlight that shines on everything without being changed. The Soul sits in every body — pure, unstained, untouched by what it illuminates. Those who see this distinction go the way that leads to life.
D: Distilled: The soul is like sunlight — it illuminates everything it touches without being altered by any of it.
Notes: Two metaphors for the soul's non-attachment: (1) Air (akasha) — all-pervading yet too subtle to be contaminated, (2) Sunlight (surya) — illuminating without being changed by what it shines upon. Both metaphors convey the same principle: the soul's relationship to the body is one of presence without identification. You are in the body but not of the body. The framework parallel: the wise person engages with the world (sunlight shines on everything) without being corrupted by it (the sun is unchanged). This is the same principle as the lotus leaf from Chapter 5 and the ocean from Chapter 2 — engagement without contamination.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
HERE ENDS CHAPTER XIII. OF THE BHAGAVAD-GITA,
Entitled "Kshetrakshetrajnavibhagayog,"
Or "The Book of Religion by Separation of Matter and Spirit."
Notes: Chapter colophon. Kshetra Kshetrajna Vibhaga Yoga — the Yoga of the Distinction between the Field and the Knower of the Field. This chapter established: the body is matter (the Field), the soul is consciousness (the Knower), and God is the Knower in all Fields. True wisdom is the catalog of qualities that enable this distinction. Multiple paths exist to reach this understanding. And the soul, like sunlight, illuminates without being stained. The framework parallel: Chapter 13 is the Gita's most direct statement of the Reality pillar — what is real (the Soul) versus what is temporary (the Field), and why confusing the two is the source of all suffering.

D: Original: Chapter 14: The Yoga of the Three Gunas (Gunatraya Vibhaga Yoga)

[Krishna]
Yet farther will I open unto thee
This wisdom of all wisdoms, uttermost,
The which possessing, all My saints have passed
To perfectness. On such high verities
Reliant, rising into fellowship
With Me, they are not born again at birth
Of Kalpas, nor at Pralyas suffer change!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
I will now reveal to you the highest of all wisdoms — the knowledge by which every saint has passed to perfection. Those who rest upon these truths, rising into union with me, are not born again when the universe is created nor disturbed when it is dissolved.
D: Elegant: The highest wisdom: those who rest upon it are not born again when worlds are made, nor shaken when worlds dissolve.
D: Distilled: The ultimate knowledge is the one that frees you from the cycle entirely — not just from one lifetime but from the rhythm of creation and destruction itself.
Notes: Kalpa (creation cycle) and Pralaya (dissolution) — the cosmic rhythms of manifestation and withdrawal. Krishna promises that this knowledge transcends even cosmic cycles. The framework parallel: wisdom at its highest is not reactive — it does not merely address the current erosion but establishes a foundation that survives the cycle itself.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
This Universe the womb is where I plant
Seed of all lives! Thence, Prince of India, comes
Birth to all beings! Whoso, Kunti's Son!
Mothers each mortal form, Brahma conceives,
And I am He that fathers, sending seed!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The universe is the womb in which I plant the seed of all lives. From this union, every being is born. Nature is the mother of every form. I am the father who plants the seed.
D: Elegant: The universe is the womb. I am the father. Every being ever born emerged from this union of divine seed and cosmic matter.
D: Distilled: All life springs from one act of creation — spirit planted in matter, and matter giving it form.
Notes: The sexual metaphor for creation: Brahman (the Absolute) as father, Prakriti (Nature) as mother, and all beings as their offspring. 'Mahat Brahma' (Great Brahman/Nature) is the womb; Krishna is the bija-pradah (seed-giver). This cosmogony is neither purely material nor purely spiritual — it requires both principles working together. The framework parallel: wisdom (spirit) must be embodied in institutions, practices, and culture (matter) to produce actual results. Neither abstract understanding nor blind practice alone is sufficient.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Sattwan, Rajas, and Tamas, so are named
The qualities of Nature, "Soothfastness,"
"Passion," and "Ignorance." These three bind down
The changeless Spirit in the changeful flesh.
Whereof sweet "Soothfastness," by purity
Living unsullied and enlightened, binds
The sinless Soul to happiness and truth;
And Passion, being kin to appetite,
And breeding impulse and propensity,
Binds the embodied Soul, O Kunti's Son!
By tie of works. But Ignorance, begot
Of Darkness, blinding mortal men, binds down
Their souls to stupor, sloth, and drowsiness.
Yea, Prince of India! Soothfastness binds souls
In pleasant wise to flesh; and Passion binds
By toilsome strain; but Ignorance, which blots
The beams of wisdom, binds the soul to sloth.
Passion and Ignorance, once overcome,
Leave Soothfastness, O Bharata! Where this
With Ignorance are absent, Passion rules;
And Ignorance in hearts not good nor quick.
When at all gateways of the Body shines
The Lamp of Knowledge, then may one see well
Soothfastness settled in that city reigns;
Where longing is, and ardour, and unrest,
Impulse to strive and gain, and avarice,
Those spring from Passion--Prince!--engrained; and where
Darkness and dulness, sloth and stupor are,
'Tis Ignorance hath caused them, Kuru Chief!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Sattva, Rajas, and Tamas — Clarity, Passion, and Inertia — these are the three qualities of Nature. They bind the changeless soul to the changeful body.

Sattva, being pure and luminous, binds the soul through attachment to happiness and truth. Rajas, born of craving and appetite, binds the soul through attachment to action and its results. Tamas, born of darkness, binds the soul through ignorance, laziness, and sleep.

Sattva draws you toward joy. Rajas drives you toward restless striving. Tamas, obscuring wisdom, pulls you toward stupor.

When sattva overcomes the other two, clarity reigns. When rajas dominates, restlessness and craving take over. When tamas prevails, darkness and dullness set in. You can diagnose which quality rules by observing the effects: when the lamp of knowledge shines through every gate of the body, sattva is in command. When longing, ambition, and unrest drive you, rajas is at work. When sloth, confusion, and torpor settle in, tamas has taken hold.
D: Elegant: Three qualities bind the soul to the body: Clarity pulls toward joy. Passion drives toward restless striving. Inertia drags toward stupor. Diagnose which rules you by its effects — when knowledge shines, clarity commands. When ambition burns, passion is at work. When fog descends, inertia has won.
D: Distilled: Every moment, one of three forces is dominant — clarity, craving, or fog — and the quality of your life is determined by which one you feed.
Notes: The Triguna (three qualities/gunas) doctrine is one of Hinduism's most influential psychological frameworks. Sattva (clarity/goodness/light), Rajas (passion/activity/restlessness), Tamas (inertia/darkness/ignorance). All three are properties of Nature (Prakriti), not of the Soul (Purusha). Critically, even sattva binds — it creates attachment to happiness and knowledge, which is still attachment. The Gita's goal is transcendence of all three, not merely maximizing sattva. The framework parallel: this is a diagnostic tool for the erosion pattern. When a civilization is in sattva, its institutions transmit wisdom effectively. When rajas dominates, institutions become vehicles for ambition and power-seeking. When tamas prevails, institutions decay into mere ritual, incompetence, and corruption. The three-guna model provides a vocabulary for diagnosing where any institution sits on the erosion curve.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Moreover, when a soul departeth, fixed
In Soothfastness, it goeth to the place--
Perfect and pure--of those that know all Truth.
If it departeth in set habitude
Of Impulse, it shall pass into the world
Of spirits tied to works; and, if it dies
In hardened Ignorance, that blinded soul
Is born anew in some unlighted womb.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The soul that departs while dominated by sattva goes to the pure realms of those who know truth. The soul that departs while driven by rajas is reborn among those bound to restless work. The soul that dies in tamas is reborn in ignorance and darkness.
D: Elegant: Die in clarity — rise to truth. Die in passion — return to restless work. Die in darkness — sink deeper into darkness. The quality dominant at the end determines what comes next.
D: Distilled: What dominates your mind at the moment of death determines where you go — and what dominates at death is what you practiced in life.
Notes: The doctrine of antya-kala (the moment of death) as determinative of the next birth. This is not arbitrary: the quality dominant at death is the quality most practiced during life. You die as you lived. The framework parallel: institutions, too, die as they lived. A university that spent its final decades pursuing prestige and funding (rajas) does not suddenly produce wisdom at the end. An institution that maintained its orientation toward genuine understanding (sattva) leaves a legacy that endures.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
The fruit of Soothfastness is true and sweet;
The fruit of lusts is pain and toil; the fruit
Of Ignorance is deeper darkness. Yea!
For Light brings light, and Passion ache to have;
And gloom, bewilderments, and ignorance
Grow forth from Ignorance. Those of the first
Rise ever higher; those of the second mode
Take a mid place; the darkened souls sink back
To lower deeps, loaded with witlessness!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The fruit of sattva is true and sweet. The fruit of rajas is pain and toil. The fruit of tamas is deeper darkness.

From sattva comes light. From rajas comes the ache of wanting. From tamas come bewilderment and ignorance.

Those dominated by sattva rise ever higher. Those ruled by rajas remain in the middle. Those sunk in tamas fall to lower depths, weighed down by witlessness.
D: Elegant: Clarity bears sweetness. Passion bears pain. Darkness bears deeper darkness. Those who live in light rise. Those who live in craving remain. Those who live in fog descend.
D: Distilled: Each quality produces its own fruit — clarity yields understanding, passion yields exhaustion, and darkness yields more darkness.
Notes: The self-reinforcing nature of the gunas: sattva produces conditions that strengthen sattva; tamas produces conditions that deepen tamas. This is a positive/negative feedback loop. The framework parallel: institutional erosion is self-reinforcing. Once tamas (incompetence, corruption, laziness) gains a foothold, it produces the conditions that make further erosion likely. Conversely, genuine wisdom (sattva) creates the conditions for more wisdom — but only if actively maintained. The middle position (rajas) is unstable: it can go either way.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
When, watching life, the living man perceives
The only actors are the Qualities,
And knows what rules beyond the Qualities,
Then is he come nigh unto Me!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
When a person watches life and perceives that the only actors are the three Qualities — and knows what lies beyond them — that person draws near to me.
D: Elegant: When you see that the Qualities are the only actors — and you are not any of them — you are close to me.
D: Distilled: Freedom begins when you realize that your thoughts, drives, and moods are not you but forces moving through you.
Notes: The liberation insight: the gunas act, not the soul. When you identify with the gunas ('I am angry,' 'I am lazy,' 'I am wise'), you are bound. When you see the gunas as forces operating within the Field while you (the Knower) merely observe, you are free. This is the observer stance — the capacity to witness your own mental states without identifying with them. The framework parallel: Reason at its highest is meta-cognitive — the ability to observe your own thinking, recognize when it is distorted by passion (rajas) or fog (tamas), and correct for it.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
The Soul,
Thus passing forth from the Three Qualities--
Whereby arise all bodies--overcomes
Birth, Death, Sorrow, and Age; and drinketh deep
The undying wine of Amrit.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The soul that passes beyond the three Qualities — the forces that give rise to all bodies — overcomes birth, death, sorrow, and age, and drinks the wine of immortality.
D: Elegant: Beyond the three Qualities — beyond clarity, passion, and darkness — lies freedom from birth, death, sorrow, and age. That is immortality.
D: Distilled: Liberation is not choosing the best quality but transcending all three — rising above the entire system that binds you.
Notes: Amrita (nectar of immortality) — the state beyond the gunas. The key teaching: the goal is not to maximize sattva (though sattva is better than rajas or tamas) but to transcend all three. Even goodness, if clung to, is a chain. The framework parallel: the goal is not to win the culture war (rajas) or to retreat from it (tamas) or even to be serenely right about it (sattva) — but to operate from a place that transcends the entire dynamic.

D: Original:
[Arjuna]
Oh, my Lord!
Which be the signs to know him that hath gone
Past the Three Modes? How liveth he? What way
Leadeth him safe beyond the threefold Modes?
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
What are the signs of a person who has gone beyond the three Qualities? How do they live? How do they pass safely beyond them?
D: Elegant: If someone has truly transcended the Qualities — what do they look like? How do they live? How did they get there?
D: Distilled: The student asks the essential practical question: what does transcendence actually look like in a human life?
Notes: Arjuna asks the same kind of question he asked in Chapter 2 (what does the sthitaprajna look like?) and in Chapter 12 (what does the ideal devotee look like?). This is a recurring pedagogical pattern: the student demands that abstract teaching be made concrete and observable. The framework parallel: any wisdom system must be able to describe what its graduate looks like — not just in theory but in observable behavior.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
He who with equanimity surveys
Lustre of goodness, strife of passion, sloth
Of ignorance, not angry if they are,
Not wishful when they are not: he who sits
A sojourner and stranger in their midst
Unruffled, standing off, saying--serene--
When troubles break, "These be the Qualities!"
He unto whom--self-centred--grief and joy
Sound as one word; to whose deep-seeing eyes
The clod, the marble, and the gold are one;
Whose equal heart holds the same gentleness
For lovely and unlovely things, firm-set,
Well-pleased in praise and dispraise; satisfied
With honour or dishonour; unto friends
And unto foes alike in tolerance;
Detached from undertakings,--he is named
Surmounter of the Qualities!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The person who has transcended the Qualities surveys the lustre of goodness, the striving of passion, and the fog of ignorance with equal calm — not wishing for any when they are absent, not resenting any when they are present.

They sit like a guest in the house of their own body — unruffled, standing apart, saying serenely when troubles come: 'These are just the Qualities at work.'

To such a person, grief and joy sound the same. Clay, marble, and gold are equal. Praise and blame fall alike. Honor and dishonor carry the same weight. Friends and enemies receive the same treatment. They undertake nothing from self-interest.

Such a person is called a Surmounter of the Qualities.
D: Elegant: Clay, marble, and gold are the same to this one. Praise and blame fall alike. Friends and enemies receive equal regard. When trouble comes, they say simply: 'The Qualities are at work.' Unruffled. Standing apart. A guest in the house of their own body.
D: Distilled: The one who has transcended does not suppress the forces of clarity, passion, and darkness — they simply stop being moved by any of them.
Notes: The portrait of the gunatita (one who has transcended the gunas) parallels the sthitaprajna of Chapter 2 and the ideal devotee of Chapter 12. The specific addition here: 'These are just the Qualities' — the transcended person has a meta-cognitive frame that allows them to name what is happening without being captured by it. 'A sojourner and stranger' (udasina-vad asinah) — the soul as temporary guest in the body, not as permanent resident. The equanimity toward clay/marble/gold is not indifference but freedom from reactive valuation. The framework parallel: this is the mature form of Reason — the capacity to observe your own states, name the forces at work, and choose your response rather than being driven by automatic reactions.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
And such--
With single, fervent faith adoring Me,
Passing beyond the Qualities, conforms
To Brahma, and attains Me!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
And the person who, with single fervent faith, worships me — passing beyond the Qualities — conforms to Brahman and attains me.
D: Elegant: Worship me with undivided faith, pass beyond the Qualities, and you conform to the Absolute — you attain me.
D: Distilled: Transcendence of the three forces is not achieved by willpower alone but by devotion to what lies beyond all three.
Notes: The method of transcendence: not analytical detachment alone (Sankhya) but devotion (bhakti) directed at the divine, which is itself beyond the gunas. The teaching is consistent: multiple paths lead to the same destination, but devotion provides the energy that analytical understanding alone may lack. The framework parallel: understanding the erosion pattern intellectually is not enough. The 'spirit' component — the passionate commitment to wisdom — is what enables someone to actually transcend the forces that pull everyone else down.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
For I am
That whereof Brahma is the likeness! Mine
The Amrit is; and Immortality
Is mine; and mine perfect Felicity!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
For I am the foundation of which Brahman is the likeness. The nectar of immortality is mine. Perfect happiness is mine. Eternal righteousness is mine.
D: Elegant: I am the ground on which the Absolute rests. Immortality, perfect happiness, and eternal righteousness — these are mine.
D: Distilled: The source of all that is absolute, immortal, and righteous is not an abstraction — it is a person who can be known.
Notes: Krishna identifies himself as the foundation even of Brahman (the Absolute). This is the Gita's highest theological claim: the personal God (Krishna/Vishnu) is not merely an aspect of the impersonal Absolute but its source. Amrita (immortality), shashvatasya dharmasya (eternal righteousness), aikantikasya sukhasya (absolute happiness) — the three attributes of the divine ground. The framework parallel: the Fundamental Questions point toward something that is simultaneously personal (it can be related to) and absolute (it underlies everything). This is not a contradiction but the nature of the deepest reality.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
HERE ENDS CHAPTER XIV. OF THE BHAGAVAD-GITA
Entitled "Gunatrayavibhagayog,"
Or "The Book of Religion by Separation from the Qualities."
Notes: Chapter colophon. Gunatraya Vibhaga Yoga — the Yoga of the Division of the Three Qualities. This chapter introduced the three gunas as the diagnostic framework for understanding human psychology and spiritual progress: sattva (clarity), rajas (passion), tamas (inertia). The goal is not to maximize sattva but to transcend all three. The method is devotion to what lies beyond the qualities. The framework parallel: the guna model is one of the most useful diagnostic tools in the FU framework — it provides a vocabulary for understanding why institutions, individuals, and cultures rise (sattva dominant), plateau (rajas dominant), or decay (tamas dominant).

D: Original: Chapter 15: The Yoga of the Supreme Person (Purushottama Yoga)

[Krishna]
Men call the Aswattha,--the Banyan-tree,--
Which hath its boughs beneath, its roots above,--
The ever-holy tree. Yea! for its leaves
Are green and waving hymns which whisper Truth!
Who knows the Aswattha, knows Veds, and all.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
There is a sacred tree — the Ashvattha, the cosmic banyan — with roots above and branches below. Its leaves are the hymns of the Vedas. Whoever understands this tree understands all that the scriptures contain.
D: Elegant: There is a tree with roots in heaven and branches reaching down into the world. Its leaves are the sacred hymns. To understand this tree is to understand everything the scriptures teach.
D: Distilled: The cosmos is an inverted tree — rooted in the divine, branching down into the material world — and understanding it is understanding everything.
Notes: The Ashvattha (sacred fig/banyan tree) with roots above and branches below — one of the Gita's most powerful metaphors. The image appears in the Katha Upanishad (2.3.1): 'This eternal Ashvattha has its root above and branches below.' The inversion is deliberate: the source (roots) is in the transcendent realm; the manifestations (branches) extend downward into the material world. The Vedas are the leaves — the doctrines and teachings that make the tree visible. The framework parallel: the visible world (institutions, cultures, individuals) is the branching; the roots are in the Fundamental Questions. Understanding the tree means tracing the branches back to their roots — from surface phenomena to underlying principles.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Its branches shoot to heaven and sink to earth,
Even as the deeds of men, which take their birth
From qualities: its silver sprays and blooms,
And all the eager verdure of its girth,
Leap to quick life at kiss of sun and air,
As men's lives quicken to the temptings fair
Of wooing sense: its hanging rootlets seek
The soil beneath, helping to hold it there,
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Its branches shoot upward to heaven and downward to earth, as human deeds — born of the qualities — shoot in every direction. Its sprays and blooms leap to life at the touch of sun and air, as human lives quicken at the touch of sensory temptation. Its hanging roots reach down into the soil, as actions performed in the human world —
D: Elegant: Branches reaching everywhere — upward to heaven, downward to earth — driven by the three qualities. Blooms quickening at the touch of sense. Roots reaching deeper into soil with every action —
D: Distilled: The cosmic tree grows in every direction at once — fed by desire, rooted by action, branching through every quality of human nature.
Notes: The tree metaphor extended: the branches (karmic actions) grow in all directions based on the gunas. The 'sprays and blooms' are sensory pleasures that attract and entangle. The 'hanging roots' are secondary attachments formed by actions — each deed creates new roots that bind you more deeply to the material world. The framework parallel: every action performed from attachment creates new conditions that demand further action — an expanding web of consequences. This is the mechanism of institutional inertia: each decision made from self-interest creates dependencies that make future self-interested decisions more likely.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
As actions wrought amid this world of men
Bind them by ever-tightening bonds again.
If ye knew well the teaching of the Tree,
What its shape saith; and whence it springs; and, then
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
— bind the doer with ever-tightening bonds. If you could truly understand this tree — its shape, its origin, its growth —
D: Elegant: Every action tightens the bonds. If you understood the tree — truly understood it —
D: Distilled: Actions performed from attachment do not free you — they bind you more tightly with every repetition.
Notes: The binding mechanism: karma as ever-tightening bonds (karma-anubandhini). The more you act from desire, the more entangled you become. The tree metaphor shows why: each action-root creates new branches, which create new roots, in an endlessly expanding cycle. Understanding the tree is the first step toward escape.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
How it must end, and all the ills of it,
The axe of sharp Detachment ye would whet,
And cleave the clinging snaky roots, and lay
This Aswattha of sense-life low,--to set
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
— and how it must end, and all the suffering it produces — then you would take up the axe of sharp Detachment and cut through these clinging, serpentine roots, felling this tree of sensory entanglement —
D: Elegant: — you would take up the axe of Detachment and cut through the clinging roots, felling the whole tree —
D: Distilled: The instrument that fells the tree of entanglement is not knowledge alone but the sharp edge of detachment — knowing and letting go.
Notes: Asanga-shastrena dridhena chhittva (cut with the strong weapon of detachment) — detachment (vairagya) is the axe. Understanding the tree is necessary but insufficient; you must also cut. Knowledge without detachment is like seeing the trap without stepping out of it. The 'clinging snaky roots' captures the way attachments feel: organic, alive, wrapping around you with a strength disproportionate to their size.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
New growths upspringing to that happier sky,--
Which they who reach shall have no day to die,
Nor fade away, nor fall--to Him, I mean,
FATHER and FIRST, Who made the mystery
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
— so that new growth could spring upward toward a happier realm, where those who arrive never die, never fade, never fall. That realm belongs to the First, the Father, the source of all creation.
D: Elegant: Cut the tree — and new growth springs upward toward a realm where nothing dies, nothing fades, nothing falls.
D: Distilled: Destroying the tree of entanglement is not destruction — it is clearing the ground for something that cannot die.
Notes: Felling the Ashvattha is not nihilistic destruction but liberating cultivation — removing what binds so that what is eternal can flourish. 'That happier sky' is the realm of Brahman/Krishna, beyond the cycles of birth and death. The framework parallel: dismantling corrupted institutions is not destructive when it clears space for genuine wisdom to take root. The erosion pattern cannot be reversed by patching the existing tree — sometimes you must cut and replant.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Of old Creation; for to Him come they
From passion and from dreams who break away;
Who part the bonds constraining them to flesh,
And,--Him, the Highest, worshipping alway--
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
To that original source come those who have broken free from passion and delusion, who have cut the bonds that tie them to flesh —
D: Elegant: To the source return those who break free from passion and delusion — who cut the bonds tying spirit to flesh —
D: Distilled: Liberation is returning to the source from which you came — by releasing what held you elsewhere.
Notes: The path of return: from the branches (manifestation) back to the roots (source). The bonds that must be cut are not physical but psychological — passion (raga), delusion (moha), and attachment to the body (deha-abhimana).

D: Original:
[Krishna]
No longer grow at mercy of what breeze
Of summer pleasure stirs the sleeping trees,
What blast of tempest tears them, bough and stem
To the eternal world pass such as these!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
— no longer swayed by whatever breeze of pleasure stirs the sleeping branches, or whatever storm tears at them. They have passed beyond the tree entirely, into the eternal world.
D: Elegant: No breeze of pleasure stirs them. No storm of pain tears at them. They have passed beyond the tree entirely.
D: Distilled: The liberated soul is not a tree in calm weather — it is no longer a tree at all.
Notes: The final stage: not merely stillness within the tree (equanimity amid the gunas) but transcendence of the tree itself. The breeze/storm metaphor captures the spectrum of worldly experience — from gentle pleasures to violent crises — and the liberated soul is beyond both.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Another Sun gleams there! another Moon!
Another Light,--not Dusk, nor Dawn, nor Noon--
Which they who once behold return no more;
They have attained My rest, life's Utmost boon!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
In that realm, another sun shines — another moon — another light entirely, neither dusk, nor dawn, nor noon. Those who behold it never return. They have reached my resting place — life's highest gift.
D: Elegant: Another sun. Another moon. Another light — not dusk, not dawn, not noon. Those who see it never come back. They have arrived.
D: Distilled: The final destination is lit by a light that owes nothing to any sun — and those who find it have nowhere left to go.
Notes: Na tad bhasayate suryo na shashanko na pavakah (Neither sun, nor moon, nor fire illumines that place) — a verse echoed in the Katha Upanishad (2.2.15) and Mundaka Upanishad (2.2.10). The light of Brahman is self-luminous — it does not depend on external sources. 'Return no more' — the finality of moksha (liberation). The framework parallel: the ultimate goal is not a better position within the cycle but escape from the cycle itself — not a better civilization but an understanding that transcends civilizational rise and fall.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
When, in this world of manifested life,
The undying Spirit, setting forth from Me,
Taketh on form, it draweth to itself
From Being's storehouse,--which containeth all,--
Senses and intellect. The Sovereign Soul
Thus entering the flesh, or quitting it,
Gathers these up, as the wind gathers scents,
Blowing above the flower-beds. Ear and Eye,
And Touch and Taste, and Smelling, these it takes,--
Yea, and a sentient mind;--linking itself
To sense-things so.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
When the undying Spirit enters the world of manifest life, it draws to itself from the storehouse of existence the senses and the mind — just as the wind gathers scents as it blows over flower beds.

Taking up ear, eye, touch, taste, smell, and the sentient mind, the soul links itself to sense-objects and begins its embodied life.
D: Elegant: The soul enters the body the way wind passes over flowers — gathering senses and mind as the wind gathers scent. Ear, eye, touch, taste, smell, and the thinking mind: these are what it picks up on the way in.
D: Distilled: The soul does not create the senses — it gathers them, the way wind gathers fragrance, and carries them from one life to the next.
Notes: The wind-and-flowers metaphor (gandhaan vaayur ivashayat) for how the soul acquires its sensory apparatus: not by manufacturing it but by collecting it from the 'storehouse of existence.' The soul carries its accumulated senses and mind from body to body like a wind carrying scent. This implies that the senses and mind are not intrinsic to the soul but acquired instruments — tools the soul picks up and puts down across lifetimes.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
The unenlightened ones
Mark not that Spirit when he goes or comes,
Nor when he takes his pleasure in the form,
Conjoined with qualities; but those see plain
Who have the eyes to see. Holy souls see
Which strive thereto. Enlightened, they perceive
That Spirit in themselves; but foolish ones,
Even though they strive, discern not, having hearts
Unkindled, ill-informed!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The unenlightened do not perceive the Spirit when it enters or leaves a body, or when it takes pleasure in its bodily experience, joined with the qualities. But those with the eyes of wisdom see clearly.

Disciplined seekers perceive the Spirit within themselves. But the foolish — even if they try — cannot see it, because their hearts are untrained and unlit.
D: Elegant: The unenlightened do not see the Spirit come or go. The wise see plainly. The foolish, even striving, cannot see — their hearts are unlit.
D: Distilled: The soul is always present — the difference between the wise and the foolish is not what is there but what they can perceive.
Notes: Jnana-chakshush (eyes of wisdom) — perception of the soul requires a faculty that must be developed, not merely biological sight. The distinction between 'those who strive' (yatanto yoginaḥ) and 'those who strive but fail' (yatanto 'py akṛtātmānaḥ) is important: effort alone is not enough. The heart must be 'kindled' (kṛtātmānaḥ) — prepared through discipline and purification. The framework parallel: wisdom is not acquired by effort alone but by effort applied with the right orientation. An untrained mind can work very hard and still not see clearly.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Know, too, from Me
Shineth the gathered glory of the suns
Which lighten all the world: from Me the moons
Draw silvery beams, and fire fierce loveliness.
I penetrate the clay, and lend all shapes
Their living force; I glide into the plant--
Root, leaf, and bloom--to make the woodlands green
With springing sap. Becoming vital warmth,
I glow in glad, respiring frames, and pass,
With outward and with inward breath, to feed
The body by all meats.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Know that the gathered glory of all suns that light the world shines from me. The silver of the moon and the fierce beauty of fire — all from me. I penetrate the earth and sustain all living things with my energy. I enter the plant as sap, making woodlands green with life. I become the vital warmth in breathing bodies, working with inward and outward breath to digest all food and sustain all life.
D: Elegant: The light of every sun, the silver of the moon, the force of fire — all from me. I enter the earth as energy. I rise in the plant as sap. I burn in the body as warmth. Every breath, every digestion, every living process — me.
D: Distilled: The divine is not above the world but within it — the sap in the plant, the warmth in the body, the light in every sun.
Notes: Krishna as Vaishvanara (the cosmic fire of digestion and sustenance). This is the immanent aspect of the divine: not a distant creator but the active force within every natural process — photosynthesis, metabolism, respiration. The Gita here anticipates what modern science describes as energy flowing through all living systems, but frames it theologically: the energy is not impersonal but divine. The framework parallel: the capacity for wisdom is not imposed from outside but is already operating within — like sap rising in a tree. The Academy's job is not to inject wisdom but to cultivate what is already growing.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
For in this world
Being is twofold: the Divided, one;
The Undivided, one. All things that live
Are "the Divided." That which sits apart,
"The Undivided."
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Being in this world is twofold: the Divided and the Undivided. All individual living things are the Divided. That which sits apart, unchanging, is the Undivided.
D: Elegant: Two kinds of being: the Divided — all individual lives — and the Undivided, which sits apart, unchanging.
D: Distilled: Everything that changes belongs to the Divided. What does not change belongs to the Undivided — and both are real.
Notes: Kshara (perishable/divided) and Akshara (imperishable/undivided) — two categories of being. All individual manifestations (plants, animals, humans, gods) are kshara: subject to change and death. The akshara is the unchanging principle behind all manifestation. The framework parallel: the erosion pattern operates on the kshara level — institutions, cultures, and teachings change and decay. But the fundamental truths they point toward (the Fundamental Questions, the capacity for wisdom) belong to the akshara level — they persist even when their embodiments do not.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Higher still is He,
The Highest, holding all, whose Name is LORD,
The Eternal, Sovereign, First! Who fills all worlds,
Sustaining them. And--dwelling thus beyond
Divided Being and Undivided--I
Am called of men and Vedas, Life Supreme,
The PURUSHOTTAMA.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
But higher still is the Supreme Person — the Highest — who fills all worlds, sustaining them. Dwelling beyond both the Divided and the Undivided, I am called by scripture and humanity alike the Supreme Soul: Purushottama.
D: Elegant: Higher than the changing. Higher than the unchanging. The Supreme Person sustains both — and is called Purushottama, the Highest Soul.
D: Distilled: Above everything that changes and above the unchanging principle itself stands the Supreme Person — source of both.
Notes: Purushottama (Supreme Person) — the chapter's title concept. Krishna identifies himself as higher than both kshara (the changing world) and akshara (the unchanging principle). This is a threefold ontology: (1) the changing world, (2) the unchanging ground, (3) the Supreme Person who transcends and sustains both. This resolves a tension in Indian philosophy between impersonal Brahman and personal God: the personal God (Purushottama) is not lower than the impersonal Absolute but its source. The framework parallel: the Fundamental Questions are unanswerable not because they are poorly formed but because they point toward something that transcends the categories of answered/unanswered. Purushottama is the Gita's name for what the framework calls 'the productive unanswerable.'

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Who knows Me thus,
With mind unclouded, knoweth all, dear Prince!
And with his whole soul ever worshippeth Me.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Whoever knows me thus — with mind unclouded — knows everything, and worships me with their whole soul.
D: Elegant: Know me as Purushottama, and you know everything. Worship follows naturally from that knowing.
D: Distilled: To know the highest is to know everything — because everything else is a manifestation of it.
Notes: Sarva-vid (knower of all) — the claim that knowing the Purushottama is equivalent to omniscience, not in the sense of knowing every fact, but in the sense of knowing the principle from which every fact derives. Compare with Aristotle's concept of first principles (archai): if you know the foundation, you understand the structure.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Now is the sacred, secret Mystery
Declared to thee! Who comprehendeth this
Hath wisdom! He is quit of works in bliss!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
This is the sacred, secret mystery I have declared to you. Whoever comprehends it possesses wisdom, has completed all that needs to be done, and rests in bliss.
D: Elegant: The sacred mystery is now declared. Whoever comprehends it has wisdom, has done all that needs doing, and is at rest.
D: Distilled: The final teaching is not an addition to what came before but the realization that understanding what came before was the entire task.
Notes: Guhyatamam (most secret) — the superlative form, indicating this is the deepest teaching of the chapter. 'He is quit of works in bliss' (kritakritya) — the person who understands Purushottama has no remaining obligations, not because they refuse to act but because their understanding has resolved all the tensions that made action feel burdensome. The framework parallel: the person who genuinely understands the relationship between Reality, Reason, and Right does not need external rules to guide their behavior — the understanding itself produces right action naturally.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
HERE ENDS CHAPTER XV. OF THE BHAGAVAD-GITA
Entitled "Purushottamapraptiyog,"
Or "The Book of Religion by attaining the Supreme."
Notes: Chapter colophon. Purushottama Yoga — the Yoga of the Supreme Person. This chapter taught: the cosmos is an inverted tree rooted in the divine and branching into matter. Cut it with detachment. Being is threefold: the changing, the unchanging, and the Supreme Person who transcends both. The divine is not distant but immanent — the sap in every plant, the warmth in every body, the light of every sun. The framework parallel: Chapter 15 is the Gita's cosmology chapter, establishing the relationship between the visible world (the tree), its principles (kshara/akshara), and the ultimate source (Purushottama). The FU framework's three-level structure — surface phenomena, underlying patterns, Fundamental Questions — mirrors this threefold ontology.

D: Original: Chapter 16: The Yoga of Divine and Demonic Natures (Daivasura Sampad Vibhaga Yoga)

[Krishna]
Fearlessness, singleness of soul, the will
Always to strive for wisdom; opened hand
And governed appetites; and piety,
And love of lonely study; humbleness,
Uprightness, heed to injure nought which lives,
Truthfulness, slowness unto wrath, a mind
That lightly letteth go what others prize;
And equanimity, and charity
Which spieth no man's faults; and tenderness
Towards all that suffer; a contented heart,
Fluttered by no desires; a bearing mild,
Modest, and grave, with manhood nobly mixed,
With patience, fortitude, and purity;
An unrevengeful spirit, never given
To rate itself too high;--such be the signs,
O Indian Prince! of him whose feet are set
On that fair path which leads to heavenly birth!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
These are the signs of divine nature: fearlessness, singleness of purpose, the constant pursuit of wisdom. An open hand and a governed appetite. Piety, love of study, humility. Uprightness, care not to harm any living thing. Truthfulness, slowness to anger, a mind that releases easily what others cling to. Equanimity, compassion that does not search for faults, tenderness toward all who suffer. A contented heart, undisturbed by craving. A bearing both gentle and strong — modest, grave, patient, brave, and pure. An unrevengeful spirit that never overestimates itself.

These are the marks of one born for the higher path.
D: Elegant: Fearlessness. Singleness of purpose. The will to pursue wisdom. Open hands, governed appetites, tenderness toward all that suffers. Patience, purity, humility, and a spirit that never overestimates itself. These are the signs of divine nature.
D: Distilled: Divine nature is not power or perfection but the cluster of qualities that makes a person trustworthy: courage, humility, honesty, compassion, and self-control.
Notes: Daivi sampad (divine qualities) — a comprehensive ethical catalog comparable to Aristotle's list of virtues in the Nicomachean Ethics. Key parallels: fearlessness (andreia/courage), truthfulness (aletheia), humility (praotes/modesty), self-control (sophrosyne/temperance). The list includes both active virtues (fearlessness, generosity) and receptive virtues (patience, contentment, humility). 'Never given to rate itself too high' (anahankarah) — the absence of ego is listed last, as a kind of capstone: all the other virtues are corrupted if ego enters. The framework parallel: this is the Gita's portrait of what the Academy's graduate looks like — not a specialist in any single virtue but a person whose entire character reflects the orientation toward wisdom. Compare with the virtues of the sthitaprajna (Ch. 2), the ideal devotee (Ch. 12), and the jnana qualities (Ch. 13).

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Deceitfulness, and arrogance, and pride,
Quickness to anger, harsh and evil speech,
And ignorance, to its own darkness blind,--
These be the signs, My Prince! of him whose birth
Is fated for the regions of the vile.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
And these are the signs of demonic nature: deceitfulness, arrogance, pride, quickness to anger, harsh and cruel speech, and an ignorance so deep it cannot see its own darkness.

These are the marks of one born for the lower path.
D: Elegant: Deceit, arrogance, pride, anger, cruelty, and an ignorance so deep it cannot see itself — these mark the demonic nature.
D: Distilled: The worst quality is not any single vice but the blindness that prevents you from seeing your own vices.
Notes: Asuri sampad (demonic qualities). The list is shorter than the divine list — a deliberate asymmetry suggesting that virtue is complex and varied while vice is relatively simple. The concluding quality — 'ignorance, to its own darkness blind' (ajnanam cabhijatasya) — is the most dangerous: self-blind ignorance cannot be corrected because it does not know it needs correction. The framework parallel: the erosion pattern is most advanced when the eroding institution cannot perceive its own erosion. A university that has ceased to pursue truth but believes itself to be the arbiter of truth is in the tamas phase — darkened and unable to see the darkness.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
The Heavenly Birth brings to deliverance,
So should'st thou know! The birth with Asuras
Brings into bondage. Be thou joyous, Prince!
Whose lot is set apart for heavenly Birth.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Divine nature leads to liberation. Demonic nature leads to bondage. But do not fear, Arjuna — your nature is divine.
D: Elegant: Divine nature frees. Demonic nature binds. Do not worry, Arjuna — yours is the divine.
D: Distilled: The reassurance every seeker needs: if you are asking which nature is yours, you already have the answer.
Notes: Krishna's reassurance to Arjuna is both personal and pedagogically important: the very fact that Arjuna is troubled by the question of right action (Chapter 1), that he asked to be taught (Chapter 2), and that he has listened with genuine engagement through twelve chapters — all indicate divine nature. The demonic nature would not be asking these questions. The framework parallel: the person who worries about whether they are pursuing wisdom genuinely is almost certainly doing so. Genuine corruption does not question itself.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Two stamps there are marked on all living men,
Divine and Undivine; I spake to thee
By what marks thou shouldst know the Heavenly Man,
Hear from me now of the Unheavenly!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Two stamps are marked on all living beings — divine and undivine. I have described the divine nature. Now hear me describe the undivine.
D: Elegant: Two stamps on every soul — divine and demonic. The divine is described. Now hear the demonic.
D: Distilled: Every person carries both possibilities — the question is which one they feed.
Notes: The binary is not between two types of people but between two tendencies present in all people. The Gita does not teach that some souls are inherently demonic — it teaches that demonic qualities can dominate any soul that does not cultivate divine ones. The framework parallel: the erosion pattern is not about good people vs. bad people but about which tendencies are cultivated and reinforced by institutions, education, and culture.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
They comprehend not, the Unheavenly,
How Souls go forth from Me; nor how they come
Back unto Me: nor is there Truth in these,
Nor purity, nor rule of Life. "This world
Hath not a Law, nor Order, nor a Lord,"
So say they: "nor hath risen up by Cause
Following on Cause, in perfect purposing,
But is none other than a House of Lust."
And, this thing thinking, all those ruined ones--
Of little wit, dark-minded--give themselves
To evil deeds, the curses of their kind.
Surrendered to desires insatiable,
Full of deceitfulness, folly, and pride,
In blindness cleaving to their errors, caught
Into the sinful course, they trust this lie
As it were true--this lie which leads to death--
Finding in Pleasure all the good which is,
And crying "Here it finisheth!"
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The undivine do not understand how souls come from me or return to me. They lack truth, purity, and right conduct.

They say: 'This world has no law, no order, no lord. It arose by accident, not design. There is no purpose — only desire.'

Holding this belief, these ruined souls — small-minded and dark — give themselves to destructive acts, becoming a curse upon the world. Surrendered to insatiable desires, full of deceit, pride, and folly, they cling blindly to their errors and follow a path that leads to death — believing pleasure is the only good, and crying 'This is all there is!'
D: Elegant: 'No law, no order, no lord,' they say. 'Only desire.' Holding this creed, they become what they believe — insatiable, deceitful, and destructive, following pleasure to death, crying: 'This is all there is!'
D: Distilled: The demonic worldview is not a theology — it is the conviction that nothing matters beyond desire, and it destroys everyone who holds it.
Notes: The asura (demonic) worldview is described in philosophical terms: no ishvara (lord/purpose), no satya (truth), no niyama (moral order), no causation — the world is random and driven only by kama (desire). This is not atheism per se but nihilistic hedonism — the belief that pleasure is the only value. 'A House of Lust' (kama-haitukam) — the world reduced to an arena for appetite. The framework parallel: this is the Pathos-dominant worldview in the FU framework — where emotional gratification replaces logos (reason) and mythos (meaning). When a culture adopts this worldview, institutions erode because there is no foundation for sustained effort, sacrifice, or genuine inquiry. 'This is all there is' is the death sentence for wisdom.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Ensnared
In nooses of a hundred idle hopes,
Slaves to their passion and their wrath, they buy
Wealth with base deeds, to glut hot appetites;
"Thus much, to-day," they say, "we gained! thereby
Such and such wish of heart shall have its fill;
And this is ours! and th' other shall be ours!
To-day we slew a foe, and we will slay
Our other enemy to-morrow! Look!
Are we not lords? Make we not goodly cheer?
Is not our fortune famous, brave, and great?
Rich are we, proudly born! What other men
Live like to us? Kill, then, for sacrifice!
Cast largesse, and be merry!" So they speak
Darkened by ignorance; and so they fall--
Tossed to and fro with projects, tricked, and bound
In net of black delusion, lost in lusts--
Down to foul Naraka. Conceited, fond,
Stubborn and proud, dead-drunken with the wine
Of wealth, and reckless, all their offerings
Have but a show of reverence, being not made
In piety of ancient faith. Thus vowed
To self-hood, force, insolence, feasting, wrath,
These My blasphemers, in the forms they wear
And in the forms they breed, my foemen are,
Hateful and hating; cruel, evil, vile,
Lowest and least of men, whom I cast down
Again, and yet again, at end of lives,
Into some devilish womb, whence--birth by birth--
The devilish wombs re-spawn them, all beguiled;
And, till they find and worship Me, sweet Prince!
Tread they that Nether Road.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
Ensnared by a hundred vain hopes, enslaved to passion and anger, they pursue wealth through corrupt means to feed their appetites.

'Today we gained this much,' they say. 'Tomorrow we will satisfy that desire. This is ours — and that will be ours soon. We killed an enemy today; tomorrow we will kill another. We are lords! We feast! Our fortune is great! We are rich and well-born — who lives as well as us? We will sacrifice and give gifts and enjoy ourselves!'

So they speak, darkened by ignorance — tossed by schemes, trapped in delusion, lost in lusts, falling down to foul depths.

Conceited, stubborn, drunk on wealth, reckless — they make a show of religious observance without genuine faith. Devoted to ego, force, arrogance, desire, and rage — they are my enemies, hateful and hating, cruel, evil, the lowest of humanity.

I cast them down, again and again, into demonic births — until they find me and turn.
D: Elegant: 'This is ours. That will be ours. We killed our enemy. We are lords. Who lives as well as us?' So they speak — drunk on wealth, trapped in delusion, making a show of religion without faith. I cast them down, birth after birth, until they turn.
D: Distilled: The demonic life is an escalating cycle of acquisition, self-congratulation, and blindness — and the punishment is not external but the continuation of the cycle itself.
Notes: The portrait of the asura (demonic person) is strikingly contemporary: wealth-obsessed, status-driven, performatively religious, genuinely nihilistic. 'Drunk with the wine of wealth' (dhana-mada-anvitah) — wealth as intoxicant, impeding judgment. The performative sacrifice ('their offerings have but a show of reverence') describes the person who uses religious or moral language instrumentally, for social capital, without inner conviction. Krishna's punishment — casting them 'down again and again' into demonic births — is not vindictive but karmic: the qualities they cultivate produce the conditions of their rebirth. The framework parallel: this is the portrait of institutional corruption at its most advanced — leaders who use the language of the institution's mission while pursuing personal gain, power, and status. The 'sacrifice without faith' is the modern institution that performs its rituals (accreditation, assessment, mission statements) without genuine commitment to its stated purpose.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
The Doors of Hell
Are threefold, whereby men to ruin pass,--
The door of Lust, the door of Wrath, the door
Of Avarice. Let a man shun those three!
He who shall turn aside from entering
All those three gates of Narak, wendeth straight
To find his peace, and comes to Swarga's gate.
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The doors of hell are three: lust, anger, and greed. Whoever avoids all three walks the path toward peace and reaches heaven's gate.
D: Elegant: Three doors to hell: lust, anger, greed. Avoid all three — and you walk toward peace.
D: Distilled: The path to ruin has only three entrances — desire, rage, and greed — and all three can be recognized and refused.
Notes: Kama (lust/desire), krodha (anger), and lobha (greed) — the three 'gates of hell' (naraka-dvara). These are the same three obstacles identified throughout the Gita: desire was named as the great enemy in Chapter 3, anger as its companion, and greed as the force that drives accumulation without wisdom. Swarga (heaven) is the alternative destination — but the Gita has already taught (Chapter 9) that even heaven is temporary unless it leads to genuine liberation. The framework parallel: lust, anger, and greed map to three modes of institutional failure — institutions captured by appetite (kama), by reactivity and conflict (krodha), or by accumulation of resources without purpose (lobha).

D: Original:
[Krishna]
.     .     .     .     .     .     .     .     .     .     .     .

HERE ENDETH CHAPTER XVI. OF THE BHAGAVAD-GITA,
Entitled  "Daivasarasaupadwibhagayog,"
Or "The Book of the Separateness of the Divine and Undivine."
Notes: Chapter colophon. Daivasura Sampad Vibhaga Yoga — the Yoga of the Division between Divine and Demonic Natures. This chapter provided the Gita's ethical taxonomy: divine qualities (a comprehensive catalog of virtues) versus demonic qualities (a shorter but devastating portrait of nihilistic hedonism). Three gates of hell: lust, anger, greed. The demonic worldview denies order, purpose, and moral law. The divine worldview cultivates humility, self-control, and devotion. The framework parallel: Chapter 16 is the Gita's most direct contribution to the ethics of character — not rules to follow but qualities to cultivate, and a stark warning about what happens when the wrong qualities take hold.

D: Original: Chapter 17: The Yoga of Threefold Faith (Sraddhatraya Vibhaga Yoga)

[Arjuna]
If men forsake the holy ordinance,
Heedless of Shastras, yet keep faith at heart
And worship, what shall be the state of those,
Great Krishna! Sattwan, Rajas, Tamas? Say!
D: Refashioned:
[Arjuna]
What about people who abandon the formal scriptures but still worship with genuine faith? Where do they stand — in sattva, rajas, or tamas?
D: Elegant: If someone ignores the scriptures but worships with real faith — what quality governs them? Where do they stand?
D: Distilled: The student asks: does genuine faith without formal observance count — and if so, what kind of faith is it?
Notes: Arjuna's question distinguishes between shastra (scripture/formal rules) and shraddha (faith/inner conviction). Can you be spiritually legitimate without following the prescribed forms? This is the perennial tension between institutional religion and personal spirituality. The framework parallel: this is the question of whether wisdom can exist outside the Academy — whether genuine understanding is possible without formal education. The Gita's answer (faith takes the quality of the believer) suggests yes, but with important qualifications.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Threefold the faith is of mankind and springs
From those three qualities,--becoming "true,"
Or "passion-stained," or "dark," as thou shalt hear!
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The faith of each person springs from one of the three qualities — becoming true, passion-stained, or dark, according to their nature.
D: Elegant: Faith comes in three kinds — clear, passionate, or dark — and each person's faith reflects the quality that dominates them.
D: Distilled: Even faith has a quality — and the quality of your faith reveals the quality of your soul.
Notes: Shraddha (faith) is not one thing but three, differentiated by the gunas. Sattvic faith is clear, steady, and oriented toward truth. Rajasic faith is passionate, ambitious, and oriented toward results. Tamasic faith is dull, superstitious, and oriented toward dark or destructive ends. The teaching: faith itself is neutral — what matters is its quality. Blind faith in something dark is not equivalent to clear faith in something true. The framework parallel: commitment to a cause is not inherently valuable — it depends entirely on what the commitment is oriented toward. Passionate devotion to a corrupt institution is rajasic faith, not wisdom.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
The faith of each believer, Indian Prince!
Conforms itself to what he truly is.
Where thou shalt see a worshipper, that one
To what he worships lives  assimilate,
[Such as the shrine, so is the votary,]
The "soothfast" souls adore true gods; the souls
Obeying Rajas worship Rakshasas
Or Yakshas; and the men of Darkness pray
To Pretas and to Bhutas. Yea, and those
Who  practise bitter penance, not enjoined
By rightful rule--penance which hath its root
In self-sufficient, proud hypocrisies--
Those men, passion-beset, violent, wild,
Torturing--the witless ones--My elements
Shut in fair company within their flesh,
(Nay, Me myself, present within the flesh!)
Know them to devils devoted, not to Heaven!
For like as foods are threefold for mankind
In nourishing, so is there threefold way
Of worship, abstinence, and almsgiving!
Hear this of Me! there is a food which brings
Force, substance, strength, and health, and joy to live,
Being well-seasoned, cordial, comforting,
The "Soothfast" meat. And there be foods which bring
Aches and unrests, and burning blood, and grief,
Being too biting, heating, salt, and sharp,
And therefore craved by too strong appetite.
And there is foul food--kept from over-night,
Savourless, filthy, which the foul will eat,
A feast of rottenness, meet for the lips
Of such as love the "Darkness."
D: Refashioned:
[Krishna]
The faith of each person conforms to what they truly are. As the shrine, so the worshipper — you become what you worship.

Those governed by sattva worship the true gods. Those governed by rajas worship spirits of power and ambition. Those governed by tamas worship ghosts, darkness, and the dead.

And those who practice harsh penance not prescribed by any legitimate tradition — self-important, driven by pride and hypocrisy, passionate, violent, torturing their own bodies and the divine presence within — know them to be of demonic nature.

For just as food is threefold, so too are worship, self-discipline, and generosity. There is food that brings strength, health, and joy — wholesome, nourishing, comforting — this is sattvic food. There is food that brings pain, restlessness, and burning — too bitter, too hot, too sharp, craved by fierce appetites — this is rajasic food. And there is food that is foul, stale, rotten — fit for those who love darkness — this is tamasic food.
D: Elegant: You become what you worship. Sattvic souls worship truth. Rajasic souls worship power. Tamasic souls worship darkness. Even food follows the same law: wholesome nourishment for the clear, burning excess for the passionate, and rot for the dark.
D: Distilled: You become what you worship, eat what you are, and the quality of your faith, your food, and your practice all reveal the same underlying orientation.
Notes: The principle 'as the shrine, so the votary' (yo yac-chraddhaḥ sa eva saḥ) — you become what you orient toward. This is one of the Gita's most powerful diagnostic statements. The threefold classification extends to food (ahara): sattvic food is fresh, nourishing, and pleasant; rajasic food is overstimulating; tamasic food is stale or rotten. The food classification is not merely dietary advice but a metaphor: what you consume (information, entertainment, relationships) shapes what you become. Rakshasas and Yakshas are powerful but capricious beings; Pretas and Bhutas are ghosts and malevolent spirits. The framework parallel: the erosion pattern can be diagnosed by examining what a culture consumes — its media, its entertainment, its heroes. A society consuming 'tamasic' content (sensationalism, outrage, trivia) is cultivating tamasic qualities. A society consuming 'sattvic' content (genuine inquiry, art, thoughtful discourse) is cultivating wisdom.

D: Original:
[Krishna]
Thus with rites;--
A sacrifice not for rewardment made,
Offered in rightful wise, when he who vows
Sayeth, with heart devout, "This I should do!"
Is "Soothfast" rite. But sacrifice for gain,
Offered for good repute, be sure that this,
O Best of Bharatas! is Rajas-rite,
